It sort of sits on its own partially because of how long it takes. It's such a different type of event in all sorts of ways from ticketing (lack thereof) to the way in which the sort of sheer duration every day but also over a weekly period.

We are of course talking about the Tour de France, the world's greatest cycling race — which starts Charlie on 4th of July I think it is isn't it? So what's the date today? The 2nd. So it starts yeah starts on Saturday.

Um, and therefore, you know, we probably one of our most requested topics. We actually haven't covered cycling at all on this show. So we've known for a couple of months that we were going to. And as with everything, I think it's great to do it as and when there's a moment of attention about to be thrust upon the sport.

Um, and as you very elegantly highlighted there, the tour is obviously the main feature of the cycling calendar. And as I've taken a look at this, you know, that's obviously amazing, but it's also a significant problem for cycling um with so much dependence, particularly commercially falling on one event.

Charlie, how much did you know about the tour before, you know, of course, I mean, you know, beyond obviously maybe tuning in a bit.

环法历史与英国自行车运动的崛起

A fair amount. I visited it myself um when I was um in my 20s and went on a summer sort of break traveling with friends in France and went up into the mountains specifically to watch a Tour stage which was great and extraordinary. You get this enormous caravan descending on what are quite small villages and towns and just totally swamping them.

So yeah, a sort of a fair amount. This was the era of Miguel Indurain who is one of the four greats of cycling history. If you look back four men have won the Tour five times if that makes sense. Um Jacques Anquetil, Eddy Merckx a Belgian, Bernard Hinault another Frenchman and Miguel Indurain Spanish — and Indurain being the most recent which was around the sort of turn of the century.

Charlie's right in saying that the tour is the biggest deal in cycling, but there are two other what we call Grand Tours, the Giro d'Italia and the Vuelta a España. So that's the Italian and Spanish tours. And these are the three major European countries that have got big cycling history. This is where cycling is an integral part of the national psyche.

Now this will slightly surprise people particularly if anyone's sort of come from overseas to visit the UK in the last 10 years and we go well surely it's a massive thing in the UK as well because there's cyclists everywhere. That definitely was not the case 30 to 40 years ago. But what you saw was a bizarre resurgence — I wouldn't even say resurgence, just call it a surgence of British cycling that happened between about 2005 to 2020.

And that was partially led on the Olympic track where the emergence of lottery money to fund British Olympic sports. A lot of that was plowed into cycling and it was determined this was a sport where Britain could pick up a lot of medals in the Olympics and they started testing youngsters for just, you know, specifically the traits that would make them track cyclists. And that was very successful.

Team Sky 与"边际收益"哲学

And then for a time the road cycling — which is what the Tours are — also took off and that was under the guise of something called Team Sky which was overseen by a man called David Brailsford now Sir Dave Brailsford which came into the historic cycling world an upstart English team coming into the established EU cycling scene and bringing what famously became known as the accumulation of marginal gains.

Which was Brailsford's sporting philosophy which said if you pay very minute attention to every single element of a sport — in this case cycling — that those will add up over time into a big advantage.

And Team Sky as a result of that became the dominant cycling team during the 2010s and you saw British cycling greats like Bradley Wiggins and Chris Froome and actually Geraint Thomas winning the Tour de France and Team Sky being the dominant team on top of that.

Of course, as we'll come on to, that was all clouded by a doping controversy which almost came out a bit subsequent to a lot of that success. We can come on to that in a moment. And that team in time was bought by INEOS to Jim Ratcliffe and really since INEOS took it over it ends into a sort of a bit of a death spiral that team and has never quite been the same again.

Anyway, so that's a sort of landscape view but Charlie you've been looking into the economics of it as well. What do you make of it?

Amaury 家族的隐秘控制

Well firstly what I make of it is it's all very secretive and that of course comes — and sorry secretive from a financial standpoint. So everything that you look at is reported figures and that's because the Amaury family which is French family the owners of L'Équipe the French newspaper sports publication — the Tour was actually built out from newspapers. It was created to sell newspapers in the early 1900s when the boom of cycling really took off.

And there's a few really cool videos on YouTube that I highly recommend going and watching just about those early years of the tour because it's fascinating to see how it's developed but the audience and the fanfare around it even in those early 1900s was absolutely incredible.

And so clearly very early on, there was a massive demand and a product that fans were deeply engaged with. But what it ensured really was that the structure was very central. And so the Amaury family — L'Équipe basically controlled the majority of the Tour and their kind of sports organization ASO is the kind of majority controller.

中央化控制带来的商业局限

The big problem with that challenge, we've seen it in a lot of sports is that with so much control then comes centralized decision-making that doesn't necessarily benefit the growth of the event or the total sport. It actually operates quite similar to something like a Masters. They don't maximize the commercial opportunity.

And therefore the numbers that have come out suggest that the tour despite having 12 million or so people that line the streets or the roadsides throughout the 3-week event which is a huge number. None of them pay for tickets. All of the money really is driven by the commercial value and the broadcast.

Right? So broadcast is about they think between 50 to 70%, sponsorship let's say 40, 30-40%. And then you have some host fees as well at about five, but the estimate is anywhere up to 150 million euros that's generated throughout the tour which is you know a lot of money — a significant amount of money — but is it a huge amount of money when you look at the number of people engaged in it?

车队预算与"电视露脸"经济学

When you look at the value attributed to some of the teams, the prize money sitting around 2 million I think Charlie for the winner which is actually a tiny amount of money when some of the top teams' budgets — you mentioned INEOS, UAE which is where Tadej Pogačar who you know is going for his fifth — you mentioned the people that have won five, Pogačar is going for his fifth Tour de France this year — they spend up to about 50 to 60 million. That's their budgets.

Now the great if you're right at the top of it but then you have teams also around 20 million. So you have a big spread, but also if those teams aren't earning from the success in the tour, you get a business model trying to figure out where the value really lies. Commercially is where it really nets out for the teams.

You mentioned Sky there. I mean the value for the tour has always been you're Sky, you buy the inventory, you put your badge everywhere and hopefully for 3 weeks you are front and center.

But there are some quite cool stories of, and I didn't really think about this, Charlie, but they were saying, if you maybe let's say a slightly lesser team whose brand doesn't get as much value, you'll suddenly see someone break from the peloton and they'll just have a charge and for half an hour there's a helicopter above them. They are on TV for that whole time, and that is full sponsor time for that person.

Now, really, from a sporting sense, there's no need for that rider to do that. It is not helping his race, but it helps drive the value for the brand because he's been on TV for the last 30 minutes and they can trade that in at their next negotiation.

环法作为"精神性IP"的哲学

Um, so Charlie, a long way round of saying I think that there's a fascinating model here. There's one that has solid numbers behind it. Yes, good broadcast, yes, good commercial. There's one that has no ticketing revenue, which is very strange for a sporting event. But at the same time with significant amount of control sitting in one family and a significant amount of the sport of cycling relying on a lot of the interest generated by the tour and some of the money generated by the tour.

How has the sport of cycling positioned itself to move forward and develop, and as we talk about every week, this ever competitive sporting environment? And that's where I think a lot of the questions that we've got on the tour have come about. It's when looking at all these various sports, when looking at the golfs, the smaller sports, the places where things have broken away in the last few years, how does cycling sit on that?

Yeah, it's a great summary, Charlie. I don't disagree with a word you've just said. I think what one is in danger of underestimating is the extent to which the Amaury family are guardians of their own event just like the Masters committee are guardians of their own event and because they don't have to satisfy higher pay masters in terms of optimizing every single little bit of revenue.

Part of what they're doing here is they're maintaining the spirit of the tour. So if you were to start looking at ticketing people who are just standing by the side of the road, you would lose some of the essence of what the tour is. And I think the Amaury family, I can't obviously speak on their behalf, but I would speculate that they think that look, we do really well out of this, right? We created it as a family to basically market newspapers. It's grown into this amazing thing unto itself, which makes us a profit of about 30 to 40 million euros a year. That's enough.

We don't need to do more than that because actually we're just a family and we care about the spirit of what we've grazed and in fact maybe even commercially we might potentially endanger that going down the track if people start to perceive that it's not what it was.

And the net result of that is it's one of these events which is conspicuously almost exactly the same as it always has been. As you say, you can watch YouTube tours stuff on from the 1950s and it's essentially the same. Now, I know in the detail people will say, well, in the detail of what the teams are doing in terms of the engineering around the bikes, in terms of the physical side around the riders — of course, that has all moved and changed, but from a spectator point of view, it is really the same as when Bernard Hinault was the sort of the great global hero.

And I think that is something precious which is is very easy to lose if one does try and squeeze it for every last picking.

兴奋剂丑闻的历史阴影

And in terms of the profile of cycling, personally I would say that the last 20 years has been probably cycling's most high-profile period. Certainly of my lifetime it's been very very high profile. So I don't think one can really say that cycling's got a problem and the tour is the problem. I would look at it the other way round and say cycling has become very high-profile and the Tour de France remaining as this pristine sort of spirit of the tour type thing is fantastic.

Now we can't avoid the non-pristine bit which is doping problems. And by the way that is as much part of tour history as any other part. Right? So you go back to the 1960s and you find — I'm trying to remember the name. I think it was Tommy Simpson, the British rider who died during the tour. That was also an era where riders were taking substances. This has been going on an awful long time.

Now obviously like other sports there is extensive doping testing that goes on but it's such a big part of the history of cycling that the suspicion has always been that there are some people who are pushing the boundaries beyond where the testers can really get to.

And I think it's just worth reflecting on the INEOS scandal because it is very much related to that era when Team Sky was dominant within the sport and this was when effectively the then medical director of that team Richard Freeman — I mean I'm just speaking a bit from memory here — but there was a sort of incident where effectively he was carrying a parcel including certain substances to the team.

Anyway, there was a long sporting scandal went on over this because this was in the team. This was in the era when Wiggins and Froome were at their peak and very much Brailsford and Co have been marketing themselves and had been marketed as the clean face of road racing.

And this came along and it really blindsided people because it then threw question marks over those victories which had been won previously by this team. And in the end Richard Freeman was I think actually he was stripped of his medical license. Certainly for a while. So effectively there was an acceptance by the sport that that had gone wrong.

And then of course predating that going back 20 years into the Lance Armstrong era when it became clear that not just Lance Armstrong himself but that other riders who'd been riding at the front of the peloton had been involved in various substances which were either fully illegal against the rules or pushing the boundaries in various different ways.

So, I think it would — I just don't think we can have a discussion about cycling without acknowledging the fact that this is a sport that has been significantly blighted by doping scandals.

车队商业模式的脆弱性

And of course, when that happens, right, it's an image problem. And an image problem is the damage to the money that will be spent on it by brands and the associations that they want to have. And really, when a huge amount of the development of your sport is dependent on the commercial development of your sport, that is a significant problem.

Because the team side fascinates me — you mentioned there and I agree to an extent about the need to maximize it but at the same time when you're asking teams or you're giving huge cost of entries budgets to teams to spend and you're giving very little back at some point there's going to be a point where a team or an owner turns around and goes well I'm going to want more for my investment than a few million quid for winning it.

And that's why you know say if you're looking at those kind of 50 million spends there eventually needs to be a point of return and something that the Cofidis, one of the team's boss said that I thought was quite interesting. We do like a quote.

This is a quote directly from him. He said, "A football club owns a stadium, a share of League TV money, and a transfer asset base. A cycling team owns a logo on a jersey and a prayer that the sponsor resigns. That's the whole balance sheet."

So, for these teams, building a business model on something as fragile as that, which as you've highlighted, can come crashing down for various reasons — that as a sports entity, as an investment, as a decision-making moment to decide well where do I want to put my money? I think it's an interesting question to ask.

车手薪资与成本控制问题

Well, it is a range. We just come from a discussion about tennis players' pay. And we're coming on to this now. And I guess so first of all the moment which it becomes a strategic question for the Amaury family is when they don't have enough teams entering right? So that's if there are enough teams entering then de facto it's not a problem. If they don't have enough teams entering then it is a problem and then they have to start looking at whether more of their profits of 30 to 40 million a year need to be recycled back into the teams.

I would say that for teams to complain they may need to have a look at their own cost structures. You know, these cyclists are very very very well paid. I don't know if you had a look at this, but Pogačar gets about 8 to 10 million a year, I think. And I think quite a lot of the teams, and correct me if I'm wrong, are spending 60% at least, some more on their wages and staff costs.

They are.

And an even an average professional cyclist is being paid the thick end of a million bucks a year or million pounds a year. I think it's £700,000 a year on average is the average that includes those who just ride down in the peloton just domestiques who are just sort of part of making up the back end of the team.

And the question I think I would ask the teams is okay perhaps you need to on the one hand be speaking to the Amaury family but on the other hand you need to be discussing amongst yourselves about having a better cost cap system.

I mean if this is a sport that doesn't generate a vast amounts of revenue then what on earth are you doing paying leading practitioners the thick end of 10 million a year?

I mean 10 million a year is absolutely colossal. There are world-leading golfers who don't own that in a sport that's frankly much more widespread and has much bigger TV rights revenue.

So I think the bottom line is at the moment it seems there are enough teams — obviously they are — if not happy then capable to meet their bills in one way shape or form or another. So at the moment the whole ecosystem is working but I do take your point that ultimately it looks quite creaky and it looks like it's over dependent on commercial revenue.

And whilst funny enough we discussed this at SailGP a couple of weeks ago — SailGP is heavily dependent on commercial revenue but equally the whole cost structure is very very controlled and limited. Whereas what you're looking at here is a situation where you're entirely dependent on commercial revenue and your cost control system is not really there. And that is a dangerous place to be I think.

可预测性与F1式改革思路

Well, Charlie, you're getting into a Formula 1 type discussion, you know, and again, reading a lot of the commentary of the people that are big fans and very much in the know more so than than I am — you know, we're pointing towards the predictability problem attached to the spend problem.

So, Pogačar's team, the UAE team, basically all conquering — one of the biggest budgets, the best rider potentially of all time — makes it predictable. And the tour's always had an element of unpredictability, which has been a huge part of the appeal.

And if you start getting success tied to the biggest budget teams and you have teams on 17 million, not 50 million, and you lose some of that competitive edge based on the finances and the structures of the sport commercially, then they do need to probably have a look at it because that's a danger to the product.

And again, if you look at the big moments that changed something like a Formula 1, bringing something that was just losing huge amounts of money because there was an ability to spend whatever you wanted, creating a competitive environment where everyone felt that they could attribute X amount of cash to develop a really competitive entity was the turning point.

Now, if you did something like this in a cycling environment and encouraged those people to invest because of that, as you said, because the teams had better discipline and controls in place, would it attract more?

车队所有权的国家化趋势与"One Cycling"

And look, just finally on the teams, you know, it's interesting. You see the UAE involved, Bahrain, Kazakhstan are involved. The types of team owners have shifted from maybe a type of Sky brand to now you're getting more kind of countries involved similar to as you've seen in other sports.

Charlie, I don't know if you've ever seen the bits on One Cycling, but this is always interesting because people are always in the market for new opportunity, new IP, and new ideas as we know. And when you've got a legacy product that's quality but maybe not optimizing itself effectively on the business side generally there's someone sniffing around looking to do something better.

One Cycling is a kind of vision that's come out of PIF or SURJ Investments. It's basically the latest — I don't want to say "LIV Golf" because it's just a cheap way of saying it — but it's a challenge should we say to the existing structures at cycling to try and build something that is really commercially viable, that does give it a future in ways that they see the tour doesn't. It really tries to maximize a commercial output in ways where the tour leaves money on the table.

They've naturally struggled to move the needle a huge amount in this because of the governance challenges that exist and again the control the Amaury family have of the organization and the tour obviously being the key legacy product. Again, you can't just take people out and ignore the fact that the tour is going to be the thing that everyone wants to ride in.

But we've started to see moves over the last kind of couple of years of people trying to disrupt this space.

INEOS 与拉特克利夫的管理风格警示

Yeah. Well, it's going to be interesting to see. I think as we've been clear all along our belief is it's exceedingly difficult and expensive to disrupt the big legacy sports events for all sorts of different reasons.

Just one last point to do with INEOS and the demise of that team under the leadership of Jim Ratcliffe. And there just a little few little warning signs there about how his management style works within sport. When Tom Pidcock, who's the leading British rider right now, ranked I think fifth or sixth in the world, when he left the team well before the end of his contract, he sort of made some sort of reference to the management style being pretty corporate in style.

Yeah.

And I think that it's just a really interesting thing that I'm a great believer that sports needs sports performance and needs professionalizing. People need to look at things in a systematic way, but you still — it still needs to feel like you're in a sporting organization, not a corporate organization. That sort of balance.

And it just feels like on the INEOS side that what they had at Sky was something quite entrepreneurial, quite sort of freewheeling and quite innovative, leaving aside some of the corporate problems we've mentioned already. And they've moved into something where there's more centralized control, more sort of corporate style speed.

There's one former writer who is unnamed who in a recent article about the demise of INEOS said: "things were good with Sky, but with INEOS there is too much structure, compliance and trying to act like a big corporation."

And it's just going to be interesting for me to see over the next few years what happens at Man United because that very much is what he's trying to do there as well, right? He's trying to make Man United act like a big corporation. And I think there's always just got to be a little question mark over whether that really works in sport. We shall see.

Um well, let's watch it. Let's see what happens.

Indeed.

话题五：AC米兰——一场数十亿欧元的豪赌

Let's move on to — we finish off with some AC Milan.

Yes, we're going to look at AC Milan. AC Milan one of the giants of European football and from my youth the dominant European football team. During that period they were miles ahead of clubs like Real Madrid and these types of people. They won what was then the European Cup now the Champions League multiple multiple times.

Their captain Paolo Maldini was a sort of global football icon. They were owned by Mr. Bunga Bunga Silvio Berlusconi. And the whole thing was just ultra glamorous in one of the most sophisticated cool cities in the world.

And since then, since those days, and of course part of that was tied to Serie A itself flying high in the 80s and 90s, but the last 20-25 years have been a much more troubled time for AC Milan and the direction they're headed in right now is something which I think we need to look at in some detail.

But perhaps it's best if we start off by you laying out why we're approaching this topic right now. What's the news hook?

新闻切入点：拉莫斯转会与阿莫林入主

Well, we had a conversation because I was surprised at seeing the news Gonchalo Ramos, the Portuguese striker who was moving from PSG to AC Milan for 74 million euros. And that just kind of stokes an immediate point of conversation because those types of transfers at that size aren't ones that we associate with Italian football of recent.

You know, there's been a huge amount and a huge amount of the player trading model in Italy now is is really the core element of the club's businesses because they are not generating enough from the media rights, from the commercial side. They don't have the infrastructure opportunities because a lot of them don't own the infrastructure to generate the kind of revenues that you're seeing from your Madrids and the Spurs and all of that.

So player trading has become a really really important — well, integral — part of the business model of many Italian teams no matter how big they are. And so seeing the money go out on the other side just was something when the Fabrizio Romano thing flashes up on your phone really really caught my attention.

And then that was also off the back of Ruben Amorim of course recently being appointed as their manager. So there was a couple of things on the football side. Of course not our key element but driven by what was going on behind the scenes in the question really in the shift.

And I know you're nicely well-versed in some of this and I know you have a huge passion for Italian football as anyone that's watched the show before knows your kind of years growing up in the 80s and maybe if we're generous the '90s.

米兰账目上的积极信号

And so a question mark around whether this was an indication of a willingness to spend a little bit more and of course the state of the contract will still be amortized over 5 years I'm sure. So it's a 14-ish million a year deal really.

They have sold a huge number of players. They sold Tijjani Reijnders last year to City. They sold Tonali of course and you know now I'd imagine they have a bit of a sell-on clause maybe for Tonali which if he's gone from 100 million from Newcastle to Spurs, they may well pick up 15 or so million from that. Rafa will likely leave on big cash as well. So I'm not saying there's a shift in that player trading model. I was just interested in it coming through.

And Charlie, I think there's another reason why as well. Well, there's a couple of reasons. However small they've posted three years of profit, six, four and three million euros.

They are also in a really interesting stage now where them and Inter Milan have created an SPV which has bought the San Siro site. So they are going to be redeveloping with Inter Milan the stadium which is a huge move. They have also refinanced finally their debt to get Elliott Management out of the club giving RedBird shall I say from an optics perspective full control.

RedBird being the owners. Gerry Cardinale is the guy that came and bought AC Milan off Elliott. We can give a quick overview of what's happened on the ownership side. But let's just say the financing of that now and giving clarity of ownership maybe comes into some of this discussion in the first place.

And so yeah, I mean I guess Charlie, that's a really long-winded way of me saying I think there was something quite interesting to have a look at here.

"这家俱乐部是一场彻头彻尾的乱局"

Everything you've just said sounds great, right? So what would you say if I was to tell you that this club is an absolute shambles?

I'd say why?

Let's just do a little bit of history here.

AC米兰的所有权变迁史

So AC Milan was owned for a very very very very long time by Silvio Berlusconi. Um obviously as many of us know the former prime minister of Italy but also the biggest media tycoon in Italy. Right. So he was huge businessman, billionaire businessman who owned AC Milan during its glory years and into the era when Serie A ceased to become the best league in Europe, but it was always just a part of the Berlusconi Empire. It was never the main thing.

It was purchased from Berlusconi — who is now the late Berlusconi, but at the time was still alive — in 2017, I think I'm right in saying, by a Chinese owner called Li Yonghong. Li Yonghong purchased this with debt, largely with debt. And the debt was provided by a US hedge fund, a famously aggressive US hedge fund called Elliott.

And a year later, when it became clear that Li Yonghong was unable to meet the terms of the loan — by which we presumably mean paying the interest effectively on his purchase of AC Milan — Elliott effectively repoed Milan. They just said, "Okay, fine. You can't pay us. So, we're now just going to take the asset off your hands."

So effectively Elliott got it off Li for chips really because it was just the value of their loan and he had of course put some equity in to start with anyway.

Elliott 的"临时看守人"角色

So Elliott never really intended to be the owners of a European football club or an Italian football club. They inherited it as a bad debt. They then sent one of their seniorish people like Giorgio Furlani who was an Italian from Milan was sent in there as a sort of operating partner to try and make sure that the thing was run on behalf of Elliott.

But from that moment on effectively the club was always going to be for sale because Elliott are not long-term football club owners. They're a hedge fund. Right. So Furlani's job basically was batten down the hatches, get the losses under control and put us in a situation where we can sell the club.

And I have to say — and he's a very nice man — he did a very good job in my view for somebody who had not really been working professionally in football before. He surrounded himself with good people. He got the losses under control.

And look during that period for the most part they didn't they weren't that great on the pitch. They won a title, right? They won Serie A at the end of that period in the last year. They actually won a title which was in season 22-23 I think it was.

RedBird 的收购与卖方贷款结构

And this was the moment at which Elliott with immaculate timing sold the club to RedBird Capital which is chaired by this very extrovert American guy called Gerry Cardinale. Okay.

So how did they sell the club? Well, they sold the club to Cardinale, including what we call a vendor loan. So that's when the seller lends money to the purchaser to enable them to buy the club — sold the house, but kept the mortgage basically.

Yes. And that vendor loan was pretty large. I mean, it was I think it was about 500 million pounds. Okay. Yeah. And the club as a whole was valued at a billion. So effectively RedBird put in 500 million and they took on a 500 million debt from Elliott in the process which — bearing in mind this is happening in 2022 — so actually 21-22 was the title they won. So the takeover happened just as they were basically winning Serie A under Furlani and with Paolo Maldini as the technical director.

卡多纳利上任后的一系列解雇

Okay. So, Cardinale and Co crash in there in August 2022, so 4 years ago. And gradually — Maldini I think left in 2023, so a year after they won the league — after disagreements with RedBird, so on and so forth.

And the mood music was that the rest of the management that had been inherited from the Elliott era led by Giorgio Furlani were also not happy. But the whole ship sort of kept going in a rather unhappy way for a while until in May of this year after they failed to qualify for the Champions League. Furlani, the head coach Allegri, the technical director Geoffrey Moncada were all fired. And the sporting director Ibarra as well.

Basically, everyone was fired except for one person. And that one person's important here because that one person was appointed the year before by Gerry Cardinale. And that person is Zlatan Ibrahimović.

伊布：从球员到俱乐部实际掌门人

So what has happened here is that Cardinale has come in. He's got his feet under the table. He's fallen out with Maldini. Okay, that can happen. And he's then thought, I want my own guy. I want my own guy to tell me how to run this club or to help me run this club. And he's appointed the recently retired player Zlatan Ibrahimović as a partner in RedBird Capital to then put him in charge of the Milan investment.

Right. So Ibrahimović for the last year and a half has been the RedBird guy in charge of Milan and therefore effectively Ibrahimović has advised Cardinale to fire all of these people.

从 Elliott 贷款到 Convest 的再融资

Right now in the meantime, as you say, they have refinanced the loan from Elliott. What do we mean by refinanced? What we mean is effectively they have gone to a new borrower to borrow new money to pay off the Elliott loan. So Elliott are now out of there and it was a great deal from their perspective. They've done well overall out of this whole situation.

130-140 million in interest alone I think.

Yeah. Now the new loan instead of being 500 million is 650 million and it's been organized by a company called Convest Credit and instead of an interest rate of 8% the interest rate is believed to be 10.5%. And instead of being an interest plus repayment loan, this Charlie, and this will be very familiar to you and Chelsea fans, is done in PIK notes, right?

God makes us nervous every time we hear it.

So this is a five-year loan in PIK notes. PIK notes, just to remind people, are payment in kind notes. So it's a loan where you don't pay the interest. Every year the interest gets added to the amount that you owe and then you pay interest on the combined amount of how much you owe plus the interest you haven't paid. And this rolls up over time.

So what does this mean? I just did the mathematics on this and what this means is that the original 650 million loan that they took out from Convest is going to end up being 950 million by 2031.

Okay? So just bear that in mind. This is an asset that was bought for a billion, which by the time that the loan matures in 2031, RedBird will be carrying debt of basically a billion on an asset they originally bought for a billion.

新圣西罗球场的巨额开支

Okay. Now, let's have a look at the new stadium because just like Madrid and just like Barcelona effectively and just like Man United, we discussed this recently, they're going into a moment of needing to pay for a big new stadium when the entity which owns Milan — and it's important to say this debt as with Chelsea is being held at holding company level, not at club level.

Right? So the club itself is not at risk here, right? It's important to say that in case of any Milan fans listening to this who don't know the situation. Milan itself is not carrying the debt. It's the holding company that's carrying the debt.

So now they need to develop the San Siro. So they and Inter Milan who obviously co-lease the San Siro at the moment have acquired the land right that needed to be done for them to then develop it because they weren't going to spend vast amounts of money in developing a stadium that they didn't even own.

Right. So they've acquired the land which for memory was about 250-300 million euros something like that. And now in a JV they need to go about building the new stadium and the building of that new stadium as we know from discussing these things with Man United and so forth looks like being at least a billion.

Right. So let's just back off our packet numbers. RedBird's equity and loan contribution to that will need to be 50%. So 500 million. So by the end of this entire process, which will presumably come at around the same time as the loan matures in 2031, the total borrowings of RedBird in connection with AC Milan look like being around about 1.5 billion.

2031年的退出难题

Right now the question is what will AC Milan be worth at that moment in time? Because RedBird is a fund, so it needs to exit investments. It can't just sit on them like a family office might do or just a wealthy billionaire or whatever it might be. They have to sell to generate funds to pay back to their investors to then encourage their investors to invest with them again.

So, at some point, they're going to need to sell. And 2031 seems like about the right moment because they entered this whole situation in 2022. 2031 is nine years after entering the investment and we've discussed before in relation to Chelsea how that is at the long end of what a private equity fund might want to hold an asset for.

意甲体系的整体估值挑战

And of course the broader challenge they've got — the broader challenge right — of also operating we said you can do so much with an asset and you can build value individually but also if you sit in a system which is also not particularly appealing that's going to significantly hamper you and as we have discussed before Italian football is not smelling of roses at the moment.

Correct. You know I spoke to the great sage of Como Roger Mitchell who sits there half an hour away from Milan and keeps a very close eye on those big Italian clubs and you know I said to Roger, look, do people in the Italian market think that AC Milan is going to be worth one and a half billion in four years time and he was like no one thinks that.

I mean, no one thinks that the Italian clubs are going to be worth those types of numbers because their media rights revenue that they acquire through Serie A is so much lower than the English leagues and even the Spanish and German leagues that frankly you're non-competitive on an international level.

So, what you're buying is prestige and history and obviously good match day revenues. If and when the new stadium gets up and running, they'll be really good match day revenues. But this is — the Italian league is just a few years behind where the French league has been.

Right? So if you look at a club like OGC Nice, which was owned by the aforementioned Jim Ratcliffe, a few years ago people were talking about Nice being worth 400 million and now it's on the market at about 100 million. Why? Because the French league has lost its TV revenues and selling sports assets in leagues and competitions where there's no TV rights revenue is extremely difficult because you then just become effectively a multiple of your commercial and match day revenue and that's just never going to add up to the same kind of thing.

米兰的估值倒推：需要8亿欧元的营收

So if you just track it back, Charlie, and say, "Okay, let's just say that you apply, you're really generous, and you say, right, we're going to apply a multiple revenue of 2 1/2x." Okay, so what revenues would AC Milan need to generate to be worth 2 billion, right? Which is what would then make a profit of the investment for RedBird — not a massive profit, by the way, by their standards, but nonetheless, it would wash, it would do okay.

Right? So to be worth 2 billion, they're going to need to be doing revenues of 800 million. Right? Currently the only clubs in the world who do 800 million are Barcelona and Real Madrid. Okay? Even Man United, even with all the Premier League's TV rights revenue, etc. don't do anything close to that.

And AC Milan from memory are doing something like 350-400... 500. They did 500 last year, but it was their record and it was significantly propped up by player trading. So again, your business model is not built on anything of consistency there. It's football lottery business.

So really the revenue I was referring to was the non-player trading revenue because it's very hard to persuade new owners to buy clubs based on player sales that have happened in the past. That's a very hard thing to do.

So let's just say it's native revenue is 400, right? Native revenue 400 and you're asking someone to pay two billion — that's 5x revenue and that's just not a multiple that anyone's going to be willing to buy.

关键人才流失的危险信号

So look, I think the other big red flashing light here is a lot of competent people have left that club, right? Maldini was an outstanding technical director as in everything he does. Furlani turned out to be a very shrewd appointment by Elliott and is widely respected in the industry for having run a business-like ship and also somebody who football people found they could deal with. Even though he himself was not a football person, he was somebody who football people seemed to get along with well — a rare breed here.

And the other people around — and Allegri — the other people around him on the football side managed to during this period when they were having to break even as you say construct a good player trading model. Right? So the whole thing was being run the way that Elliott would want it to be run.

So Cardinale comes in, all of those super smart super experienced people leave and the club's now being run by Zlatan Ibrahimović and a new chief executive who has no background in football whatsoever who comes from RedBird itself and whose previous sporting experience was with the ATP.

拉莫斯与阿莫林代表了什么？

Yeah. So Charlie, if we're talking about the reasons why, as you said, we started talking about it, it was purely on an interest on the performance side with two notable names, Ramos and Amorim going in. If we're associating spend on the pitch and looking to put in place a manager that's still vastly unproven in Ruben Amorim. Do you think — I mean is it telling us anything?

Well, I think what it's telling us is that this is turning into a big bet. Just like Chelsea, this is turning into a big bet, right? We're going to — I'm going to give Zlatan Ibrahimović his head. I know because effectively you look at this loan that they've taken from Convest. It pays off Elliott plus a bit. So my instinct is that that plus a bit is what's being used here to fund increase in player trading activity on that side.

So this is a bet that under Ibrahimović the club can go back up to winning Serie A, qualifying for the Champions League, big things happening, new stadium, blah blah blah, telling this big news story, but it's placing a vast reliance on somebody in Ibrahimović who has no technical background working in the sport.

Thought he was a player until 3 years ago was it?

Yeah, something like that.

I mean it's extraordinary appointment when you look at the experience that's walked out the door to say that this is the guy who's going to be in charge of this immensely complex beast and then to put a CEO in who doesn't come from a football club background but comes from a non-football background.

It just strikes me that Cardinale is up to his neck in this investment and is almost saying kind of go big or go home here, right? I can't just sit here and let the thing bump along. If I'm going to make sense out of it, I'm going to have to throw everything at it. I'm just not sure looking at the decisions that he's made, those are sensible decisions.

收尾

Nearly agree. Fascinating. Good look at a great club. So, thank you, Charlie.

Um, I think it's cricket time. I think it is cricket time. So, if you want to come and join us for that, I have no doubt you'll enjoy it. If you've been following any of the last conversations we've had, come over to Substack. As I said, the link is going to be below in the bio, so come and join us.

If not, thank you so much as always, and we'll see you next week. Thanks, Charlie.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、AC米兰的"救赎剧本"其实是一场庞氏结构的加速器
表面上看，红鸟资本让AC米兰"三年盈利、还清埃利奥特债务、启动圣西罗新建"是一个教科书式的复兴故事。但如果把数字摞在一起看，你会发现一个诡异的现象：**红鸟正在用一笔更贵的债（10.5% PIK notes）去替换一笔更便宜的债（8% 常规利息），并把利息滚进本金里。**
这不是"再融资"，这是**债务延迟爆炸装置**——2031年到期时，650m会滚到950m，加上新球场约500m的股权投入，总敞口逼近15亿欧元。而米兰要能被卖到20亿欧元（红鸟的最低体面退出线），需要"非球员交易"营收做到8亿欧元——**全世界只有皇马和巴萨达到过这个量级**。
出人意料的观察是：**红鸟其实不是在经营一家球队，而是在经营一个"9年到期的看涨期权"**。它赌的不是米兰会重新伟大，而是2031年之前一定会出现某个中东主权基金或美国科技新贵，愿意付出"梦想溢价"接盘。而伊布被任命为负责人的真正原因，可能不是他懂足球——**而是他本人就是那份Pitch Deck上最贵的一张封面**。
## 二、环法、大师赛、AC米兰——三个"家族式垄断"

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 億萬富豪：如果你存到100萬美金，那還算很窮！Grant Cardone【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被误读的核心命题：Grant 卖的不是"财富"，而是"现金流神学"

大多数人把 Grant Cardone 理解成"教你怎么变有钱的人"，但如果把他这次谈话的所有变量抽出来做一次系统性归纳，你会发现他其实在推销一种近乎宗教式的现金流哲学——一种把"时间焦虑"转译为"现金流秩序"的信仰系统。

他说的那句最反直觉的话是："我从来不花我的 earned income，我只花 passive income。" 表面看是理财建议，本质是把消费与劳动彻底解耦。这是一种心理会计的极端版本：earned income 属于"生产神殿"，只能用于再投资；passive income 才是"神赐之粮"，可用于享乐。这套双账户结构让他即使身家数亿，仍然可以自称"broke"——因为他从不承认自己有"可花的钱"。

出人意料的观察：这套系统的真正功能不是让人变富，而是让富人不焦虑。因为一旦你相信"只花被动收入"，你就永远不会感到"钱在减少"，从而消灭了财富的心理耗损。这和佛教里的"不占有"逻辑意外同构——只不过佛教通过放下欲望达到，Grant 通过让欲望永远由被动现金流供养达到。结果一样：主体从"消费"中抽离出来。

二、Grant 的整个世界观是一个"债务永动机"，而不是"投资组合"

他在描述 Boca Raton 那栋楼时，无意中泄露了他真正的 mental model： - 用 235M 买 335M 的资产 - 加入 100M 的比特币 - 通过 Fannie Mae 债务锁定 30 年低息 - 未来向两个资产同时借款 - 两个资产永不卖出，只借款

这不是"投资组合"，这是一个债务永动机。他把比特币和多户住宅当作两条独立轨道的抵押物，用长期低息美债为动力源，让财富在债务系统中永远滚动、永不结算。他甚至说："wealthy people don't sell assets, they refinance."

跨领域连接：这个模型的结构，和中世纪威尼斯商人的复式记账法 + 教会永续债券几乎是同一个抽象。威尼斯人从不"变现"他们的贸易网络，而是通过持续的债务再融资维持流动性；教会通过永续债券（rentes perpétuelles）在几百年里锁定资金。Grant 只是把这套"欧洲贵族式的永续债"迁移到了美国 Fannie Mae + Bitcoin 的双资产结构上。

更深一层：Grant 频繁攻击"housing is not an investment"，但他自己买的正是 housing。这个矛盾一旦拆开就非常清晰——他反对的不是房子，而是"单元式思维"。买一套房 = 消费者思维；买一栋楼 = 资本家思维。他反对的是"人们把自己活成单元格"这件事。

三、他与 Bitcoin 的关系，暴露了他真正惧怕的是什么

注意一个细节：他一边说"Bitcoin 不产生现金流"，一边把 100M 比特币加入房产投资组合。这在他自己的"cash flow 神学"里是逻辑矛盾的。

那么他为什么要买？答案在他一句被人忽略的话里：

"You need to outproduce the printing press in all countries."

Grant 真正惧怕的不是穷，而是印钞机。房地产给了他"现金流"，但只有比特币能让他与法币系统脱钩。他的整套策略实际上是一种对冲身份的分裂人格： - 白天：他是 Fannie Mae 的最大客户之一，深度嵌入美元债务系统 - 晚上：他是比特币持有者，一个赌未来美元崩溃的人

最反直觉的洞察：Grant Cardone 的资产结构本质上是一场"我押注美元会烂，但我要用美元的债去买'反美元'" 的对赌。他既是这个系统最忠实的用户，也是它最激进的下注者。这是一种寄生式反叛——比任何"传统 Bitcoin maxi"都更精巧。

四、他对"贫穷"的定义，是一种时间政治学

他说："If you have a million dollars and you stop, you are poor." 这句话被误读为"精英傲慢"，但拆开看是时间维度的重新定义： - 传统贫穷 = 资产存量少 - Grant 的贫穷 = 速度停止

他实际上把"贫穷"重新定义为熵增——一切静止的东西都在贬值，因为印钞机永不停歇。所以"stop = poor"不是道德判断，而是热力学判断。

跨领域连接：这跟红皇后假说（Red Queen Hypothesis）惊人一致——在生物进化中，物种必须不断进化才能维持原有的生态位。Grant 只是把这套生物学规律移植到财富领域：你不是在与其他人竞争，你是在与货币贬值本身赛跑。停止 = 后退 = 死亡。

五、婚姻观里藏着他真正的商业哲学

最容易被忽略的一段，是他讲婚姻："I can't be in warrior mode and she's going to go be in warrior mode." 这段被贴上"性别保守"标签，但其真正逻辑是：他相信"角色的不可替代性"高于"能力的可替代性"。

这直接反映在他的商业策略上： - 他反对多元化投资（"负面案例：SpaceX 不产生现金流，所以不留"） - 他反对情境化的资产切换（"永远不卖两个资产中的任何一个"） - 他坚持角色纯粹性（"我是 hammer，Elena 是 dreamer"）

惊人的一致性：Grant 的婚姻观、投资观、员工观（"tenants become investors"）实际上是同一个几何结构——每个元素在系统中有唯一的、不可替代的位置，一旦角色混淆，系统就崩溃。这是功能主义 + 反冗余的组合，与 Elon Musk 的 first principles 思维正好相反：Musk 拆解一切，Grant 固化一切。

最后一个啊哈时刻：如果你把 Grant 整个人生看作一个系统，你会发现他真正在优化的不是财富，而是"不可停止性"——房产给他物理上的不可停止（租金持续），比特币给他货币上的不可停止（对冲美元），婚姻给他情感上的不可停止（后方稳定），子女信托给他世代上的不可停止（代际传承）。他不是在赚钱，他是在与"停止"本身作战。

这也解释了为什么他必须每天在 Twitter 上大声喊话、必须打官司、必须公开一切——沉默 = 停止 = 死亡。他的整个人格本身就是那台印钞机的反向镜像：印钞机不停印，他不停发声、不停买、不停借。他成了美联储的负镜像。

📝 转录文本

开场：一句震撼的财务建议

This is financial advice right here. Okay. Housing is not a good investment. It is a terrible. The average home in America has only gone up like 1% a year for the last 100 years. The housing industry has been in a three-year recession.

185 months medium household salary to pay for a medium like an average apartment in Taipei. You cannot make housing affordable. What if I follow your advice, rent my whole life, and then later discover that nobody wants to rent to me because I'm too old? There's age discrimination. Oh wow. How much money do you need to not be broke? When you have a million dollars, you are as close to broke as possible. What do you mean by that, Grant? Oh, yeah. If a person only spent their passive income, they would never ever be broke. So, basically, what I do is you need positive cash flow. You don't need cash, you need cash flow. I think it's the best time in the history of the world to make money. It's an old game. So, don't be a poor person. Don't have a poor mindset. Don't hang around poor friends. "人们只记得名字，不记得细节"

A banker a banker was in my office recently and he had a couple other people with him and when they left he stayed back and he says, "Hey, I got a question. How do you handle all this stuff on the internet?" I said, "What are you talking about?" He's like, "Dude, there there's like he's like, you know, I have a responsibility when we're lending you money." Yeah. I have all this gets delivered to me. I said, "Yeah, okay." He's like, "Well, it looks to me like you instigate some of this." I said, "Yeah, okay." And he's like, "Is it a net positive?" I said, "Yes, it is. There's a net positive." He's like, "Well, how how do you how do you how do you work it out?" I'm like, "Bro, look, here's the deal. People are going to remember the name. They're not going to remember the details." Tylenol. Tylenol was taken off of every shelf in every store in every country and city on planet Earth. It is the number one bestselling headache medicine on planet Earth today. People remember the name. They don't even remember why it was removed. JP Morgan, $5 trillion JP Morgan has been sued many times lost billions of dollars in lawsuits. Okay. They're still worth5 trillion dollars. Yeah.

婚姻观：不要与朋友讨论关系问题

Uh I think um in the last segment, I think we can move on from this. Bonnie's got some questions about

about relationships. Are you ready for this? Well, we just we just talked about me and you, Bonnie, having a relationship and having a baby. Elena, one of your one of your viewers should create a meme with me and you and baby 10x little Italian Korean uh uh hybrid. David, I think your audience audience and get the get the internet going. Oh man. Um so many layers. Okay. Yeah. I can I start? Yeah, go ahead. I I I I pass it to you now. I love watching Elena's videos and there are some some things that she said about your relationships and I like your take. She said, "Do not discuss your relationship problems with your friends." Yeah. Do you follow that, too? Yeah. I would never go to my friends and say, "Hey, I'm having a problem with X." Do you go through It's our relationship. It's our relationship, you know? It's our It's me and Elena to work out. It's not me and Bob to work out me and Elena. You don't you don't go to a third party for advice on your problems with your wife. And I definitely wouldn't go to a psychologist or psychiatrist to talk about it because they have the highest suicide rates and highest divorce rates on planet Earth. So, you would not do couple therapy? Well, I would No, I would not do couples therapy because uh I I know that the couples therapy doesn't work because I've done it before. I didn't do it with Elena, but that that that is not a that is not a there there is no technology there that proves that the relationship graph improves there. There's no science. It'd be like me taking some met a vaccine that hadn't been tested. That's what relationship therapy would be like.

婚姻的意义与全球生育率危机

What is the purpose? Well, let me I have one I just have one question then I know Bonnie has a a few more. What is the purpose of marriage? More and more people are cohabitating without marriage. Uh all around the world uh fertility rates are declining where Bonnie's currently based. Taiwan I think is one of the worst in the world. What is it like 2 or something? Lowest. Yeah. Lowest fertility rate of all developed nations in the world is Taiwan. Anyway, um Grant, my question is, yeah, why why why why get married? Yeah. I think the purpose of marriage is to create a family to solidify and commit to a relationship that where you're going to build a family together and you're going to stick it out and you're going to go through hell. And by the way, you will go through hell. You're going to build something together, grow together. Um you know, it's very it's very challen I've been married 22 years. There's times where it's, you know, it's tough, man. You got your own stuff to deal with, the other person's stuff to deal with. Then you bring kids into the into the fray. You got to deal with them. It's not an easy thing, but it is the most valuable thing that I've ever done in my life is to have a family and have kids. Um, I can't imagine having the success I have and not having someone to share it with intimately. Uh, that would be lonely as well. If something happened to Elena, I I would, you know, I'd be miserable, man. You would think, okay, guy's got a bunch of money and he get gets to do what he wants. And I mean, it's not like I'm going to be by myself. Somebody's going to be with me, but do I really want that revolving door? I I've had that in my life before. I know how miserable I was. And and um so for me, I'm I'm a guy that likes monogamy, likes to have stability at least in that place in my life because there's enough random stuff going on. And you know, back to the haters, dude, like you, you know, people going to say, "David Lynn doesn't know what he's talking about." Like I get this all day long. Like to to to the point you don't even look at it anymore. Mhm. And and so you that's when you want to go back to your wife and kids in the family unit and a handful of friends. Uh like I don't have a lot of people in my life. I don't have tons of friends and buddies and um you're lucky to have a good wife, couple of good kids, handful of friends. If you have all that, man, and you happen to have some money to go with it and did it your way, dude, you got a charmed life.

家庭是他成功的加速器

Do you think you would have been this successful without the family?

Well, I know for sure I wouldn't have been because I was I wasn't mar you know, we didn't have kids till I was 51. It's when we when when you look at my graph at 51 everything exploded and we were starting to have babies. Why is that? So, I don't know. I think there's something about the unit, you know. I think there's something about two can pull more than one can by himself. Even though I did all the work, by the way, Elena did not do the work. I did the work. I'm the hammer. She's the dreamer. And um I'm the one out I'm the one that went out and killed everything. So, she's the queen and I'm a warrior. I'm not a king. I'm not really a king. I'm really more of like the I would be a a warrior king. like frontline guy going out to grab something. But I like doing that. I would do that whether Elena was here or not. Now she can manifest the beauty of the woman to me. The aesthetic is one thing. But the second thing is the ethereal the thing the the she's a much lighter being than I am. I'm very heavy live in an effort. Um she she's a much lighter particle if you will. That's a dreamer. More of a dreamer. And you put the two together, you got you got a great combination. So, I don't think I would be as successful without Elena. I I I would be successful.

双 Alpha 关系不能长久

There's this theory that, you know, in a lot of modern relationships, two people with alpha personalities or, you know, they end up just fighting and instead of building together. Yeah. Is that is that what it is?

anytime we've had any problems, anytime Elena and I have had any problems is when we were not fully in our roles. And um if we've ever had problems, it was it was like you don't need two warriors here. Okay, I can't be in warrior mode and she's going to go be in warrior mode and we're going to come back tonight and celebrate. No, no, you're not. You you both been in warrior mode. It's already hard enough when one of them's out there killing stuff to come home and not think that there's a threat in the environment. Like, man, this is this is not an easy thing to do. I'm competing with some of the biggest institutions in the world. I'm being lied to every day, attacked every day, used worked manipulated negotiated with. I'm dealing with guys that have hundreds 100x more resources than I have. I'm a privately held guy trying to like find the glitch, you know, jicking and jacking trying to make my way in in in the universe and you know when you're dealing with Blackstone and Goldman Sachs and JP Morgan and the IRS and the government blah blah blah and then you come home and you're like the last thing you want to do, man, is have to worry about your home home environment being a threat or being competitive. Mhm.

Like Elena asked me once, she's like, "If I made more money than you, you make, you know, how would you feel?" I'm like, "Bro, that's never going to happen." Number one. Never. It's not possible. Number two, if it was, I'd be cool as long as you make a lot of money.

男人应该赚更多钱吗？

Do you think the man needs to make more money that for the relationship to stay? I I I I do. Yeah, I think the woman I think I think the woman should be at home raising kids. I think there's nothing more valuable than a woman at home. Personally, now I know that's going to sound sexist and old and blah blah blah, but look, we're going to lose our civilization if we don't have kids. Taiwan will cease to exist. South Korea as well. That's No, that's a fact. South Korea, let me ask. Anywhere, anywhere you go, anywhere you go below 2.4, you're going to cease to exist. You can never reverse the civilization at even a birth rate of 2.0. I think it's 2.0. You're done. You can't reverse. Yeah.

女儿们怎么办？

Okay. But your daughters, they're super capable, independent, they're beautiful. Now, how do you find a guy that match that? Right. You want them to be independent. uh look being independent doesn't mean you need to be you know doesn't mean you know it doesn't mean my my my girls they they shouldn't go work they shouldn't go kill themselves working like that there's no reason for them to do that they should they should find some killers okay if my kids are smart they would figure out how to how uh to multiply what I what I leave and and um but look, they're on their own. They they got to figure out their way, too. Like their way might not be my way. I I think the best example is what Sam Walton left and what their kids what his kids did with what he left. Sam Sam left like I think he left around 50 billion. And you know, the the least successful of the Waltons is worth 13 billion. The least successful doubled. The most successful is at 118 today. Uh so they they figured out how to multiply what the old man built. And I think my kids would be smart to do that. Now, would that mean that they picked the right guy? You know, that's going to be hard. That's that's on them, though. If they don't get prenups though, they I won't give them a penny.

婚前协议的必要性

Uh, one quick funny uh one quick introduction. You you you want to propose to a woman. She says yes. Then she says, I'm not signing a prenup. What's your next response? Like, look, we're going to get a deal. We're going to get a deal now. We're going to get a deal later. You understand? Not signing a prenup today is worse for you than it is for me. Yeah. Okay. Because later, what's going to happen is we're going to go to the courts and then we're going to get a negotiation. So, if we're going to have to sit down and work it out, well, why should we be planning on divorce? We shouldn't be. So, let's just do a deal. Sure. I I don't have a prenup, by the way. And it was a mistake for both of us, by the way. It was a mistake for both of us. We weren't willing to confront that conversation around money. And and if two people cannot confront that when they're getting married, guess what? You will never be able to confront it later. and two lawyers are going to get involved and they're going to steal a third of your marriage and they're going to create a lot of problems for you. So, it's best for two people to simply sit down and say, "Look, you came to the party with this. I came to the party with that. When we build together, this will be the split if we can't make it all the way." And then it's worked out and then everybody knows what what so something's not held over one of the one of the two people because something's gonna if it's not worked out, David, something's going to be held over. David, you're not going to know what she's going to get or she's not going to know what she gets. And then everybody's in this doubt thing in those troubled moments.

如何守住财富：范德比尔特家族的教训

I have one final question and then I'll pass it to Bonnie and then um you know, it was a pleasure speaking with you today, Grant. Learned a lot. We've talked about building wealth. I want to ask for your opinion on how to preserve wealth. Let's say you're somebody who is you or of your stature and you want to preserve your family's wealth. History is rife with examples of wealthy families losing all their wealth over generations. The Vanderbilts, for example, I was reading just the other day by I think the 70s at a family reunion, it was revealed not a single Vanderbilt family member was still a millionaire. They lost in today's terms billions of wealth over a few generations which is not very long when you think about it. How did that happen? How do we prevent that from happening? Uh they gave too much to two people that couldn't carry the weight the the the water. You know you you can't you cannot give people more than they can handle. And so what I'm doing is building a trust so that when I pass certain criteria has to be met to make sure they can carry the weight of whatever's left. Um because I do want because it has been done successfully. By the way, I just brought up Sam Walton. His family did it perfectly. Mhm. There's no dead beats. There's no problems in that family. He raised a bunch of good kids. He brought him up right, gave him a great education, show taught them the value of money. I've done the same thing with my kids. I haven't proved out the case yet, but um but he certainly did a great job with them. And those kids took, you know, whatever wealth he left and actually multiplied it. I think five of them have been in the top top 25 list for decades. Um, so you know, it's upbringing, training, education agreements written agreements, and while while I'm here teaching my kids how to do things like Bonnie asked earlier about negotiating, like my kids have to negotiate. My kids when the bill comes, my kids don't pay pay for the food, but they they they have to they have to decide on the bill. Is it right? Will we overcharge for something? What's the tip going to be? Okay. Uh, my kids have been involved in major negotiations with me since they were six years old. They're in the room with me watching. Uh, Trump did this, by the way, with his kids. Uh, that big litigation that you brought up earlier with T-Mobile. One of my daughters was in the mediation in the room when this thing's going down. If you've ever been in a mediation, they they can be very nasty. So, that's the other thing. The way to prepare people to to prepare children is to put them in the environment. We've been homeschooling our kids since they were six years old. So, they've seen everything. They they hear the the problems and the challenges. They're in the room and and you know, I think that that's that's going to be a big benefit to them in the future.

AA 制？——匮乏心态的表现

My question is for Grant and David. Do you think what are your thoughts on going Dutch in relationships? Oh, I'll let Grant take that first. You mean you mean exploding the bill? Yeah. Can't even imagine that. Yeah. I want to hang around people that that fight to pay the bill that the you know like in you know that I was with a guy this weekend. I'm like I I couldn't pick up the bill fast enough. Like the guy picked up every bill. He wants to pay. People should want to pay. Is he Chinese? I'm just curious. That's every Chinese No. No. Why? Why do you say that because that's Chinese culture. If you go eat at a Chinese restaurant with some Chinese friends, that's what people we fight the Anyway, I'm just especially older people or parents, they have to fight the bill. Yeah. But I I I I agree with Grant. Um please continue. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, you you you just you know, it's so empowering to pick up the bill, you know, but I also want to be around other people that can pick up the bill when they can, too. Like, because you you never want to be in that subservant situation. And me splitting the bill. Okay, baby, you pay for half, I pay for half. It's it's ridicul. It's a complete waste of time, by the way. And it's a scarcity mindset. Let me just add to that. I I completely agree. You know, the man should pick up the bill. uh a relationship shouldn't feel transactional like oh I get the bill you get the bill next time like what's the point but I like it when the the girl or the woman does something for me just to contribute but she doesn't have to pick up the bill could be something else right and it could be like my wife should be like just to show that she cares right just to you know show that you're part of this you're you're part of this team not just for free ride but she can express that in other ways besides paying for her bill

家庭内的金钱结构：赋能而非控制

Yeah. And like Ela and The way we do money is she has her own money. Okay, I have my own money. I I control the money in the household because I'm the earner. I'm the earner of the household, but she has her own account. She has her own money and she could I don't think Elena's ever bought dinner. If she's buying dinner, it's because I I didn't bring my card or because she wanted to. She's like, "Let me get dinner tonight." It's a nice gesture, you know. Now the kids the kids from time to time even though they don't pay most of the time I'll be like hey you're buying dinner tonight and it empowers them you know it makes them feel like okay I have because they have their own money too by the way they work for the company they get a salary since they were six and then and then that money gets invested in Cardone Capital. Cardone Capital pays a dividend every month and they can live off their dividend. So, if they want a dress or something, I'm like, "You have your own money." And that empowers the kid. And when it's their own money, by the way, they're like, "You know what? Maybe I don't need to spend 160 bucks on a dress. I can just wait." But if it was my money, they'd be like, "Buy it right now. I need it."

收尾：坦诚的价值

All right. Uh, on that note, I think let's wrap. Um, this was a fantastic interview. Um, I I think you've I haven't seen you this open and frank in a while. on other shows. So, I appreciate you uh doing this here and uh you know, it's important that people get a very human side of you. Yeah. Well, thank you. Get attacked by people who don't know you. You know, it's important that you explain who you are as a human being. Well, you know. Yeah. So, I I I enjoyed that a lot. I enjoyed that a lot. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you, David. I love you guys. You guys are awesome. And the next time you're in Miami, let's uh let's have you in the studio and and uh really appreciate what you guys do. I know these shows aren't easy. Doing interviews isn't easy. So, you know, congrats to you for for uh giving somebody, you know, giving giving people something to look at and learn from. Thank you. Yeah. Look forward to seeing you in person and thank you for watching. Thank you. Yeah. Thanks, guys. Appreciate it.
