## 一个被忽略的悖论：数学天才最不擅长的，恰恰是"元数学"
Grant 的回答里藏着一个几乎令人不适的诊断：那些立志成为数学家的人，往往是从小被"通关式奖励"驯化的孩子——他们擅长解开题目，却几乎从未把自己的人生当作一道题来解。他们进入数学，不是因为想清楚了"我要为世界增加什么价值"，而是因为"在哪里我还能继续做我一直被夸奖的那件事"。
这里有个惊人的反讽：**数学训练的是抽象建模的能力，但数学学生最不愿意对自己的职业路径做抽象建模**。他们能推导拓扑空间的性质，却推不出"薪水从哪流来—价值在哪产生—两者如何耦合"这条最基础的因果链。AI 冲击的其实不是数学，而是这种"通关反射"——因为 AI 恰好也是一台通关机器，而且更快、更便宜。
## 跨领域连接：数学家、寺庙住持与米其林主厨的隐藏同构
Grant 拆解出的三种"数学家赚钱方式"——品牌溢价、公共品代理、直接教学——其实并不是数学独有的结构。它和**宗教机构的经济学**几乎一一对应：高僧的"存在本身"是品牌资产（大学挂名教授）、寺庙接受供养以维系"信仰这件公共品"（NSF 基金）、日常讲经与冥想指导是直接服务（教学）。

item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Rick Rule: The Coming Resource Shortage Will Be Historic
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"时间套利"：叙事与物理之间的18年时差

Rick 最反常识的洞见并非"资源牛市来了"，而是把利润切成了两块截然不同的东西——易得之利（easy money）发生在情绪从"憎恨"转向"容忍"的心理转折点，而必得之利（sure money）则埋伏在勘探周期的物理曲线上。他说得很轻描淡写，但内涵极重：如果你今天和他一起去找铜，最早也要 18 年后才能真正影响供给。

这意味着大宗商品市场其实是一台时间错配机：它先给叙事定价（3–6 个月），再给库存定价（1–2 年），最后才给地质学定价（10–20 年）。今天所有人焦虑的是前两层，但真正的"确定性"藏在第三层——那是一个没有人可以在自己任期内解决的问题。这也是为什么政客、CEO、基金经理都会系统性地忽视它：它不出现在任何绩效考核周期里。

二、跨领域连接：铜的"勘探赤字"就是 AI 的"基础研究赤字"

这里有个令人意外的对照——过去 30 年铜业投资不足的曲线，几乎完美复刻了同期基础科学研究投入相对于应用技术的下滑曲线。两者都陷入了同一种"贴现率陷阱"：当资本要求的回报周期短于问题的物理成熟周期时，理性的个体决策会累积成集体的结构性灾难。

更妙的是 Rick 自己无意中揭示的一个悖论：他自称是"technophobe（技术恐惧者）"，却押注 AI 数据中心最终会找到效率突破以避免"用价格来配给铜"的局面。换句话说，最优秀的资源投资者其实在暗中做空自己的世界观——他们为物理稀缺定价，同时相信技术会持续通缩。这是一种罕见的"认知对冲"：既承认地质学是缓慢的，也承认人类的适应速度可能超越自己的悲观。

三、被低估的观察：Rick 的"仓位管理"其实是心理学，不是数学

Rick 提到自己 10 月清掉 25% 的初级矿业股、1 月清掉 80% 的白银——他反复强调 "I didn't do that out of some wonderful forecasting sense"。这句话被大多数人当成谦虚，但其实是全场最深的一句话。

他真正说的是：他构建仓位的方式，让"预测"这件事变得不必要。25% 卖出恰好回收 5 年全部本金——从这一秒起他不可能亏钱，涨跌都是"赢"。这不是投资技巧，这是把不确定性外包给结构本身的哲学。它和塔勒布的"barbell"、和凯利公式的心理版本、甚至和军事上的"防御纵深"都是同构的：当你无法预测世界时，你就设计一个无论世界怎么走你都能赢的仓位形状。

四、一个南非白金的"隐藏悖论"——工人的诅咒即资本的诅咒

Rick 描述南非白金业陷入的困境时，无意中说出了一个可以推广到发展中国家所有资源部门的普适定律：工人工资太低无法生存，但又无法上涨，因为他们缺乏教育来使用更多资本；而资本不愿进入，是因为政治不稳定；政治不稳定，又源于工资太低。

这是一个教科书级的"贫困陷阱三角"——本质上和 15 世纪马尔萨斯陷阱、和当代平台经济中"零工经济劳动者陷阱"、和早期硅谷"venture 资本回避非软件行业"的逻辑完全同构。它揭示了一个反直觉的真相：白金的稀缺，最终不是地质学问题，而是社会学问题。你所投资的每一克 Pt，其价格里都埋着约翰内斯堡矿工的一份未解决的政治债务。

五、最后一个惊喜：Rick 数错了自己的"四个问题"

他讲 2500 亿美元铜矿维护资本时，明明说"first / second / third / fourth"，但开头却说是 "a few problems"——他没有意识到这四个问题不是相加，而是相乘的：钱不够 × 通胀 × 只维护不增长 × 需求还在涨。这在系统论里叫做耦合式约束（coupled constraints），任何一维的恶化都会放大其他三维。真正让 2029–2030 年铜供应缺口如此结构性不可逃逸的，不是任何单一因素，而是这个四维乘积的爆炸速度。

这也是为什么资源市场往往会出现"突然的"价格跳跃——线性的头脑无法预测乘法系统的临界点。

📝 转录文本

Opening: Why Rick Is a Buyer Again

Now that there is pervasive skepticism around precious metals and resource markets, I'm a buyer again. The easy money is made when a commodity goes from being hated to being tolerated and then to loved. And we've seen that happen across the whole spectrum of natural resource-based businesses. But the sure money, I think, is likely ahead of us.

When you have a real shortage, the kind of shortage that occurs after a five-year period of underinvestment, we will use as much [copper] as his been mined in recorded history in the next 20 to 30 years. And it wouldn't surprise me if 2026 saw a real economic slowdown. We may in fact be in one today. But looking in the 5-year time frame, the 7-year time frame, the 10-year time frame, there is an inevitability around higher nominal base metals and energy prices. That's where the sure money comes in.

Introduction

Host: Welcome to the Microscopic Podcast. I'm your host, Alex A. Jouroof, and today we are the 29th of June, 2026, and I'm joined by recurring guest, the legendary Rick Rule. Welcome back, Rick.

Rick: Pleasure to be with you, sir.

Host: As I jokingly said, let's let Rick and roll um with the first question, which is about obviously the — I would like to start with a bit of broader picture here. We haven't talked to each other since I think it was February last time I was in South Africa. And obviously — this is something that I recurringly say — but especially in the world we live in today, there's not a week that goes by without anything major happening, I would say. And so now you have to compound this for many many weeks.

Gold is now around $4,000 an ounce, silver nearing the $60 an ounce — just flirted with the 58. Platinum and copper also have had variations, but oil as well made spectacular volatile moves in the last months. And it's been tied obviously to the Strait of Hormuz crisis, the ongoing war between the US, Israel and Iran. I want to know from you today: where are we in this phase of the resource bull market considering all those geopolitical factors as well? What is, let's say, your broader analysis?

Section 1: Why Lower Prices Are Good News for Long-Term Believers

Rick: Well let's start with the obvious, Alexe. Gold and silver prices in particular were — and the mining equities prices — were much higher in February than they are today. If you believe in the long-term case for precious metals, and separately natural resources, that's a good thing. Because if you believe in a long-term case, you're a buyer, and lower prices are in your interest. It's difficult for people to wrap their heads around that.

During the period of our earlier interview in February, I had been fairly public in communicating that in the October previous to that I'd sold 25% of my junior resource portfolio, and in January of this year I sold 80% of my physical silver portfolio. I didn't do that out of some wonderful forecasting sense that the prices were going to fall. I did it because in both cases they were in hyperbolic up moves. And I've learned in 50 years in these markets that when you have a hyperbolic up move, it almost always resolves to the downside — not always pleasantly if you happen to be long.

I didn't know when I sold the junior stocks if they would continue to rise or if they would have a decline. What I knew was that I could sell 25% of my portfolio and recoup all of the capital that I had invested in the sector for 5 years. That seemed to me to be good enough.

Similarly with regards to silver, I sold because silver occupied a speculative part in my portfolio. When I bought it, I thought that the silver price could easily double if it went from being hated to unhated. I was wrong. It tripled. Suffice it to say that was enough.

Now that there is pervasive skepticism around precious metals and resource markets, I'm a buyer again. And I would suggest to those listeners that you have that are interested in these sectors to consider the fact that if you like the long-term thesis, low prices are good, not bad.

Host: Very good. I mean, that's — indeed it's more of a 50% haircut for silver, around 30% for gold. And I think in the last conversation you've also mentioned how in the past gold has moved with corrections of 50% to then only gain eightfold its gains throughout the years that followed. So if anything, history can be kind of a guide on that line.

Section 2: Interest Rates, the US Debt Trap, and the 1975 Parallel

Rick: Another comment. I think the strength in the gold price is fairly easy to explain — or pardon me, the weakness in the gold price is fairly easy to explain — which is to say the higher US interest rates. Higher US interest rates have made almost everything denominated in dollars, including gold and euros, cheaper.

I don't believe that the US government has as much room as they lead the world to believe to allow higher interest rates this year. Interest on the US debt will exceed expenditures on things like Medicare. I don't know how long US society will tolerate that. I believe that were the market to determine interest rates, that interest rates would be much higher. But the market doesn't determine interest rates. To some degree, the government does.

And I believe that while 2026 may see persistently strong interest rates, that we will have a circumstance where the political class capitulates much like they did in 1975. When that happens — and I think it's a when, not an if — I think that that will be a signal to savers in the United States and around the world that the political class in the United States and the voters frankly care much more about short-term politics than they do about the franchise that is the US dollar. And if that happens, I think gold will respond, and respond over time fairly dramatically.

Host: And so, if you resume this correctly, you think that Kevin Walsh — the new Fed chair — there's no way that there's an ability or even fathoming that they can raise interest rates much higher than they already are today?

Rick: In the very near term, I think you could. But let's look at what happens when interest rates go up.

  • First of all, the interest on the federal debt, soon to be $40 trillion, goes up. If you are running a $2.5 trillion deficit now, what do you do when the interest charge goes higher?
  • There's a second question though. In order for interest rates really to fall, the government's need for credit would need to fall, which is to say the deficit needs to fall, which is to say spending needs to fall. I wouldn't expect you in Europe to know very much about the internal dynamics of the US government, but suffice it to say lower government expenditures in Europe or in the United States are unlikely to follow.

And that is the conundrum. When you spend $7.5 trillion and you take in $5 trillion, that $2.5 trillion has to come from somewhere. And the somewhere it usually comes from is the unborn — which is to say we either borrow it or we print it. That hasn't changed frankly in my lifetime.

The Predictable Consequences of Higher Rates

Let's look too at the impact of higher interest rates. Higher interest rates do a few fairly predictable things:

  • The first is that they clobber the long bond market because interest rates adjust. The principal value of institutions holding long bonds — say pension funds, university endowments — get slaughtered.
  • Equity markets feel the pain because the yield on deposit products begins to outpace the dividend yield in common stocks.
  • The ability to sell developed real estate — which is to say single family residential, stuff like that — suffers because the cost of ownership goes up with higher interest rates.
  • And the sales of consumer durables, in particular automobiles, falls as they become less easy to finance.

Imagine the United States with stagnant real estate markets, declining bond markets, declining stock markets, declining auto sales, and declining new home starts. That's the United States that we had in 1975. The consequence of that was that the political class and the voters lost their nerve, reduced the interest rates, and set gold off on a romp. I don't understand how in its present configuration the United States, and by extension Europe, has any choice.

Section 3: "Easy Money" vs. "Sure Money"

Host: Fair. And that brings us to the next topic about the easy money era. You've also said recently — or you've said in Austin general — that the easy money era is gone but the sure money may still be ahead. What do you exactly mean by that?

Rick: The easy money in cyclical capital intensive investments happens when a sector is hated. And there's no sector in natural resources that's hated. You know, if you take gold back to either 2000 or 2015, there were no believers. Gold was — not just really, but parenthetically — a four-letter word. People hated it. Similarly silver, similarly uranium. Similarly, as recently as February when you and I talked, conventional energy — which is to say oil and gas. Nothing is hated anymore.

The easy money is made when a commodity goes from being hated to being tolerated and then to loved. And we've seen that happen across the whole spectrum of natural resource-based businesses. But the sure money, I think, is likely ahead of us.

Precious Metals: A Certainty in Nominal Terms

If you believe like I that societies will lose their nerve with regards to market sensitive interest rates and manipulate interest rates and begin quantitative easing, then it is certain — if that follows — that the gold price at least in nominal terms goes higher. If gold maintains its purchasing power while the US dollar as an example loses 65 or 75% of its purchasing power, the nominal price of gold goes up to match the decline in the purchasing power of the US dollar. Fairly certain.

Base Metals and Energy: The Copper and Oil Setup

Moving away from precious metals into industrial materials, base metals and conventional energy — those two look like certain moves to me, albeit not as dramatic as the moves that we've seen in the past.

Copper as an example: copper demand is growing, depending on who you look to, between 1.5% and 4.5% compounded. Depending on who you listen to, we will use as much copper as has been mined in recorded history in the next 20 to 30 years. And we haven't been investing enough money in copper for a very long time to make up for the supply deficits that exist currently — never mind the supply deficits that will exist in the not too distant future.

Similarly with regards to oil and gas, we just learned with the Straits of Hormuz what happens when you tighten supply. And supplies are going to tighten in 2029 and 2030, not necessarily as a consequence of war, but rather as a consequence of the fact that the oil industry on a global basis, particularly state controlled firms, has been underinvesting in sustaining capital necessary to maintain production, to the tune of between a billion and 2 billion US per day.

We just came through a circumstance where oil began to be priced in anticipation of a shortage. We didn't experience a real shortage because we had strategic reserves. When you have a real shortage, the kind of shortage that occurs after a 5-year period of underinvestment, oil doesn't become rationed in anticipation of shortage — oil becomes rationed by price. And unless we begin spending a lot of money now, 2029, 2030 will be the sure money in oil and gas, and the sure money in copper.

A Near-Term Caveat: 2026 Slowdown

Now I want to introduce a nuance to that for your listeners, which is to say in the very near term, it wouldn't surprise me if the temporary impact of higher oil prices as a consequence of the Straits of Hormuz didn't act like a tax on the economy, withdrawing liquidity from other sectors of the economy in favor of energy. And it wouldn't surprise me if 2026 saw a real economic slowdown. We may in fact be in one today.

If we have a temporary economic slowdown, that can cause in the near term prices for things like oil and copper to decline. You can have production shortfalls if you have an economically inspired demand shortfall — which is to say if the decline in demand meets the decline in supply, there doesn't necessarily need to be a price response.

But looking in the 5-year time frame, the 7-year time frame, the 10-year time frame, there is an inevitability around higher nominal base metals and energy prices. That's where the sure money comes in.

Section 4: Why Capital Is Not Yet Flowing Into Exploration

Host: So I was wondering — talking now about the Strait of Hormuz, isn't that such kind of crisis, because you just said the real crisis is the moment when we are not able to produce or to extract those resources out of the ground? So by looking at the events that we've seen today, and also just in general the geopolitical conflicts between the big blocks and that they are all scraping for resources, isn't it actually now the moment where all this money is starting to be finally allocated to natural resources exploration capex and all that?

Rick: We haven't seen it. We've seen a pickup in exploration certainly in the last 2 and a half years in the mineral sector — and a fairly dramatic pickup, but from a very low base. It's important to know that. And if the current malaise in equity prices continues, you will see a turndown in exploration in the not too distant future.

Right now, despite the fact that headline prices on resource equities are declining, the amount of money that's being raised, particularly in Canadian and Australian capital markets, is still fairly stout. So you're seeing a maintenance of exploration expense. But if the exploration equity prices continue soft, you'll see those budgets contract fairly quickly. That's not a prediction, by the way. I'm just saying that lower equity prices increases the cost of capital for issuers and will constrain budgets if they continue.

The 18-Year Lag

But irrespective, it takes a very long time for exploration expenditure to begin to impact supply. If, Alexe, you and I were to pool some capital and go looking for copper today, it is likely that project-level grassroots exploration takes a decade to bear fruit. After we made our initial discovery, it would take us two or three years to drill out that deposit, pardon me. Then two or three years to permit it and finance it. Then 2 years to build it.

Which is to say, if you and I went looking for copper today, we might, if we were lucky, be able to impact supply 18 years from now. The period that I'm talking about in terms of the sure money is the period 4 years, 5 years, 7 years, 10 years from now — before today's exploration expenditures begin to impact supply.

Section 5: How to Position Yourself in Copper

Host: And now talking about copper specifically, how would you then position yourself? Would it be then in the forms of mining stock or the actual physical or royalties? How would you actually go about that?

Rick: I believe that copper is too cheap for most people to store. It makes a lot of sense to buy and store gold, platinum, palladium, even silver — you know, $60 US an ounce, you can store a fair bit in a safe deposit box. Copper is very different. About $6 US a pound. The storage cost — let's just say it's not something that you want to put in your basement. You wouldn't have room for anything else in your basement.

So you need to consider the equities. Which equities you choose is really a function of how hard you're willing to work, how greedy you are, how fearful you are, and how much volatility and cyclicality you are willing to endure.

Tier 1 — The "Do Nothing for 10 Years" Portfolio

I have no doubt in my mind that if one built a portfolio of very high-quality senior copper producers and did nothing for 10 years, while they would experience some volatility, the beta that they would enjoy — I define beta as the outperformance of a sector relative to the broad market — 10 years from now would be wonderful. That isn't to say that a year from now that package of stocks might be down. I don't know. But if someone were to construct a portfolio of three or four of the super major copper producers and then do nothing for 10 years — you know, play with their children and grandchildren, garden, read books, go fishing, whatever they chose to do — they would have a happy outcome.

Tier 2 — Takeover Targets

If you want to get a little more bang for your buck, you probably look to smaller companies that will either experience vertical or horizontal amalgamation — which is to say takeover companies. One of the consequences of underinvesting in copper for 30 years is that the only way that the big guys can maintain their production is to buy some of the little guys. And if you're willing to do the work to identify those little guys, you likely can be taken out at a premium — not a bad thing.

Tier 3 — Developers

If you want to take more risk, if you want to work harder, you need to look at the development play. Again here, the story's pipeline. We've underinvested in the industry for three decades to maintain production. The companies that have bucked the trend and made discoveries — at least high quality discoveries — will likely be taken over at eye-popping multiples. But you have to do the work to identify what companies those are.

Tier 4 — The Ragged Edge: Explorers

And then the ragged edge of the sword. Once the developers get taken over and money begins to focus on the sector, the high-quality exploration stocks will do very well. Notice I said the high-quality exploration stocks. One will need to do the work to determine which those are. And they are extremely volatile. Those stocks can go up 10 or 15% or down 10 or 15% in a day for almost no reason whatsoever. Perhaps a mutual fund gets redemptions and they have to sell part of their portfolio including that stock. The stock can fall 10 or 12% in a day for no reason whatsoever. That's unnerving to many investors.

So how to construct a portfolio depends really on who you are, how much volatility you want to endure, how much cyclical risk you want to endure, how hard you want to work — all of those factors.

Section 6: The Demand Side of Copper — Beyond China

Host: What are, let's say, the biggest demand drivers? Obviously China is a big demand of copper and is in the whole industrialization process, emerging markets. And you also just mentioned if the world economy slows down the copper demand could also fall. But what are the other types of sources of demand that could offset this — like AI, electrification, defense, grid demand — that could actually replace this dependency on China demand in the super cycle?

Rick: Yes. Yes. And yes.

China Still Has Room to Run

First of all, let's look at China. China has become the workshop of the world. So Chinese demand in some sense reflects world demand — which is to say electrical goods and appliances made in China aren't just consumed in China, they're consumed everywhere. And to the extent that people want to continue to lean uh energy dense material lives, that will drive Chinese demand. In effect it reflects rising standards of living around the world.

There's another form of Chinese demand too that isn't over. China has succeeded in the last 30 years in raising about 550 million people's standard of living from rural absolute penury to a lower middle class existence. The Chinese government believes that there's 150 million people in China left to urbanize. 150 million people is a mid-size country. So increased Chinese demand will also come, amazingly, from China. Let's get that one done.

The Billion Off the Grid

There's a billion people on Earth, Alexe, with no access to primary electricity. Those people would like to live like you and I. And a materially robust life is an energy-intensive life. I believe that 20 or 25 years from now, functionally, nobody in the world will be off the grid. And that'll require massive amounts of copper. It'll require copper for electrical generation. It will require copper for transmission. It will require copper in the home for utilization. A billion people on Earth are going to be ushered into this century in the next 25 years. That'll require a lot of copper.

EVs, Air Conditioning, and AI

And all of us, you and I, desire better material lives. It might be that you choose to drive an electric vehicle rather than an internal combustion vehicle — that uses about three and a half times more copper. It might be that, you know, from what I hear right now, Europe's a little warm. It might be that you decide that you'd like air conditioning in Northern Europe. That requires a lot of copper.

And then, of course, there's AI. If the current demand forecasts for energy and copper around AI materialize — by the way, I don't think they will, but if they do — we won't have anywhere near enough copper. We will have to ration by price. There are suggestions, if you take the demand forecasts literally, well, the demands are off the chart.

Rick the "Technophobe" on Efficiency Gains

My belief — and this comes from watching the technological advances in the United States in the last 40 years — is that when we begin to produce things at scale, including AI centers, we will find a way to do so cheaper. That we will find a way to manipulate that much data more efficiently than we do today.

Notice that an old technophobe named Rick Rule said this. It doesn't need to be true, but I have noticed that everything that you begin to build at scale, and everything that requires goods to be rationed by price, inspires us to find ways to make this material more efficient.

One example would be nuclear power plants. For 35 years of not building nuclear power plants in a really truly regulatory — you know, inspired — world, a 1 gigawatt plant in the United States cost between 28 and $30 billion to build. The same capacity plant in China, where they're standardized, costs $6 billion to build. This efficiency that comes from standardization and building in batches, I think will extend beyond, as an example, nuclear power plants, all the way to AI and data centers.

The $250 Billion Problem

But the truth is, in the copper business — returning to copper — demand forecasts vary between 1.5% compounded annually and 4.5% compounded annually, in a market where there's already a structural deficit, which is to say we already consume more copper than we build. And we can't impact supply in the near term because we would have had to invest, as we discussed earlier, 20 years ago to do that.

Right before you and I last talked, in November of last year at Metals Week in London, there was a paper released that said that the 10 biggest copper mining companies in the world would need to invest $250 billion US over 10 years to maintain their current production.

Now, there's a few problems with that statement:

  1. The first is they don't have $250 billion.
  2. The second is that that's constant $2,025. The inputs are increasing in price between 8 and 10% compounded a year. So by simple math, 250 billion becomes 370 billion 5 years out.
  3. The third problem is that that is maintaining production. It isn't addressing the current deficit.
  4. And the fourth problem is that demand is increasing, depending on who you listen to, between 1.5% compounded and 4.5% compounded.

That tells you something about where the balance of supply and demand in copper is going to be in the 5-year time frame and the 10-year time frame. Understand that that doesn't tell you where things are going to be 3 months from now or 6 months from now. So it really depends — that is to say, your deployment in copper depends on who you are as an investor, and whether you have a three-year or a 5-year or a seven-year or a 10-year outlook. If you have a three-month outlook, it probably pays you to be cautious.

Section 7: Why Western Governments Made Mining Hard

Host: If copper is so strategically important, why are Western governments making it then so hard to mine?

Rick: I think that we lived through a period where it was less important. In the decade of the 70s and the 80s, we spent a lot of money on copper exploration. And we found way more than we needed. And the consequence of that is that we didn't look for copper 30 years ago because we didn't need copper 30 years ago. We had way more than we needed.

We got lulled into a sense of insecurity — and for private investors there were higher return investments elsewhere: technology as an example, consumer services, big scale financial services. There were opportunities that generated better near-term values than copper.

Secondly, I think consumers/voters around the world looked at the externalities of copper and they took copper for granted. If you were living where you live in Western Europe, or where I live in the United States, the critical nature of access to resources didn't matter to you because you lived a materially rich life. And you didn't think perhaps as much as you should have about the lot of somebody living in Pakistan or Bolivia or Malawi.

We've done a great job in the last 30 or 40 years lifting two billion of our fellow humans from abject poverty to making them just plain poor. And I think we're going to finish the job. But all of that requires energy density and material density in a fashion that we haven't experienced on this planet before.

Section 8: Platinum — Gold Relationship, South Africa, and Russia

Host: So I want to move to another asset, an asset that is very interesting, an asset that is often linked to South Africa and auto catalysts — and that is platinum. I want to pick your brain on that. Especially looking at the price of platinum, which obviously rose tremendously in the past year before it corrected again. How much of that price gain do you still think is still tied to its historical relationship to gold — and which often actually traded at premium? And where do you see it going from here?

The Honest "I Don't Know"

Rick: To the first question, here's the answer that hosts always hate. I don't know. I wish I did, you know, but I don't know, and I need to disclose that to your audience. That question being the relationship between gold and platinum. My assumption is that gold is a financial reserve asset — although platinum fulfills that function in some cultures.

The Auto Catalyst Reality Check

But the criticality of platinum and palladium to various process technologies, particularly auto catalysts, is linear and it's important. I think one of the things that happened in the platinum market is we made the assumption for some years that the projections around consumption of internal combustion engines that were made by, you know, those financial geniuses Angela Merkel, Greta Thunberg, Joe Biden — that they were right. Those people told us that peak oil demand would occur in 2028 or 2030 and that the internal combustion engine would go the way of the dodo.

They were wrong. And there was a sudden realization on the part of industry that they were going to be selling cars after 2030 and that to sell those cars would require auto catalyst platinum and palladium.

The economics of that are fairly interesting. It costs about, at current prices, about $150 US to equip a modern automobile — that might sell for 60 or $70,000 US — with the necessary auto catalyst. Which means if the platinum price doubled, it wouldn't impact the finished price of the car. That's pretty dramatic demand inelasticity, or put the other way, price elasticity.

And I think some of the interest in platinum that we've experienced in the last year and a half was the realization that while the market share of internal combustion engines relative to electric vehicles would decline, absolute sales would not.

The Supply Side: Political Fragility

The second realization that you have with platinum — something that I realized 30 years ago — was that on the supply side you are unusually prone to political risk. Platinum is produced in South Africa. It's produced in Russia and to a lesser extent Zimbabwe. None of those companies I would describe as particularly socially stable.

If you experienced real social chaos in any of those countries, you would really impact platinum supply. And as we already discussed, in the near term demand is relatively inelastic relative to price because the utility of platinum and palladium are so high. That to me has been the attraction.

South Africa's Miner's Conundrum

The other attraction is somewhat more subtle. There is political and social uncertainty in South Africa which constrains platinum. Specifically, there are policy disputes in the ANC as to who ultimately should own the platinum mines — the state, or the people who own them. Now in that circumstance, the private miners are loath to invest in the capital upgrades that those mines need. They're labor intensive. And laborers who aren't educated enough to employ capital, or laborers in markets like South Africa where the capital isn't available, don't command very high wages.

Specifically in South Africa, the workers' wages have to go up. They don't make enough to live. But they can't go up because they don't have the education to employ more capital. This is the miner's conundrum. Stuck between a rock and a hard place. And I don't know how it resolves itself.

Certainly there are mines — Mokopane, the new Ivanplats mine — which do have adequate capital invested and are able to train workers to the extent that they employ enough capital that they make a living wage and better. But across the length and breadth of the South African platinum industry, this trade-off between the political instability that constrains the ability to adequately capitalize these mines, and workers' wages, is a real real real conundrum and threatens over time real labor unrest.

The Russian Question: End of Destocking?

A second question involves capital adequacy in Russia. This is a different circumstance. Russia has other uses of capital right now — which is to say specifically a war in Ukraine. And we believe, but do not know, that the Russians have been guilty of the same sustaining capital sins in their platinum industry that they've been involved in with regards to the oil and gas industry. And again you have a circumstance where if you don't maintain the sustaining capital investments you really impair over time your ability to produce.

One suggestion as to the recovery in the platinum price that we saw over the last year and a half was that we witnessed the end of Russian destocking. That theory says that with the onset of the war in Ukraine that the Russians sold anything that they could to generate hard currency to maintain the war and maintain their domestic living standards, and that the price of platinum ran up when the Russians ran out — in other words, when they didn't have any supply left. That the end of Russian destocking generated a supply response that led to the increase in price.

That information isn't information I am privy to. Mr. Putin doesn't have me on speed dial. I've not been able to ask him about his need for cash and where that cash may come from.

Section 9: The Rule Symposium

Host: Rick, you also have a symposium that you organize — it's the Rule Symposium. Would you like to maybe say a few things about it? It's happening soon in July. Would you like to share some more information for our listeners who might be interested in joining this?

Rick: Love to. The symposium's gone on for about 30 years. It's one of the longest running natural resources investment conferences in the world. The live portion in Boca Raton is sold out, but it's available from the comfort and convenience of your own home via live stream. We've been doing this now for 7 years. Last year we had 1,400 attendees from 33 countries worldwide. And we invite your listeners to join us via live stream this year.

What to Expect

What might they expect? A big picture dialogue of the type that you probably don't hear from popular media in the United States or Europe. Certainly not the sort of thing that you would hear from CNBC or the CBC or Fox. And you hear macro from players, not from journalists.

  • You hear from Nomi Prins about the corrupt nature of Wall Street. How might she know? She was a Goldman Sachs partner.
  • You hear about the US Fed from Danielle DiMartino Booth. How might she know? Because she was senior researcher with the Dallas Fed.
  • You hear about the intersection between Wall Street and Washington from say Jim Rickards, formerly chief counsel Long-Term Capital Management — a firm that damn near brought down Wall Street.

After we're done giving you a unique macro view of the world, we allow you to put that into practice by introducing you to portfolio managers and researchers with 30 or 40-year track records in resources. Not some wet behind the ears newsletter writer who made his or her reputation the last 18 months of a bull market, but rather people with long long long experience.

The Vetting Standard

Every single exhibitor at our conference — every public company exhibitor — has been vetted. Which is to say, if the conference organizers don't know the company well enough to own the stock, we don't let them on our floor. We turned down twice as many exhibitors this year as we accepted.

The Unconditional Money-Back Guarantee

And this allows us to make you an extraordinary offer. If you attend either live — which is sold out — or live stream, which is available, and you think for any reason at your sole discretion that you didn't get your money's worth, we'll give you your money back. Nobody else that I know of has this type of confidence.

We've been doing it for 30 years. And for 30 years, we've had to refund a little less than one-tenth of 1% of the tuitions that we've charged. But that guarantee is your guarantee that we know our content well enough that we know that that content can make you money. So there's an unconditional money-back guarantee and you are the judge.

By the way, whether you attend live or live stream, we'll give you more programming — 46 hours in 4 days — than you can absorb. So we have recordings of the conference available to you for unlimited use for the balance of the year.

Closing

Host: Beautiful. Well, on this note, Rick, thanks a lot for your precious time. And for all the viewers and listeners, I've also added a link down below in the description if you're interested, that you can just click and also get more information about it and secure your virtual ticket for the Rule Symposium. Rick, thanks again.

Rick: Alexe, my pleasure. I look forward to our next conversation and I hope to host you at the Natural Resources Investment Symposium at some point.

Host: I am looking forward to this day. Maybe next year. Thanks a lot, Rick. All the best.

Rick: Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的"时间套利"：叙事与物理之间的18年时差
Rick 最反常识的洞见并非"资源牛市来了"，而是把利润切成了两块截然不同的东西——**易得之利（easy money）**发生在情绪从"憎恨"转向"容忍"的心理转折点，而**必得之利（sure money）**则埋伏在勘探周期的物理曲线上。他说得很轻描淡写，但内涵极重：如果你今天和他一起去找铜，最早也要 **18 年后**才能真正影响供给。
这意味着大宗商品市场其实是一台**时间错配机**：它先给叙事定价（3–6 个月），再给库存定价（1–2 年），最后才给地质学定价（10–20 年）。今天所有人焦虑的是前两层，但真正的"确定性"藏在第三层——那是一个**没有人可以在自己任期内解决**的问题。这也是为什么政客、CEO、基金经理都会系统性地忽视它：它不出现在任何绩效考核周期里。
## 二、跨领域连接：铜的"勘探赤字"就是 AI 的"基础研究赤字"
这里有个令人意外的对照——**过去 30 年铜业投资不足的曲线，几乎完美复刻了同期基础科学研究投入相对于应用技术的下滑曲线**。两者都陷入了同一种"贴现率陷阱"：当资本要求的

item.title: [Lex Clips] How eunuchs secretly ran the Roman Empire | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、宦官作为"人肉防火墙"：权力的反物质设计

大多数人把宦官理解为"皇帝的仆从"，但这段对话揭示了一个更冷酷的系统性真相：宦官是皇帝为了对抗自己一手建立的官僚体系而制造的"反物质"。

这里藏着一个深刻的悖论——皇帝最大的敌人从来不是外敌，而是自己创造的高效系统。一个运转良好的官僚机构会逐渐发展出自己的规则、路径和内在逻辑，最终让皇帝本人变成"多余的零件"。皇帝越勤政，官僚越成熟，皇帝就越危险。于是宦官被引入——不是因为他们更能干，而是因为他们没有根：没有家族网络、没有子嗣、没有故乡的忠诚。他们是"漂浮的执行者"，只能依附于皇帝个人的意志而存在。

这就是为什么一个从更衣室里走出来的小老头能突然被任命去指挥一支军队——这不是荒谬，这是设计。

二、跨领域连接：宦官 = 硅谷的"CEO 直属特别项目组"

这个模式在现代权力结构里以惊人的方式复现。想想现代科技公司里"绕过正常汇报线的 special project team"：

  • 谷歌的 X 实验室、苹果乔布斯时代的"海盗团队"、贝索斯的 S-team——这些团队都有一个共同特征：直接向最高权力汇报，不属于任何常规部门，成员往往是"外来户"或空降兵。
  • 他们的作用不是替代正常组织，而是制造一条平行轨道，让常规官僚永远不能垄断信息和决策。
  • 甚至现代政治学里所谓的"厨房内阁 (Kitchen Cabinet)"、白宫的"West Wing 幕僚"——本质上都是功能性宦官。

更令人意外的连接：AI Agent 系统的设计者正在无意识地重演这个千年剧本。当我们担心一个高度自主的 AI 系统"取代人类"时，我们的解决方案往往是——设置一个"外部审计 agent"、一个"对齐监督 agent"——这些监督者被刻意设计成没有自己的目标、没有自己的持续存在、完全依附于主用户。这不就是数字宦官吗？

三、被忽视的观察："仇恨"本身就是宦官的岗位职责

Kaldellis 顺口提了一句让我觉得极为惊人的话："They're also kind of there to be hated."

这不是副作用，这是核心功能。

宦官是罗马权力体系里的情绪吸尘器。当臣民对朝廷失望时，仇恨必须要有一个可以落地的对象——如果这个对象是皇帝，那么帝国不稳；如果这个对象是元老院，那么贵族反噬；但如果这个对象是"那个可恨的宦官"，那么怒火就被局部化、人格化、可处置化。皇帝甚至可以在必要时"清君侧"——处决一个宦官，等于向社会派发一次情绪 refund。

这个洞察可以直接映射到现代： - 公司里的中层经理为什么总被员工痛恨？——他们承接了 CEO 不愿承受的仇恨。 - 税务局为什么在任何国家都不受欢迎？——它替政府吸收了"被剥夺感"。 - 甚至社交媒体的算法都在扮演这个角色——用户骂"算法推给我垃圾"，从而不去骂平台背后的商业逻辑。

一个成熟的权力系统，永远会主动培养自己的"合法仇恨承接者"。 宦官不是罗马的历史奇观，而是任何长期权力结构的必然产物——只是换了皮肤。

📝 转录文本

宦官为何在罗马官僚体系中占据显赫位置？

Lex： A good fraction of the bureaucracy seemed to have been eunuchs, which are castrated men. Can you explain why this was a prominent thing?

Kaldellis： The eunuchs are part of the household staff of the emperor. So that would be like — what would it be? The the West Wing of the White House or something like this. So they are the people who handle the imperial person, the bed chamber, the organization of palace life, um, things like banquets, you know, these kinds of things, the wardrobe.

为什么宦官如此重要？

And why are they important? So, for starters, if you're like physically with the emperor all day, right? Like you're handling the meetings, you're right? People can come to you and say, "Hey, I need you to put in a good word for whatever," right? So they can get things done. So you you bribe them, they get things done. So they're very useful in this way.

They're also kind of hated for that reason, right? But they're also kind of there to be hated. In other words, they soak up a lot of the frustration that people sometimes feel when they're not getting, you know, what they want from the court. Okay. That's one thing.

官僚体系的悖论：皇帝如何避免被自己的制度取代？

Here's the other thing that's more interesting. How do you as an emperor make sure that your bureaucracy doesn't displace you? Like if the bureaucracy is handling all the matters of running the empire, what do we need you for? Right? Like the bureaucracy can develop rules of its own for solving all the problems, for dealing with petitions and exemptions and whatever. Like they can do it. So where's your power exactly?

So what the emperors did not want is to be displaced or overshadowed by these kinds of more impersonal institutions. And so they would often set up like parallel tracks of power.

宦官为何是完美人选

This is going to be a bit confusing. So eunuchs were perfect for this reason because often their origin was from outside the empire, or they were former slaves. They weren't connected to like networks of powerful families. So you knew that they would generally be loyal to you because they depended entirely on you, the emperor, for their position and their power.

And what you would sometimes do is you'd say, "Okay, I'm putting you in charge of like this army." Literally, like putting you in charge of an army. Just so you can counter-balance the the kind of networks of established generals — and you'll have like "no, a eunuch from my wardrobe, you know, I'm putting him in charge." And so this kind of shook things up a little bit so that people who are part of a system or a network could never fully count that that could get them all the power that they wanted. Now they have to answer to this eunuch from the wardrobe who's like outside of their system.

案例：纳尔西斯（Narses）——被歌特人嘲笑的"小老头"

And sometimes these eunuchs were extremely competent. Um, like Narses, Justinian's general, who defeated the Goths in Italy in this massive battle. Like this guy was a total hard ass. He's just little old man. He was very — he was very old. He was tiny, and he was a eunuch, and the Goths laughed at him, but —

[laughter]

— he had the last laugh, right?

无子嗣：一个关键的政治设计特征

Lex： Is there some component to them being eunuchs so they can't propagate the bloodline?

Kaldellis： Yeah, they can't have their own family, so they can't be like looking out for their own offspring. They're kind of, uh, um, isolated individuals who depend entirely on your favor.

阉割是被迫的吗？

Lex： Is it known whether the castration is almost always by force?

Kaldellis： There are eunuchs in society generally. They're often bishops. They're appointed patriarch of Constantinople sometimes. They're in all kinds of positions. Um, many of them are children who are enslaved and castrated against their will.

Lex： Yeah.

Kaldellis： Sometimes, however, they are castrated very much according to the will of their families.

Lex： Mhm.

宦官的"产地"：家族策略与地区专业化

Kaldellis： Um, in some parts of, like, um, in — um, the in Caucasia, there were some regions that were for producing eunuchs. Also in Persia and so forth. Um, but also we are pretty sure that this happened within the empire because there were some families that like really wanted to place a eunuch at the court.

Um, so from Paphlagonia, there were a number of — uh, this is in northern um Asia Minor. Um, and so they would — uh, it was almost like a family's uh strategy.

一个跨越世纪的权力模式

Lex： I mean, it's fascinating. It's fascinating that they had so, uh, so much representation in the inner circles —

Kaldellis： Yeah.

Lex： — of the emperor. And therefore had real influence.

Kaldellis： Oh, yeah. But this was the case always, even in Rome. Like in the Julio-Claudians, under, you know, the first Caesars, there are all these powerful eunuchs. And the senatorial class always hates them. I mean, it's just — it's the same dynamic.

观看原视频 →

大多数人把宦官理解为"皇帝的仆从"，但这段对话揭示了一个更冷酷的系统性真相：**宦官是皇帝为了对抗自己一手建立的官僚体系而制造的"反物质"**。
