Claremont：作为"建国真正守护者"的自我理解

So I'll start with the Claremont Institute. They see themselves as the true defenders of the founding. And the mission of the Claremont Institute was to restore the principles of the American founding to their proper place in American society. And they were inspired by Harry Jaffa and his work on Lincoln primarily, and especially Crisis of the House Divided. It was a really important book that Jaffa wrote all about the Lincoln-Douglas debates.

And this really is — you know, I think we're going to have to get into some of that, right? If we're going to be talking about — we're already pretty well into this conversation, but we've got to talk about Lincoln and his understanding that the Declaration and what might be understood as a second founding, right? And this idea that the Declaration was this promise and that needed to be fulfilled — that was unfulfilled but was possible after the Civil War. And so the Claremont scholars sort of embrace that history.

But Harry Jaffa did write this important book about the moral core of Lincoln's project and his deep belief in equality and his understanding of just the logic of equality, and how that actually worked with the future of America and the problem of slavery. Yeah. And so he wrote about that, and the book is pretty fascinating.

But then later in life, he also decided that, of course, you needed Lincoln, but that the founders had it pretty good at the beginning — that the American founding was so impressive that he talks about it being the best possible regime. I mean, he caveats it just enough to say that that's possible in the real world. But he holds it up as a very high version of politics that is almost insurmountable in terms of its perfection and sort of represents this accommodation of Athens and Jerusalem, reason and revelation.

And I think that's where there's this sort of — it's extremely idealistic, which is appealing. I mean, that's nice, but is it true, right? And so we have this problem with the Claremont Institute, which is that they hold these ideals so highly, and they have a very rigid understanding of it, in my view.

So they take something like the Declaration of Independence and they want people to understand it their way. And so the Declaration is sort of one thing — it means something specific. Or, I mean, maybe I'm exaggerating a little bit, but I think they've got a pretty rigid understanding of the Declaration and of the constitutional system, such that any departure from that — whether it's the New Deal, whether it's the Civil Rights Act and then the demographic changes that have happened because of the immigration changes, or even Roe v. Wade — I mean, you can get into all these different things. And the growth of the administrative state, they've been very anti that for a very long time.

So you have any sort of changes — they're not very well equipped to deal with that. And they're very explicit about that because they think that they're a violation of the true constitutional order. And so it becomes an existential threat. Those changes, those historical changes, become catastrophes and moments of crisis. And on par with the crisis of the two constitutions and the House Divided. And so that warrants radical action, right? And counter-revolutionary measures, whether it's January 6th or the election of Donald Trump or, you know, who knows what's in the future this year.

I mean, you get this kind of justificatory schema for really dramatic and violent reactions. And so there's a real vehemence to the Claremont world, and I think they legitimate that using arguments associated with the founding fathers and with their own pretty limited way of understanding what that moment meant.

Clarence Thomas 与"自然权利"的党派化

And I guess a good example here — which I haven't had the time to fully — at full disclosure, I haven't watched the whole speech yet, right? So I've been reading around this — but Clarence Thomas comes in, and it's a very classic — he gave a speech in Texas last week where he's appealing, where he's talking about natural rights and the principles of the Declaration. And he's also criticizing progressivism. And bringing up Woodrow Wilson and the progressive abandonment of natural rights.

And the idea here being that progressives don't believe in the principles that ground our political order. And you can find — he's not making this up. You can find places where Woodrow Wilson and John Dewey criticized a certain way of understanding natural rights and conceptualizing the ideas that informed the Declaration and the ways in which the founders thought about politics. Absolutely.

But then to project that onto a deep critique of that whole history and to say that that's also causally connected to Nazism and Stalinism — I mean, that's a big claim. And it's highly partisan against today's progressives. And the idea that so many — I think that the Claremont people are quite comfortable saying that people on the left undermine the political order because they don't believe in natural rights and because they don't believe in freedom and equality in the way that they do.

And I just — I think that's a big fudge and kind of a lie. Because I think that then, what are we going to do? Go examine every American's civic concept? So, how do you understand natural rights? Are you based on a natural law? You know, there's something really perverse about that — about this assumption that we should all be understanding the Declaration in the same way. So maybe I'm getting too quickly into the critical mode instead of exploring what the actual —

But that's sort of the Claremont — how I think about the Claremont's take. And, as you can see, you get into pretty complicated territory philosophically once you start unpacking it.

谁能垄断"自然权利"的解释权？

Kristen: Yeah. Yeah, I mean — you've done excellent podcast episodes before on these topics too. So I know for a fact we could spend hours talking about the specifics of any one group, including the Claremont thinkers.

杰斐逊悖论：作为悲剧人物的建国者

Yeah. So let me get some of your thoughts on that history, on the founding of natural rights.

Just to put my cards on the table in terms of what I think the challenge is: when we think about our history — Tom Merrill, we'll have him hopefully on the podcast in a later episode — but he has some great work on Thomas Jefferson on the Declaration, and a really great Law and Liberty article as well, on how do we negotiate the fact that Jefferson seemed to not live up to his ideals over the course of his career.

In terms of, he writes the Declaration of Independence, this very vehemently powerful vision of natural rights, and also has a paragraph critical of slavery originally in his first draft of the Declaration that gets cut for various reasons. He's clearly aware of the way in which slavery is a violation of natural rights. But then he —

Laura: But he was using it sort of to take a cheap shot at the British, right?

Kristen: Yes.

Laura: Which was a little, I think, is my understanding.

Kristen: Yes, that's right. So Tom talks about how the issues with this paragraph is that on the one hand, it's critical. On the other hand, it's also blaming everything on the king, and is very much embedded in Jefferson's own skepticism and criticism of the British system and of the attempt to create an agrarian, Republican, virtuous community in America — that actually also requires expanding slavery, requires expansion of land, all these various things.

And that is to say — so they have this figure who has an incredibly powerful authorship of the Declaration that later generations will draw on to expand freedom for people who are oppressed. Right. And yet he himself has these contradictions.

And what Tom talks about is that there is this tendency for people to make sense of this as just a matter of personal hypocrisy. But he argues that instead we should think of Jefferson as a tragic figure, as somebody who has these grand self-contradictions, who also is maybe deluding himself, wants to not be aware of what is even happening just in the course of the early 19th century — in early America, and the kind of Missouri crisis and the debates around expanding slavery in the territories.

And what Tom talks about is this idea that we have Jefferson's broader political coalition of these kind of agrarian Republicans — they were concerned with a variety of issues. And after a career of mostly public silence on the slavery question, has this letter that is published — you know, we don't have to get into it, he knows it's going to be public — where he is essentially justifying the fact that slave owners don't want to — if slavery isn't allowed to be expanded in territories, then people in the southern states are going to be afraid that the federal government is going to come and forcibly emancipate the enslaved people, and as a result they will be unsafe, right?

And so this fear of this threat of federal power ends up being justifying for why it should be okay to allow the expansion of slavery, even when people know — and Jefferson was critical of people who were offering more positive visions of slavery. So it's not — again, it's a complicated coalition, and a thinker who is not necessarily endorsing it as somehow consistent with the natural rights. But it is also something that is — there's this inherent tension here.

And it gets us into a position where we have to reckon with this legacy and we have to reckon with the fact that we have the principles of the Declaration of Independence that we draw on for this idea of liberty, while being compatible for decades in America with a horrific, oppressive practice that can be —

Laura: Understood as nothing other than a flagrant abuse of human beings. Barbarism.

Kristen: Of humanity. Yes. And Smith has incredible passages on this, too. So we have to reckon with this history. We can't just wish it away or imagine it didn't exist, especially if we're even just trying to think of a figure like Jefferson.

So I guess I want to ask, how do you think of those moments? How do you think of the idea of how do we approach this as understanding the promises of the founding, the ideals that we want to draw on, this idea that we're celebrating the anniversary of the Declaration — while also doing justice to the fact that I think people who get frustrated by the simple idealization, or the sort of claims that this was this brilliant moment that was not — when in reality it was marred with all sorts of compromise, moral and political?

英雄崇拜与"揭穿模式"之间

Laura: Yeah, I mean, I think it's a very difficult thing. And again, it brings us into what our moment is and where we're sitting and what conversation are we having?

You know, when you talk about that problem of self-delusion — I think is what we're talking about with Jefferson — and he wanted these ideals to be true, but he was also drawn in his political world to make these bizarre, and in his life to make these awful compromises, and then to kind of justify things in bizarre ways and just bend and twist into something, I mean, I think in hindsight, entirely contradictory, right?

And I think we see that problem again and again in politics. I mean, that is politics: it's always going to be a problem of living up to one's ideals.

And where we are sitting now, in the book, I try to — I have some sympathy with the people who want to lift up the founders. Right. And to — like, I love reading Tom Merrill's work on Jefferson because it's so subtle and it shows us all these different dimensions of these very complicated people. It's not jingoistic, but it still has a respect for what they did, what they were capable of, of some of the higher parts. And so I'm sympathetic to that.

I think there has been maybe — if we're just taking the broadest view of what the last 50 years in academia have emphasized — it is not the glory of the founders, obviously. I mean, except for at Claremont, the Claremont Institute. In most contexts, it's a critical approach. And we haven't been living up to the principles of the founding, and that there's been terrible injustice that needs excavation and change.

So there hasn't been a lot of "rah, rah, the American founders," I think. And I'm sympathetic to the need to do better. That doesn't mean telling a simple story, though.

But I can see that what happens with a lot of people on the right is they are so put off by the kind of debunking mode that they elevate the founders beyond what is reasonable. But they're sort of doing something that I think we can understand as human beings — this desire for heroes, right? And this desire to look up to something. And a lot of what happened at the founding is worthy of admiration and worthy of celebration.

And you have Nicole Hannah-Jones saying these principles were — in an interview, right, that I quoted my book — these principles were incredible at the founding, if only they had lived up to them. Kesler 与 Lincoln 对 Dred Scott 的回应

But I think what happens in a lot of instances is that this desire to elevate and celebrate — you know, we have blinkers on. And in the case of just thinking about the Claremont group and some of what happens in their scholarship, it's a pretty consistent pattern.

I'm thinking of Charles Kesler's The Crisis of the Two Constitutions, which is a book that sort of borrows the valence of Jaffa's title, right, and of course of Lincoln's speech, to write about the Founders' Constitution versus the Progressive Constitution, and how these things are in massive tension, and the Progressive Constitution is a betrayal of the Founders' Constitution — so all these familiar things.

But he looks at that history between the founding and the Civil War, and he writes as though there had been massive progress. And it's not true. And there was a massive expansion of slavery. There were all these efforts, obviously, to continue to expand slavery. There have been some limited legal improvements.

But even Lincoln — I'm thinking of this wonderful speech, well, I mean, wonderful but very upsetting speech he gave after the Dred Scott decision — where he's very explicit that the quality of life of American Black — like enslaved peoples and Black people — has only deteriorated. And it's delusional. And, you know, Taney, the author of this decision — this is the famous decision that says that Black people did not have standing in the court and that they did not have equality, that the Declaration of Independence was not speaking to them because they are inferior, right? And that they had no rights that the white man was obliged to uphold. And so that's the ruling from the Supreme Court.

And Lincoln says in response — he offers a reading of the Declaration of Independence that says: that's not true. Black men in some states were, in our history, free men and were allowed to vote. And there's been a kind of transformation, the growth of slavery.

Now this — as you know, there's a whole, you know, Lincoln built up this idea that there was a — a little conspiratorial, right — that there was this agenda, that slave power to expand slavery to all the states in the West. And, I mean, that's all complicated history, but it's also true, right? Some of that was happening, and that was a big part of his argument against Douglass, and the amount of money that was being made. It was the anchor — you know, the slave system was the anchor of the American economy in a lot of ways.

And so Lincoln, though, offers a beautiful defense of the Declaration as this expansive liberationist sort of document. And I don't think that Kesler — I mean, but a lot of that history gets fudged by the Claremont Institute, and the ugly parts get kind of disappeared. And it's kind of — I think it's part of that desire to elevate — is means not encountering the other side.

And when you do that, you're excluding — I think it kind of comes up in the response to the 1619 Project. If you can't even hear the arguments that are being posed by critics of the founders or critics of the current elite powers or the current ways in which we're taught this history — then it's not just blinkers, it's claiming a monopoly on knowledge. And I don't think that's in keeping with the sort of liberatory spirit of what the Declaration means, which is that we get to be free people who have our own ideas and who stand for the rights of people, right? And who get to act on that.

And so there's a lot here, obviously, but that's kind of how I think about the Claremont people. It's very complicated. They're not all the same. They don't all think in the same ways. And some of it gets really ugly, and some of it's just kind of blinkered and, I think, incoherent — or ahistorical, I should say.

政治理论家在做什么：与经典的对话

Kristen: Yeah. And I was going to say — I think it's interesting because I don't think it's necessarily that it's interesting because one could be frustrated on a pure materialistic methodological ground of feeling like this is poor history, right? But there's also a way in which political theorists — what we do is always more than just history, right? It's usually — we're not just doing intellectual history. We're not just trying to understand. We do do projects that are heavily historical. But it's also like we are making political arguments in conversation with thinkers, with these texts.

And there's kind of — I want to segue this into thinking about what we're doing when we're working with great books, and when we are in conversation with the founders, with Lincoln and with these different thinkers, and how that comes to play in this conversation, too, and relates to that maybe "ideas first" notion.

"美国的爹地问题"与大书教育

I'm going to frame it in something I'm just going to put on the table — something that I think you and I have talked about a little bit. I think about it a lot now, actually, specifically in the American founding context, which is this idea of the canon. But also when we think about the founding in America, it just kind of feels like America has daddy issues. Like we have our issues with our founding fathers, and we all are kind of navigating these hang-ups we have about where we've come from in different ways.

Like there are some people who want to lean into the lionization and just focus on them as heroes because it's comforting and inspiring. And then there's other people who kind of see love as meaning a mode of critique as well — of also saying like — and I don't love family metaphors in politics, but I am using one here — which is like, any kind of family, sometimes there's a bad dynamic at heart and somebody wants to speak up and criticize it. And then they might get treated as a black sheep or something like that, because it's like they're disrupting this harmony that maybe works for the majority, but not for them. Right.

And so there is this kind of all these tensions, all these difficulties. And sometimes the people who are calling them out are kind of — it's a shooting-of-the-messenger type of problem, right? And so how do we allow ourselves to maybe take a step back, not be so personally wrapped up in whatever any one given narrative is of America, in order to listen to the people who are coming from the more critical side?

Like you said, if you can't listen to the critics — I mean, how is it that you can actually understand? And how is it that you can actually understand what it is that you're saying and the value of what you're saying if you're not going to be engaging with the critics?

And so I want to segue that into thinking about the great books. So that's the kind of way I think about — we have these thinkers that we — almost these kind of fathers of the Western canon that we're thinking about here. And there is this great quote you have in your book from Alan Bloom about the kind of "spurious intimacy with greatness" that the great books cult can have.

Now, it's fascinating to read this because Bloom was very enthusiastic about a great books education. So this is coming from somebody from within. It's a sort of immanent critique of: yes, I can celebrate great books. And I'm speaking as myself, too — like I can celebrate the idea of a great books education while also thinking that there's also some pitfalls with it. There's also this risk that we kind of oversimplify, take for granted, and also maybe even just defer to the kind of authority figures of the canon, and also aggrandize ourselves just by virtue of the fact that "I've read, you know, I am such a smart intellectual and I should be the philosopher-king because I know all these great big ideas" — when what do those ideas necessarily have to do with actually managing the difficulties of political life in reality? Right.

So hopefully that's not too much.

阅读伟大之书带来的形塑力量 —— 及其陷阱

Laura: No, I mean, I think it captures a phenomenon that's very common, and the slippage — I mean, I think that there's the natural desire to admire something and learn from the great, and just the experience of reading something magnificent or learning about something really profound and important that matters, right? That experience is kind of — you can't shake it once you've had it, and so it shapes you.

And so when that's happened, then anybody who's critical or who wants to debunk that or take that away from you, it becomes very threatening, I think. And it's a kind of way of saying — I mean, maybe erasure would be sort of an obscene way to put this, right? — but it does feel like a threat. Because it's taking away something that is formative for you and that is part of your, like, a constellation of things you care about and want to defend in life.

Kristen: Derived meaning. How you've derived meaning.

Laura: That idea, and I think that's something that liberals, we don't talk enough about, is how important it is for people to make meaning in life together.

批判理论也能带来形塑体验

And I will say this too, that the right loves and has been critiquing postmodernism and critical theory — obviously like now with a really strong political agenda — for not just decades, but like, I guess, like centuries. Right. I mean, in a way, Burke and stuff.

But I want to say before I go on about the delusion and how we get caught up in this stuff and why we become so defensive as someone who's had those experiences — that experience exists with critical theory too, right? And you know this and I know this, but we need to talk about that too. Because when you're reading something inspiring from a critical perspective that makes you feel empowered and that you've understood injustice and that you've understood how to secure your freedom or how to fight for something, that's also really empowering and important. And that shapes people.

And the right critiques this as, you know, a kind of like just raw activism or decisionism or like politics. And that's not true. And part of what the critical mode does is it allows you to see alternative histories and to see alternative pathways and to see what is and isn't sort of a permanent feature of things. Right.

And so this is like the human experience of profound engagement with works of philosophy and history and of individuals of the past. It's certainly just not you and — I mean, I've experienced it more from the conservative approach right and in the Straussian world — but that is something that people get to experience from all different kinds of perspectives.

So we have to be clear about that. But we also have to be clear about — and the critical theorists could be clear about this too — about what it is we're loving and what we're appreciating and what we're not just debunking, but valuing and deriving meaning from.

那种"仿佛也是伟大人物"的膨胀感

So that experience is so profound and important, and I'm sure it happens in religious contexts too. I mean, we're talking about the formation of our souls, right, in some ways — or our characters. So this can happen in a number of different contexts.

If you've had these experiences where you feel like you're understanding the complexity and the greatness at the same time, and you're appreciating that and you're kind of vibing with it — Aristotle talks about this — this is part of what art does for us. This is part of what the reading experience does for us. And we have a kind of vicarious experience of these things.

And so then, you know, you do that enough in grad school — you sit around for four years, writing things and reading this way, and then you know being pumped up — yeah, it's — you feel like you're on top of the world and that you might be that kind of world-historical actor too. And maybe that's true for you, and maybe — but the problem is that — and this is, you know, you get someone like Nietzsche is so powerful on these themes, right, of the great man and of the ugliness of the person who would take that away from you, right? 其他阵营：后自由主义者、Vermeule 与 Deneen

Laura: Yeah, I mean, let me sort of — I should go through the other groups and how they think about the Declaration, because I haven't fully done that. And I can go more quickly, because I think the Claremont people are the one who really — that's their identity, right? And the others, it's not. And so it's much more explicit rejection of some of that.

Patrick Deneen 与后自由主义者

So you have someone like Patrick Deneen. Here I classify him as a post-liberal, and I bundle him with Sohrab Ahmari and Adrian Vermeule at Harvard. And there's these different figures in that world and Gladden Pappin, who was until recently in Hungary. But these are figures who have a pretty deep critique of liberal democracy that I've kind of — I've already said something about that. So they have this critique of liberal democracy as individualizing, and so forth.

And for Patrick Deneen, that is what the American founding represents. He's very explicit: you know, this was a liberal founding. This was an Enlightenment project. And his idea is like — and that's bound to fail. That's going to eat away at itself. That's going to have this cratering effect. And so we need to — it's kind of an anti-modern inflection that he offers, which is like: we've got to shift gears completely and have — now he's talking about regime change. And so that's a pretty radical.

You know, I don't know what he's saying these days about the Declaration, but he's pretty explicit, I think, that he doesn't like this stuff. He doesn't like individual rights. He doesn't like the individualizing dimensions of it. And he thinks it's too weak and thin a foundation for a healthy political order.

Adrian Vermeule 与"共同善宪法学"

And Adrian Vermeule, though, does something very different, which I think is interesting because he is offering — I think he agrees with a lot of what Deneen says, but his argument as well is — and he doesn't say much about the founding, but he talks about jurisprudence and modes of interpretation and constitutionalism.

And so he's got this book called Common Good Constitutionalism, where his argument is against originalism. And so it's against this thin way of understanding the founding documents as a text or a kind of sort of understanding that we can recover and then base our judgments on in politics. He says, well, no, there's a thicker background here that we've forgotten.

And he's also against progressive living constitutionalism. I mean, both — like Kesler's founding and those are both out. He says there's actually beyond this, there's a truer and deeper foundation to all of this.

I think — he doesn't explicitly say it's not an Enlightenment document, but he kind of does. He says this is the old English tradition of common law plus natural law, classical legal tradition brought in from the Christian world. There's a kind of natural law philosophy here. This is the background against which the Constitution was built up.

There are principles that are more important than liberty and freedom, and there's — you know, there's like the common good — and he gives this six-fold definition of that — and he says those are the true orienting principles. That's the true forgotten foundation for our law. And we need to recover that.

And that gives basically — it's a very short book, so he's trying to do a lot with it — but it's like that basically gives this whole new way of understanding how judges might make rulings on cases, how politicians might organize their thoughts in terms of new legislation and what it aims for. And it's a rejection of liberal individualism. But that's claiming that the original Constitution also didn't ascribe to those things in nearly the way that our tradition tells us it has.

So that's very different. And I think it's a little — I think it's fudging some things. I'll just put my cards on the table. So I don't think that's true of the American tradition. I think Deneen's closer to the truth. It's a mixed bag. Again, it was contentious. There's different traditions being embraced. But I think the idea that it's just this one thing that Vermeule says it is is not accurate historically.

Yoram Hazony 与国家保守主义

And then you have somebody like Yoram Hazony of the NatCon tradition. Okay. So the NatCons is National Conservatism, and in my thinking this is really the organizing body for the MAGA New Right. This is a person who is a great organizer. He's also a political theorist, right? But he's an Israeli-American. He's built institutions. He was someone who founded the Princeton Tory or something — one of these journals when he's young — just like Peter Thiel did at Stanford. I mean, you've got these guys who are political actors.

And he's been very involved in trying to organize this ideological side of MAGA since, you know, 2017, 2019 — I guess some of the first conferences were. So he's been hosting these conferences. People from the different camps will come to talk about policy.

But he published a book in 2018 called The Virtue of Nationalism. And this book — you know, it's trying to restore the idea of nationalism, like capital-N Nationalism, as opposed to patriotism or what have you. And it's the idea being that a robust politics needs — you need people with a shared history, shared language, shared religion, and sort of monolithic, and against, again, liberal pluralism.

But he grounds this in the Hebrew Bible, in a totally different sort of — I mean, he doesn't invent, but he sort of really exaggerates or tries to recover this Protestant — well, this older liberal — this not liberal — this older tradition of political philosophy that's kind of more grounded and rooted in cultural things and particulars and nationalistic particulars. And it's a rejection of modern individual rights theory. So this is like the anti-Locke doctrine. It's like modern liberal theory completely underestimates the extent to which you need all these background features.

And so it's kind of similar to Vermeule and Deneen in a way. It's like: we need something much more robust, built and founded in actual communities that have grown up organically. But I mean, that tips pretty fast into blood and soil. I mean, he denies that. But then you also have the book kind of chronicles how that has tipped into inviting bizarre figures to their conferences and a kind of ethno-nationalist — ethno-nationalists are very explicit about being very attracted to the NatCon sensibility. And this movement he's trying to build is connected to the far right around the world.

So you have that. But that's a very clear rejection of liberal individualism and of sort of anything that we think about — if we agree that the Declaration was inspired at least in part by early modern political thought and then these ideas of consent of the governed, which I don't think you can read the Declaration and not — you know, that's not a projection, right? Consent of the governed, individual rights to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. I mean, that stuff — you can't take Locke out of that.

And so they're really trying to muffle that part of the American tradition and reorient things in a new direction. And so that bleeds into these debates we're hearing where you have JD Vance talking about "heritage Americans." You have somebody like Neil Gorsuch finding that he needs to go defend the idea of America being a creedal nation.

Yeah, that's surprising, I think. I wouldn't have 10 years ago thought that that would have been something that a conservative Supreme Court justice would have had to do. So that can maybe give us some insight into actually what's happening in our world right now around some of these issues.

And it's striking to me. Like I said, I have encountered a few people who don't even seem to really be aware that that's where we're at — that you have suddenly a need to defend the idea of America as a creedal nation. And you suddenly have so much of this really imminent danger and threat to birthright citizenship that is core to American history. I mean, there really is no way around that.

I'm going to use the tech right thing as a moment to pivot to Lincoln, because I've been thinking quite a bit about Lincoln and this Dred Scott ruling. And it's a little — I think it's a little tendentious what I'm about to do — but I think I have a good point here.

Lincoln 论《独立宣言》

So he says about the Declaration — he says:

"I think the authors of that notable instrument intended to include all men, but they did not mean to declare all men equal in all respects. They did not mean to say that all men were created equal in size, color, intellect, moral development, or social capacity. They defined with tolerable distinctness in what they did consider all men created equal — equal in certain inalienable rights, among which are life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. This they said, and this they meant."

Okay. Now it sounds here like he's defending this idea of some kind of equality but not full equality, for even maybe even with regards to color and stuff. Like it sounds like a pretty limited defense here, right? Very thin defense. And Michael Anton uses this in one of his essays, right, to defend the idea that Lincoln didn't really believe in equality the way we think he did.

But then he goes on. And this is the part that Michael Anton consistently avoids, I think. So here he says:

"They did not mean to assert the obvious untruth that all were then actually enjoying that equality, or yet that they were about to confer it immediately upon them."

Again, a note of realism, right? Like they weren't saying this had actually arrived. He says:

"In fact, they had no power to confer such a boon."

Okay. But this is then he says this:

"They meant simply to declare the right, so that the enforcement of it might follow as fast as circumstances should permit."

So, I mean, that's a pretty — I mean, it's a little vague, maybe abstract, but I think it's beautiful. And I think I first read that in Harry Jaffa's book, because he also prizes this statement. And that's a very expansive understanding of what the Declaration is doing.

And then he goes on even further. He says — let me say — this is where I think he fudges something. Okay, I think we can all appreciate that was beautiful. He says:

"The assertion that all men are created equal was of no practical use in affecting our separation from Great Britain."

Which is, I think, shocking, right? And he says:

"And it was placed in the Declaration not for that, but for future use."

Here he says:

"Its authors meant it to be — thank God, it is now proving itself — a stumbling block to those who in aftertimes might seek to turn a free people back into the hateful paths of despotism."

And here's the part — this is my favorite part, which will allow me to pivot to the tech bros. Okay, he says:

"They knew the proneness of prosperity to breed tyrants. And they meant when such should reappear in this fair land and commence their vocation, they should find left for them at least one hard nut to crack."

Again, he's talking there about — I mean, he's talking about what Lincoln later has called the slave power and King Cotton. The next summer there will be — or like the next year, there will be this essay about King Cotton that basically argues that the economy of cotton and slavery is so powerful that nothing will break it. And that it's also sort of embedded in nature — that Black people are sort of meant to toil, that some parts of humanity are just meant to toil. It's really awful stuff.

But then, so Lincoln is concentrating on this sheer amount of wealth and money that is behind the slave movement.

类比不完美，但结构相似

And I am not trying to say that the tech bros are like slaveholders or that there's a clear analogy in any way. But I think that there's a lesson here about the accumulation of money and power and how that works in politics. And that Lincoln has some things to say about that. It's not straightforward. I'm not saying that the founders were all arguing for economic equality or equality of outcomes, but that there's a problem with an incredible amount of accumulation of wealth because it turns into a kind of despotism and it attracts tyrants.

So — in terms of the tech side of things, it's not really — I realized in writing the book that to do justice to all of that was not going to be in my capacity in the time frame I had to write it. So it's in there a little bit.

Kristen: Yeah, oh yeah, yeah, yeah.

Laura: But I have book recommendations, and I think you know a lot about that. So, I mean, I'm sure you can — I do think it matters. It's obviously mattered an enormous amount, and some of these guys are really enriching themselves a tremendous amount currently. And I think that — the whole — it seems to me like Silicon Valley has really benefited from aligning with Trump. I don't know that all of them are ideologues in the way that someone like Thiel is or Musk is. And so I think that there's still — I don't think it's all kind of ideologues or all the way down in Silicon Valley. So — but I don't have super refined thoughts on that.

国家俘获（State Capture）作为民主问题

Kristen: Well, yeah. So this is great. And that's why I had a feeling I was going to really like what you're going to have to say, because it's also very compatible with what — there's been a sort of theme — yeah, I shouldn't say it's accidental — I mean, it's definitely deliberate — with it.

But our first episode this season was with Sam Bagg, who's a political theorist who writes about state capture as a democratic problem, and a theory of democracy as a solution to a way of counteracting state capture by any kind of one big group, right? And dispersion of powers, the title. And we talked to Henry Farrell. So I talked to Maria Pia Paganelli about state capture and Smith and Sam. We have an episode with Gloria Liu coming out soon.

Laura: Wow.

Kristen: And so that is 100% to me — if you look at the founding, I think — to bring us back again to Tom's great articles and essay that I read, and hopefully we'll talk to him again — is that Jefferson, what Tom argues (and I think he's not alone in this) is that Jefferson kind of made this deliberate decision early in his career — not just him, other founders — to — they had to figure out a way to — this is also what happens with the Constitution — a coalition of creating and defending a republic.

And they, in the process, set aside the tricky problem of slavery. But setting aside the problem of slavery set up the conflict that would become the Civil War — because you also entrenched a powerful interest group. Yeah. An interest group that derived its power and its wealth from the oppression of human beings, right — of this gross violation of humanity, right? And so, yes, it's inherently wrapped up in morality, and then people make these arguments for the sort of positive good defense of slavery. But also it's also just interest. It's also the fact that people have this pecuniary interest in the system as it is.

And it's incredibly difficult to unroll that, especially as it becomes ideologically and morally justified. Right. I mean, it becomes even more of a cause for war. Right.

AI、硅谷与个人自主性的丧失

And so I agree with you. We should not oversimplify or, you know, obviously I don't mean to malign people in Silicon Valley by comparing them to people who own slaves, by any means. I really again just really want to emphasize that's not what I think is useful about this analogy.

It's still — it's an analogy of what do you do with entrenched special interest groups, especially when it's not just wealth — which I agree with that kind of framing there — but it's also wealth that is dependent on a particular kind of institutional political-economic policies, and also creating those policies, also lobbying for the government to have certain policies that benefit — that may be impinging upon the autonomy of individuals, right? So you could also put it in this way.

I mean, people feel very frustrated with AI, just as an example, for a lot of reasons. I mean, honestly, I think a lot of the issues we take with it come from — I think they resemble problems we were already having in society with privacy and data collection on social media platforms and the internet more broadly. But AI, I think, brings it to a whole other level, where people feel that they're — material, anything that they post online, things that they've published, authors suing tech companies because their copyrighted materials are being used to make incredible returns for these companies. And the companies at the same time, and maybe this will not be the case, but at the same time are kind of pitching their products as a way of replacing or kind of substituting for human-generated knowledge. Right.

And so how is it that people — if they're being told that there is this very frightening economy that's coming to play and also presumably this does reduce the incentives for people to generate knowledge on their own originally. And there's all these issues in the publishing world with people. You know, you can't — if you use an LLM to generate your book, you can't copyright that. So what are you going to do? Right. We have these incredible problems. Yeah.

And an ordinary person is going to look at major tech companies that are valued at enormous amounts of money and also seem to be getting closed-door meetings at the White House. And all this wrapped up — you know, power on the social kind of economic side, getting wrapped up and having more access to the political side of things, especially at the executive level, especially as power is getting wielded mostly by the executive rather than by Congress.

For the ordinary person, how are you supposed to look at that and not feel as though you are being pushed aside?

Laura: It's a democratic problem.

Kristen: It's also a problem from Republican political theory, too. If people want to say that America's not a democracy, it's a Republic — well, it's a feeling of being threatened, of this possible domination.

Laura: And of not being a self-governing person, right? Exactly. Not having autonomy.

Kristen: And then not having autonomy, again, in a personal way. If you feel like at work you can't exercise the autonomy, you can't do things the way that you want to do things. Or have no choice in where you end up in the economy because of your circumstances.

Laura: Yeah, because you've, you know —

Kristen: And also as students who are booing graduation speakers for talking about AI, who feel — and I think it's complicated why students are having a hard time getting jobs out of college. I think it's more than just AI.

Laura: All — yeah, it's also the war and it's wars. Yeah.

Kristen: And our economy is more than just — yes, this is an AI moment, but it's more than just AI. And there is also this risk of the hype kind of being used as cover for actual other decisions that are made.

Laura: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

Kristen: And so we don't want to reduce everything to that. But my point is that there's this personal autonomy question of, like, in your private life feeling like you don't have control, feeling like you don't have agency, but then also looking at the political system and feeling like it doesn't represent or be responsive to you, your friends, your family.

And then there's also this element I just want to say — that it's like the way that people experience digital media and AI and the tech world right now feels overwhelmingly like they are just passive consumers, right? Like it's very hard, especially these — you know, the best, you know — Claude, OpenAI, ChatGPT, a lot of these are proprietary models and people don't really know how they work. There is this sort of promise that like you can do Claude Code, you can create with it. But I think there's still — people are still figuring out this way in which how is it that they're actually going to be empowered to do these things, and are mostly seeing the ways in which they're being kind of told like, oh, you don't need to write. You don't need to know how to express yourself anymore.

公民教育、人文学科与"自由的教育"

Laura: Okay, I'm going to bring this around. Okay. You're very sort of eloquent on these subjects. But I'm going to bring it back to this question of what we do next and the Declaration. Because I think that — and this is going to get hokey — but I think what you're describing is real. There's this real sense of a loss of agency. And I think that ties to this question of civic education, right? And what education does for us and what students should be getting out of their education.

And this is where I side with some of the conservatives and some of the ways they talk about these things. But we need an education for liberty. Right. And if there's one thing that the Declaration does or that John Locke does, it's teach people how to think like free people.

Right. If you read — you can't just read the opening abstract statements. And this was great. You know, Osita's book, right? Osita Nwanevu has this book called The Right of the People. And it's talking about — you've got to read the rest of the Declaration where it's pretty inspiring. You know, you've got these rights. You get to control your future. You get to get together. And if you feel like there's an injustice, you get to act.

And our history is full of ways of thinking about this — of resources for learning how to be a free person and to live a life of meaning and of contestation and deliberation. And all of these things matter for democracy and for republicanism. But I think that — that's one thing.

It can sound really inadequate to talk about civic education as a response to all of the problems that you're describing, and I don't think it's the full solution. But I think that we do have resources in our tradition — in all kinds. I don't mean there's no singular tradition, right? There's so much, though. And I think that more of us need to become equipped for this kind of contestation and action.

通过古人训练当代人的判断力

And it's so partisan, which is also a problem. But one thing that I love that the history of political philosophy does — and just studying history does, and especially studying antiquity and also comparative politics or comparative literature — you get, if you start doing these things, you start reading from other parts of history where people are thinking really differently. It forces you out of the easy categories of our world. Right.

And so suddenly you're not thinking in partisan terms. You're thinking — but you're still working on reasoning, and you're reasoning politically in a different context. And that means you have to kind of get rid of your own knee-jerk prejudices and think more expansively. And I think that's part of being a citizen, too. And so that you can get out of your own partisan mode and think about the future in new ways. Right. And just think differently and not have such a knee-jerk reaction against the sort of political opposition.

And I think that's what we need. We need creativity. We need ambition and we need a kind of delicacy with one another, too.

学院、红州公民机构与两党的可能空间

And I think that for me — I guess this is what I'm comfortable talking about because I'm familiar with the academy and academia, but I actually believe it really matters, and not just for people at the Ivy Leagues. The right has these programs in red states, these civics institutions. I wish that there could be much more of this that was much more bipartisan and full of contestation, so it wasn't sort of one version of history, but a much more expansive.

And I think that this is the moment. You know, I wish people would be doing more of that and trying to find ways to revive the humanities. I think it speaks to our alienation from technology and our problem, and just it would allow us to be more conversant about what it is we're so uncomfortable with, right? And where we get our meaning from and why we're frightened and what to do about it. And so I think there's just a huge opportunity here that I think would be very popular.

So that's my two cents and my pitch for liberal democracy and for reading the Declaration, right, Kristen. And a little maybe a little more John Locke.

分歧、合作与谨慎的希望

Kristen: Yeah, no, I love that. I think that's excellent. And I do want to underscore, just to bring it all around, that part of what the value is about having these spaces where we communicate and study and discuss and debate and argue — is that we also then see, like, our positions, we gain a distance or sense proportion from like our commitment.

Laura: Yeah.

Kristen: And then that allows us to again be able to both, like, be able to articulate our own perspective and what we stand for.

Laura: And where it comes from.

Kristen: And where it comes from. And then also be able to listen and learn from other people who disagree and not feel as though to just to disagree is suddenly an attack. Because I don't — you know, I do think there is a way in which there's this twin problem of people feeling as though disagreement is somehow incompatible with cooperation and with our own kind of self-development, when in fact it's part and parcel.

Laura: Yeah, and part of a — I mean, we've agreed a lot in this talk. So that's — maybe we're not the best example of this. But you do have — I mean, what happens through a deliberative conversation with an antagonist or just someone where you disagree on some stuff is you find the places where you agree and you disagree, and then you maybe find the places where you can cooperate.

Right. And I think in the United — I think probably given our political reality right now, and this might just be me with my sort of optimistic — you know, that's just my nature — is I think there's probably a lot of people who would be pretty open to some compromising and some cooperation. And I think that if we can find leaders who can articulate some of that, I think there will be an appetite for it. Or that's my naive hope.

Kristen: Yeah. Well, I love to end on something hopeful. And I'm not going to say it's naive. I think it's always important to hold out hope for the future.

So is there anything that you wanted to get to that we didn't get to talk about? I think we had a lot of great — we covered a lot, I think. And I really love talking to you, Laura, always.

Laura: Me too, Kristen. And I'm so glad we get to share this.

Kristen: Me too. And is there anything else you want to share before we go about anything you're working on and you're excited to talk about?

Laura: No, I think I'm good. Thank you. I loved being here. I loved talking with you. This is kind of the end of — I've been super busy. And so this is one of my last conversations for a while. And I'm very, very happy that it was with you. So thank you.

Kristen: Oh, I'm so grateful that you took the time to chat with us. So thank you so much, Laura.

Laura: Thank you.

主持人尾声反思

I am beyond grateful that Laura took the time to chat. And as usual, there's a few points I wanted to reflect on to wrap up this episode.

MAGA 新右派知识分子的地图

The MAGA New Right intellectuals that Laura discusses in her book create the philosophical and theoretical foundations that justify President Trump's first term and his general approach to governance, including into this new term. And the value of engaging with these is both to understand them on their own terms and also to chart the American right to move away from the old alliance between traditional conservatives and libertarians.

This is not at all to say that the book covers all perspectives — either of people who are big fans of President Trump or Vice President J.D. Vance, nor all the people who identify as being on the right or center right, or conservative, Republican — regardless of their stance on these new developments.

She breaks the groups down into:

  • The Claremonters or Claremonsters, as I understand they like to be called — who are largely Straussians and followers of the political philosopher Leo Strauss and his students, including Harry Jaffa.
  • The post-liberals, who tend to be socially conservative Catholic thinkers.
  • National conservatives.
  • And those who Laura refers to as being on the hard right — these are the most extreme views who sometimes or really often express outright misogynistic, racist, antisemitic, white nationalist points of view.

"观念优先"的诱惑与危险

For Laura, despite disagreement within this coalition, these groups share an "ideas-first" approach to politics, which is generally a view that politics is downstream of culture. So that even means diagnosing a lot of today's ills as they see it to be because of changes in political philosophy and ideas from, honestly, the 17th century through today.

That we political theorists and others who trade in ideas are actually shaping policy and even history just by engaging in intellectual debates is a tempting view, and is common, isn't just unique to people on the right.

Now, I am a political theorist and I think ideas matter. But I'm struck by this conversation and reflecting on the history of the founding how hard it is to bring ideas into life, and how much of the actual work of politics is so much messier and harder to control.

The beauty of writing is that there's not a lot of pushback from the page or the screen. I mean, any writer knows that we have our own arguments with ourselves and our interlocutors. And even the drive to write is its own kind of force that we have to reckon with, that sometimes we feel as though we obey rather than lead. But still, it's a lot easier for me to control what I write than what happens if I try to influence policymaking — let alone dream that I could play the role of Aristotle to some political or economic leaders, Alexander the Great.

The dangers of an ideas-first approach for the right and the left alike are twofold. On the one hand, it can be hopelessly naive. Political, economic institutions, laws, policies, and social norms that are very difficult to change — at least in the directions we want them to — might have a bigger impact on what ideas gain traction in public discourse in the first place, rather than the other way around.

But also, commitment to ideas as though they are the only things that matter can leave one ill-equipped to deal with the consequences of those ideas when they actually begin to bear fruit in politics — when one uses them to delegitimize elections, let's say, and then we see a massive mob attack the Capitol building, just to give an example.

杰斐逊的悲剧与自我欺骗

Now, I think a good antidote to this dangerous naivety about the power of ideas is to reflect on history and the founding. Laura and I discussed Tom Merrill's work on Thomas Jefferson, and I'll link to one of his publicly accessible pieces in the show notes.

Merrill highlights the tragedy of Jefferson's self-contradictions. On the one hand, Jefferson expressed a beautiful commitment to individual rights in the Declaration, one that remains crucial for extending rights to people who were excluded at that time throughout history to today. While later in his life, Jefferson ended up lending support to a movement to not only defend slavery but expand it into the territories via the Missouri Compromise.

I think we need to face up to the self-delusion of figures that we might wish we could just reduce to heroes, because it helps us see the risks and dangers from when we try to realize our ideas in politics — which necessarily demands of us compromise, bargaining, and the exercise of power. Without proper humility and respect for critics, as well as the many diverse perspectives and interests held by others in our community, including the most vulnerable, human beings are apt to delude ourselves about our own ethical perfection and to deny that we might be complicit or even outright, if unintentionally, supporting and entrenching oppressive policies and institutions.

与经典阅读的对话：为多元社会储备心智肌肉

Knowing that ideas are not everything, Laura and I discussed the benefit of engaging with political philosophy anyway. This includes the value of the classic great books, but also all sorts of other works that might be traditionally excluded. All of these help us encounter totally different perspectives and get us beyond what we're used to thinking, and our own ordinary assumptions that we take for granted. It also helps us move beyond the simplistic right-to-left or other partisan labels that we're used to.

I challenge you to read the classics like Plato or Rousseau or Adam Smith and see that they could be easily categorized as Republicans or Democrats in the American sense today.

This does have to be done, though, through a true commitment to pluralism and openness to criticism — a real attempt to expand what we might consider the canon, including criticisms of figures that some might want to just treat as simple heroes, like the founders.

This isn't going to solve all of our problems we've discussed throughout this season about the problems of state capture, of powerful interest groups that might be more able to impact policy and leaving other individuals and groups feeling disempowered and ignored by our government. But engaging in this intellectual discourse and taking seriously critique and disagreement might help us both refine our own perspectives, maybe change those perspectives, and perhaps most importantly, give us the intellectual and affective skills and habits required of individuals who have to live in a free and pluralistic society peacefully with one another.

I hope that this podcast helps you engage in that kind of thinking and trying to go beyond the everyday partisan debates.

Thank you so much for listening and joining me on this episode. Thanks to Laura for joining as well. Till next time.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：MAGA新右派其实是"最像左派"的当代右派
最令人意外的洞见藏在这场对话的**方法论**层面，而非内容层面。Laura 反复提到这些右派知识分子的"ideas-first"（观念优先）方法——认为"政治是文化的下游"，只要重塑观念就能重塑政治。这句口号出自 Bannon，但其思想血统真正的祖父，其实是 **葛兰西（Gramsci）** 的"文化霸权"理论。
换句话说，Claremont、后自由主义者、国家保守派——这批人在"如何改变社会"的**操作系统**层面，运行的是新左派 1960s-70s 的代码：长征穿越体制、占领机构（Heritage、ISI）、培养干部、构建反霸权的意识形态叙事。他们指控进步派"通过教育与文化摧毁美国"，同时**逐字复制了进步派的策略手册**。这就是为什么 Patrick Deneen 读起来像倒过来的 Marcuse，Yoram Hazony 的"民族"话语在结构上极像早期认同政治——只是把阶级/种族换成了"heritage Americans"。
这构成一个隐藏的系统性真相：**当代美国政治光谱的两极，在"元政治"（meta

item.title: [Lex Clips] Taxation in the Roman Empire - historian explains | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的悖论：帝国越"专制"，反而越难产生"深层国家"

大多数人会本能地认为——庞大的官僚机构 + 集权皇帝 = 深层国家的温床。但 Kaldellis 在这里给出了一个反直觉的观察：罗马帝国拥有比现代任何民主国家都更庞大、更纵深的官僚层级，却几乎没有出现"官僚系统合谋架空领袖"的现象。

这里藏着一个精妙的时间尺度错配理论：深层国家之所以在现代民主中形成，本质上是"永续的行政机构"对"临时的政治领导人"的一种自我防御性演化。而在罗马，皇帝的时间尺度和官僚的时间尺度是同频的——他理论上可以在位到死，甚至传给儿子。当上下两层的"生存期"匹配时，寄生性的隐形政治就失去了进化压力。

跨领域联想：这其实和生物学里的宿主-寄生虫共进化模型高度相似。寄生虫之所以进化出复杂的"隐藏机制"，是因为宿主寿命远超单个寄生虫世代；一旦二者世代长度接近，寄生策略会退化为共生策略。深层国家，本质上是一种"政治寄生虫"，只有在"宿主任期短"的生态位下才会被自然选择出来。

戴克里先的真正革命：不是税收，而是"可预测性"这种稀缺资源

表面上看，戴克里先做的事是"扩大税基"（意大利不再免税）+"普查资产"。但真正被低估的洞见是——他创造了古代世界最稀缺的东西：预算。

在此之前，罗马财政几乎是"打到哪算哪"的现金流游戏，皇帝靠战利品、临时征税、没收富人财产维系。戴克里先第一次让帝国拥有了"可预测的收入曲线"。这个东西看似枯燥，但它是所有现代国家能存在的前提——能否借贷、能否养常备军、能否规划十年后的工程，全都建立在"未来现金流可估算"上。

跨领域连接：这和 17 世纪荷兰发明"永续债券"、19 世纪铁路公司发明"折旧会计"、20 世纪凯恩斯发明"国民账户体系"是同一条主线上的事件。戴克里先其实是第一个 CFO 型统治者，而不是通常被描绘的"军事强人"。他给帝国装上的不是新的剑，而是新的资产负债表。而拜占庭之所以能比西部多活一千年，本质上是因为这套会计基础设施在东部保存得更完整——不是文化，不是宗教，不是地理，是记账方式。

"土地拥有人"到"土地拥有人"：被殖民语汇遮蔽的经济事实

访谈里有一句一闪而过、但极为锋利的话："most of the time it's people who own the land. And in this arrangement, it's the land who owns the people."（通常是人拥有土地，但在这种安排下，是土地拥有了人。）

这不仅是修辞。它精确描述了一种财政拓扑学——当税收单元从"人"切换到"土地"，人和土地的主客关系就反转了。农民之所以被"绑定"，不是封建领主锁着他，而是因为国家需要土地上有一个"可寻址的纳税人"。人成了土地的税务代理。

让人意外的跨时代对照：这个逻辑在今天以另一种形态回归——现代平台经济中的"账号绑定"。你的抖音账号、你的微信号、你的信用记录，本质上和罗马晚期的"土地绑农户"是同构的：平台需要一个可寻址的收入生成节点，而你恰好是那个节点。所谓"数字农奴"的说法不是隐喻，是财政拓扑学意义上的精确类比。

一个更冷的观察：Kaldellis 说"没人抱怨不想当兵"，因为当兵有丰厚福利。这暗示了一个我们容易忽视的规律——当强制义务与实际收益对齐时，"强制"本身就消失在感知层。今天很多人抱怨"996"、抱怨"平台锁定"，从罗马经验看，问题不在于"是否被绑定"，而在于绑定的收益端是否还在兑现。戴克里先系统稳定运行三个世纪，靠的不是暴力，而是"绑你 + 让你划算"这套双向合同。一旦收益端崩塌，绑定就变成叛乱的引信。这对任何现代组织——公司、平台、国家——都是同一条规律。

📝 转录文本

一、戴克里先创造了"深层国家"吗？

Lex： So, just to linger on that point, is it fair to say that Diocletian created the deep state, quote, "deep state"? So, the tax collectors, the logistics, the thing that you've kind of hinted at, which is the separation of the civil and the military layer of society.

Kaldellis： So, the concept of a deep state — I've had discussions about that with experts who work on the idea of the deep state. And I'm not sure that I would characterize the Roman Empire that way, in part because while you do have big government, you do have bureaucracy, you have bureaucrats who are buried in various echelons, and nobody knows exactly what they're doing and things like that. But I have not come across the idea of some sort of sinister elements who are working within the administrative apparatus to undermine the emperor.

From a distance, occasionally you might get glimpses of this kind of thing. Once [an emperor] complained that "nobody ever tells me anything." In other words, he's so removed from the realities of what's going on on the ground that he felt sometimes he'd lost touch. But I don't find this idea of undermining imperial power from within the bureaucracy.

二、为什么罗马难以形成"深层国家"？——时间尺度的错配

Lex： Yeah, I think one of the ways to explain that is: in the modern-day democracy, in quotes, there's a kind of temporary nature to the leader at the top. So it incentivizes the deep state to form. The deep state thinks of themselves as a thing that propagates long-term periods of time, and then the leaders are very ephemeral, short-term things.

With an emperor, technically, you can go for a long time, so it's best to be integrated deeply — with the bureaucracy should be integrated with the leader.

Kaldellis： Yeah, makes sense.

Lex： Also, in modern social thought, the fear is that the deep state might be undermining the democracy itself — rather than it can cut both ways. But there is a bureaucracy. There is a big government. There's a big machinery.

Kaldellis： Yeah, compared to before, yes.

三、戴克里先的模式：为什么能延续三个世纪？

So Diocletian creates these larger bureaucratic structures, and these are the ones that define the society that I study. As a specialist, I focus on the period after him mostly. But he implemented those to solve a lot of these problems. He was mostly successful — in terms of generating revenue from a much more troubled and precarious situation, sustaining large armies, and putting down these rebellions and usurpations. He was generally successful at doing that. So he created the model that they kept for the next well, three centuries or more.

Lex： But a lot of the elements of that model lasted even beyond that, right?

四、普遍征税：意大利不再是"避税天堂"

Kaldellis： Yeah — like universal taxation. I'll give you an example. Up until then, Italy was exempt from taxes. Italy was the land of the conquerors. The conquerors don't pay taxes to themselves. The conquerors live off the taxes that others pay, right? So Italy was this big tax haven.

Diocletian, Galerius, you know, his guys — these guys are from Illyria. They're from the Balkans. They got: "Why is Italy tax-free exactly? That doesn't make any sense. It's a very, very wealthy, prosperous part of the world." And so — no, they tax Italy.

So now, not only do all Romans have the same citizenship and the same law, they all have the same tax system. And the Diocletianic tax code, as it were — it's not a code, it's almost like a flat tax, which for us sounds regressive, but by the standards of that time it's actually kind of progressive in a way. It means that even elites paid the same.

五、税收：帝国的一切核心？

Lex： So, how much of all of this — everything we'll talk about in the coming centuries — is all about taxation?

Kaldellis： I think it's huge. Taxation is at the heart of it.

Lex： [laughter] So the functioning, the success, the flourishing of society, the way the government works, the way the people are represented — all of it has to do with the civil wars and everything. It feels like taxation is at the core.

Kaldellis： Yes. If you ask me to put my finger on one factor, it would be that.

Lex： So the taxation system — that feels like the early framework for the Eastern Empire.

Kaldellis： It did create a more sort of homogeneous framework, for sure. By the end of the 3rd century, it's pretty much in place. What Diocletian does is a universal census. And this is continued by his successors. So every taxable asset in the empire is censused. And so, you can now start to have a budget.

六、法律限制流动：真的是"种姓制度"吗？

Lex： Now, there is some element — and then you push back against that — some element of Diocletian where he legally limited social and class mobility, in part for the taxation system. It made it in many cases illegal to move and illegal to quit your job.

For example, as I read: to guarantee the food supply and land tax, farmers were legally forbidden from moving away from the land they worked, so the state always knew who to tax. And then for soldiers, sons of veterans were legally obligated to serve in the army to maintain troop numbers.

You argue that these were tax enforcement mechanisms, not a rigid caste system. And so, in practice, the empire remained a society of high mobility.

Kaldellis： This is a complicated matter.

七、士兵与行会：世袭为何自然发生？

Let's talk, for example, about soldiers. It's probably the case that most soldiers' sons were expected to succeed them. Like most professions where you have an apprenticeship, the son will usually succeed, right?

There are a number of other professions that were kind of guild-like in nature — like, for example, shipping — where there were considerable assets involved, in order to secure contracts, for insurance purposes, for the ships, and so forth. And it made sense for those assets to be passed from father to son. In other words, with them comes the obligation to continue in that line of work.

Some of these things are inherently sort of hereditary. And I don't think most people would have objected to it.

八、被土地"拥有"的农民

The farmers is probably the only case where it might have actually involved some coercion.

There was a category of farmers — and we don't know how many of them — who were, for fiscal purposes, bound to the land. This doesn't mean they couldn't leave the land, like physically — no one was preventing them from leaving — but they were fiscally responsible for providing the taxes or rents or whatever, because the state wanted to have a secure revenue stream from, say, those lands.

It's one of these complicated situations. This did create some limitations on those people. It's a topic of current research — these "bound farmers," as it were, who are tied to the land.

Someone put it that: "most of the time it's people who own the land. And in this arrangement, it's the land who owns the people."

九、总体图景：一个高流动性的社会

Lex： But you're saying the bigger picture thing is that there was mobility. So there's class mobility and physical mobility, meaning people moved. And we'll see this from the west into the east and Constantinople and all this kind of stuff.

Kaldellis： There's tons of mobility. Yeah. No, this is not a society where people are bound, really. We don't find anybody who's like, "I really don't want to be a soldier, but my father was a soldier and I'm obligated to be one" — we just don't hear those kinds of complaints, of people who don't want to be a soldier.

Why wouldn't you want to be a soldier in the Roman Empire? I mean, it's got tremendous perks.

Lex： Right, right.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽略的悖论：帝国越"专制"，反而越难产生"深层国家"
大多数人会本能地认为——庞大的官僚机构 + 集权皇帝 = 深层国家的温床。但 Kaldellis 在这里给出了一个反直觉的观察：罗马帝国拥有比现代任何民主国家都更庞大、更纵深的官僚层级，却几乎没有出现"官僚系统合谋架空领袖"的现象。
这里藏着一个精妙的**时间尺度错配理论**：深层国家之所以在现代民主中形成，本质上是"永续的行政机构"对"临时的政治领导人"的一种自我防御性演化。而在罗马，皇帝的时间尺度和官僚的时间尺度是**同频**的——他理论上可以在位到死，甚至传给儿子。当上下两层的"生存期"匹配时，寄生性的隐形政治就失去了进化压力。
**跨领域联想**：这其实和生物学里的宿主-寄生虫共进化模型高度相似。寄生虫之所以进化出复杂的"隐藏机制"，是因为宿主寿命远超单个寄生虫世代；一旦二者世代长度接近，寄生策略会退化为共生策略。深层国家，本质上是一种"政治寄生虫"，只有在"宿主任期短"的生态位下才会被自然选择出来。
## 戴克里先的真正革命：不是税收，而是"可预测性"这种稀缺资源
表面上看，戴克里先做的事是"扩大税基

item.title: [Lex Clips] The 3 biggest threats to the Roman Empire | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"慢变量霸权"：帝国的真实生命是无聊的

Kaldellis 指出的这个反直觉事实值得反复咀嚼：一个存在了千年以上的罗马帝国，其"戏剧性时刻"加起来不过几十年——630年代的阿拉伯征服、1070年代的塞尔柱冲击、1204年的第四次十字军。其余 95% 以上的时间，它都在做一件历史书不爱写的事：缓慢地长大。

这里藏着一个认知偏差的经典陷阱——我们在讲述任何长寿系统（帝国、企业、婚姻、身体）时，总倾向于用"危机时刻"来概括它的一生，仿佛稳态不值得叙述。但真实的秩序恰恰相反：能持续千年的东西，其本质从不在高潮里，而在日复一日的、几乎无事可记的复利式积累中。这也解释了为什么大多数关于"如何伟大"的教科书都在误导读者——它们只研究爆点，而爆点是幸存者的装饰品，不是骨骼。

二、一个让人后背发凉的观察：拜占庭的三次致命打击，有两次来自"自己人"

细看这三个崩溃节点，会发现一个几乎被主流叙事回避的模式：

  • 阿拉伯征服（外部异质文明）——预期之内
  • 塞尔柱人（外部异质文明）——预期之内
  • 第四次十字军（1204）——名义上的"基督教兄弟"洗劫了基督教最伟大的城市

也就是说，罗马帝国最惨烈的三次伤口里，有一次是结构性盟友的背叛造成的，而且这是唯一一次首都被攻破。这个模式在系统论里有个残酷的推论：一个系统真正的致命脆弱性，往往不来自它明确防御的敌人，而来自它假设不需要防御的"自己人"。

跨领域看，这几乎就是安然公司、苏联解体、2008 金融危机的同构版本——摧毁大型系统的从来不是外部敌人的正面攻击，而是"内部信任协议"的突然反转。企业防火墙做得再厚，也挡不住董事会的一次内斗；免疫系统防御外敌再强，也治不好自身免疫疾病。罗马的教训不是"外面有蛮族"，而是"你以为的同盟可能是熵值最高的那扇门"。

三、历史学家与数据科学家共享的同一个原罪：把过程压成日期

Kaldellis 关于"476 年、395 年这些日期其实是我们贴上去的标签，真实过程持续数十年"的说法，看似是史学方法论，实则揭示了一切人类认知的通病：我们无法忍受"无边界的过程"，所以强行发明"节点"来让世界可讲述。

这与数据科学中的 change point detection（变点检测）、金融中的"熊市起点"定义、医学中的"疾病发作日"完全同构——都是把连续函数暴力离散化的产物。它带来一个非常反直觉的推论：

你人生中那些自认为的"转折点"（分手日、跳槽日、开悟日），大概率也是你事后为了让自己的故事讲得通而虚构的日期。真实的转变发生在你说不出具体某天的漫长阴影中。

而 Kaldellis 特别保留了一个例外——君士坦丁皈依基督教。这个例外的意义惊人：它承认了"少数拥有极端权力的个体做出的不可预测决策，确实能撬动几个世纪"。这几乎是 Taleb 黑天鹅理论的古典版本，也是对"历史决定论"最优雅的一次刺穿。

啊哈时刻： 历史学家其实分成两派——一派认为一切皆过程（结构主义者），一派认为一切皆偶然（事件史家）。Kaldellis 在这段简短发言里，实际上完成了一次教科书级别的方法论调和：过程是常态，但拥有极端权力的个体偶尔可以打破常态。这句话如果放进管理学，就是"文化决定 95%，CEO 决定 5%，但那 5% 决定你是否还存在。"

📝 转录文本

帝国的三次致命时刻

If you wanted to produce a sort of very swift timeline of its history, um it's obviously important to focus on the moments when uh lots of territory was lost to foreign invaders. And these are actually three main moments.

  • There's the Arab conquests in the 630s. This is a decade. In a decade, that war was lost.
  • It is the Seljuk Turk conquest of Asia Minor, modern Turkey, in the 1070s. And that was very swift and partly reversed afterwards, but only partly.
  • And then there's the Fourth Crusade, 1204, when the armies from mostly France sack, conquer Constantinople and dismember the empire as much as they could. Um it didn't last that long. The Romans managed to regroup and recover.

Uh but those are the three main crisis points. They are very swift, uh but they cause incredible damage.

缓慢生长才是历史的主旋律

For all the rest of its history, it is a state and society that is generally um sort of consolidating, uh you know, um regrouping, and generally engaged in slow but steady economic growth, territorial um expansion, a small-scale but steady. Uh so, we can talk about this. Um it's I think it's an important thing to to to keep in mind about the big picture, that the defeats were swift and kind of, you know, terrible. Uh but the most of its history is one of slow growth.

There's some traumatic events that have a big impact, but a lot of the developments we'll talk about, uh it might seem or feel like there's a particular moment when those developments or changes in the way the government or the military structured arise, but really it's a gradual process. That's the tension we have to be constantly dancing with.

史学方法：事件与过程的辩证

Yeah, so as historians this is a point of method and it's very important.

Uh there are moments when like individuals with a great deal of power make choices that impact everybody else and have you know long-term consequences downstream. Constantine's conversion to Christianity for example, right? Not something that anybody could have or did predict.

Okay. Otherwise, most of the developments that we identify by specific dates — like you mentioned 395, the division of the Eastern and the Western Empires — those are conventional dates that we use to actually capture what is in fact a much longer process. There was no split between the two halves. It was one among many allocations of jurisdiction to like one son and another son in the East and the West. The two halves were subsequently reunited, then they kind of divided again among different emperors. It's just a process. And so we sometimes use fixed dates for what is in fact a process.

Or 476, the fall of the Western Empire. This is the last date. It's actually a very long process that took decades. Right?

So that's just something to keep in mind that sometimes you're talking about processes, sometimes you're talking about very discreet events with long-term consequences. Yeah.

观看原视频 →

## 一、被忽视的"慢变量霸权"：帝国的真实生命是无聊的
Kaldellis 指出的这个反直觉事实值得反复咀嚼：一个存在了千年以上的罗马帝国，其"戏剧性时刻"加起来不过几十年——630年代的阿拉伯征服、1070年代的塞尔柱冲击、1204年的第四次十字军。**其余 95% 以上的时间，它都在做一件历史书不爱写的事：缓慢地长大。**

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1447期】滑头凯文沃什！开源稳定币？CRCL。开仓一个医疗股✨20260630#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"理发指标"：情绪套利者的自我暴露

小桃开场的"理发梗"看似玩笑，其实暴露了一个耐人寻味的行为金融学悖论——他不是因为理发才准确，而是只在情绪低点才想去理发。这是一个典型的"内生性偏差"陷阱：当分析师的行动本身成为情绪指标时，指标与市场之间的因果关系就被彻底扭曲了。

更深的一层意外观察是：这其实是所有"感觉型交易者"的隐秘操作系统。华尔街那些说"我就是有感觉"的老交易员，他们的"感觉"往往就是这种未被明确编码的身体信号——理发、失眠、突然想吃甜食。他们本质上是在用生理系统做一种低维压缩的市场情绪采样。这解释了为什么AI量化模型再强，也难以完全替代资深交易员的直觉——不是直觉神秘，而是身体本身就是一台被市场噪音训练了几十年的传感器。

二、Open USD 的真正威胁：不是"开源"，而是"稳定币的安卓化"

小桃把 Open USD 的核心分析停留在了"美债利息分配"和"铸币费"这两个层面，但我认为他低估了这条新闻背后的结构性重排。

真正值得警惕的类比不是"USDC vs USDT"，而是iOS vs Android 之战的重演： - Circle/USDC = iOS：垂直整合、收益封闭、由 Coinbase 独家分发 - Open USD = Android：Stripe 牵头的开源联盟、生态贡献者共治

历史规律是：在支付基础设施领域，开放标准的胜出并不需要"更好"，只需要"够用+免费+可嵌入"。想想 SWIFT、TCP/IP、HTTP，甚至 Linux 服务器——它们都不是最优雅的，但因为开源可嵌入，最终吞掉了 90% 的市场。

但小桃说对了一个关键点，只是他自己没意识到有多重要：140家公司的治理会撕裂方向。这恰恰印证了另一个跨领域规律——"委员会不生产愿景，只生产妥协"。Linux 之所以成功，不是因为民主，而是因为背后有 Linus 这个技术独裁者。Open USD 如果找不到自己的"Linus"，最终会变成第二个 R3 Corda（银行联盟链的失败案例）。

三、凯文·沃什的"五个工作小组"：一个非常聪明的政治拖延术

小桃提到了这个细节但没有充分展开——这五个工作小组本质上是"制度性拖延"的教科书级操作。

跨领域连接：这与罗马元老院的"临时委员会"传统、中国明代内阁的"廷议"制度、甚至现代硅谷大公司的"OKR 校准会"是同一种权力技术——通过制造程序正当性来延迟决策，同时不承担任何决策成本。

更意外的观察是：沃什说"倾听市场声音"其实是一个贝叶斯陷阱。市场预期加息 → 他不加 → 惊吓 → 市场重估 → 他再引用"新的市场声音"来解释。这是一个可以自我合理化的无限循环。真正的鹰派和鸽派都不需要这么多委员会——只有"政治家"才需要。

四、Intuitive Surgical 的隐藏叙事：小桃自己都没说透的护城河

小桃开仓 ISRG 时纠结 PEG 3 倍偏高，但他略过了一个跨领域的结构性洞察：

达芬奇手术机器人的护城河不是技术，而是"外科医生的肌肉记忆"。一个外科医生一旦在达芬奇上完成 200 台手术，切换成本几乎是无限的——就像键盘的 QWERTY 布局、飞行员的机型认证。这种"人力资本锁定"型护城河，市场往往会给出 3-5 倍的 PEG 溢价，因为它的现金流可预测性接近公用事业。

真正的意外：ISRG 的估值下调（从 80 倍 forward P/E 掉到 36 倍）不是坏消息，而是"叙事从奢侈品切换到基础设施"的信号。当一家公司的估值曲线从"想象空间型"过渡到"稳定复利型"时，它才刚刚进入巴菲特型价值投资的射程。小桃可能低估了自己这次开仓的历史意义——这不是抄底，是叙事迁移的起点。

📝 转录文本

开场：市场概览与今日议程

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小桃，欢迎大家来到周二的更新。

今天这期内容，我们先聊一下市场，然后来看一下彭博团队写了一篇文章，分析了凯文·沃什到底是鹰还是鸽，给了他们自己的一个结论，我们一起去看这篇文章。

然后我们聊一下稳定币——今天市场里面的一些声音和新闻不是那么多，所以关于稳定币的一条新闻几乎把市场给炸开了，Circle 下跌的幅度比较大，和大家分享一下我对于今天这条稳定币新闻的解读和看法。

最后我们聊两个标的： - 一个标的是在上期视频下面有一位观众问我怎么去看待接下去麦当劳这边的一个走势，因为过去这段时间确实下跌的幅度比较大，从我的视角和大家聊一下； - 然后和大家分享一个我今天才刚刚开仓的一个标的，医疗板块里面的一个标的，其实大家可能也非常的熟悉，最后和大家分享一下到底是哪一家公司。

好吗？我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开始之前，大家记得留言、点赞、加转发。

一、周二资产价格走势

我们一起先来看一下资产价格在周二的走势。

比起很多的一些指标——恐慌贪婪指数、CNN 的恐慌贪婪指数、参与度来说，其实有一个指标我觉得准确度非常高。大家回过头去看我周日那期视频里面应该可以找到这个答案。

是什么答案？就是理发。

基本上，我不知道有没有观众有总结过，就是基本上我每一次理完发之后，接下去五天、我们就三天或者五天美股这边的走势——大家有没有做过总结？这个概率我觉得，我个人的印象里面，起码是 60% 以上了。这个胜率大家是不是同意，在留言区里面告诉我，好吧。

当然，开个玩笑，不过半开玩笑。为什么？是因为我每一次剪头发的时间其实我都挑的、我都是有挑的。因为我自己可以控制我什么时候剪。如果我能够控制什么时候剪，我能够感觉到市场的情绪面非常低，一些数据也告诉我情绪面非常低，那我自然就去理个发。除非实在是太长了，个人需求没办法。

各类资产表现

看一下周二资产价格的走势：

  • 美债市场：这边利率出现了上涨，也就是 9 月份加息这个概率再次来到了 83%、84% 左右。
  • 黄金 / TLT：TLT 出现了 1.2% 的下调。
  • 美股：三大指数都走得不错，标普 +0.8，纳指 +1.5。周一的市场也是走得不错的。
  • 芯片板块：过去两天出现了比较大幅度的反弹，周二反弹了 4.3%。两天加一起可能要反弹了将近 10%，应该有 10%。因为我记得上一周的时候最低点，YTD 到现在为止芯片板块的涨幅应该是低于 100% 的，所以从 100% 到现在 113%，涨幅要超过 10% 了。
  • 罗素 2000 / 罗素 1000 成长股：上升 1.8%。
  • 七大科技股：今天的反弹比较厉害，昨天也出现了反弹，上涨了 1.3%，那拉近了七大科技股和标普 493 YTD 到现在为止的一个走势差。

而相反，其他的一些板块今天就会相对弱一些，多数的板块都出现了下跌。也就是说整个市场的参与度在周二是出现了下跌的，即使指数出现了反弹。

宏观数据：JOLTS

周二还有一组宏观数据 JOLTS Index。从 JOLTS Index 这个数据去看，最后的结果比市场预期要高，也就是劳动力市场比市场的预期要好。

到现在为止，我原本在年初的时候认为今年的劳动力市场会放缓，但是六个月过去了，到现在为止其实我还没有看到任何劳动力市场放缓的一些信号。

比起 JOLTS Index 来说，周四早上公布的非农数据和失业率将会变得更重要一些。周五的话是休市的，大家别忘了。所以这一周的话只有四个交易日。

二、凯文·沃什：鹰还是鸽？——彭博视角

然后在明天周三 7 月 1 日，凯文·沃什将会在欧洲央行年度辛特拉会议里面有一段讲话的记忆和时间。

彭博的判断：他是政治家

从彭博这个视角，凯文·沃什到底是鹰还是鸽？彭博他们认为：首先凯文·沃什是一位政治家。所以他既可以割、要割的时候割，要鹰的时候鹰。就可以理解为是左右横跳，并没有一个非常明确自己的观点。

在美联储会议上面，凯文·沃什将重点放在了价格稳定上。他聊过很多次"我们要把通胀压回到 2%"，而且不会改变 2% 通胀这个目标。所以因此在美联储会议上的这个讲话，他的一个言论可能是偏鹰派的。那也正好借由这一次机会向市场去表明美联储是独立于川普、或者是独立于美国财政部。

五个工作小组的玄机

但是在上一次美联储会议的时候，凯文·沃什也宣布了接下去成立 5 个工作小组。

彭博他们认为，成立了这五个工作小组来研究美联储接下去的一些货币政策是非常关键的一个问题和一步，也为下一步到底是加息还是不动去争取时间。

彭博的这个视角和我们一部分的观众是比较类似的——就是凯文·沃什提出了成立五个工作小组，也就是在这五个工作小组得出结论之前，美联储并不会有任何的一些行动。他们并不会像市场现在预期加息就预期 80% 的可能进行加息而进行加息。

我的疑问：市场声音 vs. 工作小组

接下去就需要去验证了。凯文·沃什他自己说过：他会听取市场的声音，他认为市场的声音是一个非常好的用来去帮助美联储判断的一个指标之一。当然他不是说唯一的一个指标，他说是非常重要的一个指标之一。

现在市场认为 9 月份 90% 的概率要进行加息。他到底加还是不加？如果市场认为要加，他却因为说工作小组还没有得出这个结论而去不加的话，那到那一次会议之前，除非有人出来放风说市场这个判断是错了，不然的话将会给市场出现一个恐慌、出现一个惊喜或者惊吓。

而这种惊喜和惊吓，随着市场的预期不同，可能最后这个结果会不同。但是对于市场来说，这就是一个不确定性。如果这种不确定性变成一个正常的常态的话，那将会增加市场的这个活动——这并不是现在的市场所喜欢的。或者应该这么说，这并不是现在市场这种习惯和判断模式，市场需要一段时间去调整，这个调整的过程对于市场来说可能会造成一定程度的一个波动。

彭博的结论

最后彭博他们这边的一个总结是：预期周三 7 月 1 日凯文·沃什在辛特拉会议上，将会充分展现他的一个政治手腕。所以他并不是简简单单可以把他归类于鹰派或者鸽派。

其实应该这么说，鲍威尔自己本身也并不是鹰派或者鸽派，你难以去对他做一个定义。当然鲍威尔刚刚上任的时候（2018 年），当时你可以理解为是相当的鹰派，要进行加息；然后 19 年的话就变成相当的鸽派；之后一段时间包括 20 年、21 年都是非常的鸽派；直到 22 年又变得非常的鹰派，举出了 Paul Volcker；然后 23、24 年又开始变得鸽派。所以我们没办法说鲍威尔的一个任期之内他一直是鸽派或者一直是鹰派，其实确实是根据数据做出了不同的一个表态。

最后彭博他们这边的一个判断是：凯文·沃什将会回避所有有关利率路径的这个问题，他们预计美联储将在今年剩余的时间之内保持利率不变。

我的判断：与彭博有分歧

我个人的一个判断和彭博这边可能有一些出入。

我个人的一个判断就是市场的这个判断。为什么我会有这样的一个判断？我对凯文·沃什这个人本身还没有非常的了解。他也许——我是同一部分观众的一些看法，包括彭博他们今天这个看法——他有可能会利用这五个工作小组来去拖延接下去美联储的一些货币政策。他可以这么去做。

但是如果这么去做，同时他又说要听取市场的声音，而市场的声音又在告诉他要进行加息，这中间就有了一个出入了。这个出入他最终到底怎么去选择，我并不知道。所以我需要看他怎么去做——这是一个了解的过程。而在这一个了解的过程里面，我只能把他说什么当做他会做什么。他既然说他要听取市场的声音，我的假设就是他会怎么去做。

所以这就是我现在的判断：我认为市场今年将会加息 1.5 次，就是和现在债券市场所做出的判断是一样的。加息 1.5 次，或者说加息两次——包括今年、明年加在一起加息两次。

对于市场会不会造成一个非常大的影响，会不会出现像 2022 年的这种熊市？我的判断也非常明确：我并不认为会出现这种情况。因为整个市场的一个主线条、美股的一个主线条现在仍然是落在 AI 身上。

隐藏细节：PCE 数据的人为调整

在凯文·沃什的五个工作小组里面，其中有一个工作小组针对接下去美联储用来判断的一些数据——这些数据或者数据源包括一些方法到底应不应该去做出一些调整。

在工作小组得出这个结论之前，首先 BEA（美国经济分析局）他们将会对于接下去 PCE 的这组数据组成将会进行调整。而这个调整之后通胀、或者 PCE 的衡量指标将会直接下降 13 个基点或者 0.13。下降完之后虽然仍然高于 3%，但是这一个调整是有利于通胀的一个下降——就是人为去修改这个数据，使得通胀变得更好看。

三、稳定币大新闻：Open USD 会杀死 Circle 吗？

OK，然后我们来聊一下今天和稳定币相关的一些消息。

首先我个人其实不大建议大家在社交媒体上泡非常多的时间，因为很多东西都是噪音，而且很多新消息本身、包括很多 KOL 的一些判断本身，我认为是非常的浅，就是完全是停留在表面的。

表层新闻 vs. 深层逻辑

你如果今天去看新闻的话，新闻就会告诉你有 140 多家公司，基本上全球最大一些公司、你能够想到的公司全部集合在一起，然后他们将要推出一个叫做 Open USD 的稳定币，那将会对于 Circle 是致命的打击。

"致命的打击"是推断出来的这个结论，但是理由就是"140 家公司集中在一起、他们自己要推一个稳定币"，然后结论就是"USDC 不行了、或者 Circle 不行了"。这中间我不认为是有一个非常直接的因果关系。今天这条新闻本身其实背后应该会产生很多的一些问题，而并不是直接可以得出这个结论。

Open USD 到底是什么？

那这个 Open USD 是什么？可以把它理解为是一个开源项目。

这个开源项目的一个发起人是谁？是 Stripe 在上一年收购的一家公司叫做 Bridge 的一个 CEO 变成了 Open USD 发行商这家公司的一个 CEO。然后接下去会有很多的一些其他公司参与者变成董事会的成员，然后董事会去决定这家公司的一些方向。所以这个项目的一个发起人是谁？Stripe，一家支付公司。

亮点一：储备金收益共享

这个项目有几个亮点，其中有一个亮点是市场讨论度比较高的：
