我们知道现在 Circle 他们发出稳定币、发 USDC，那这个 USDC 他是以美债的这个形式所存在的，然后美债的利率或者利息全部是——就起码在现在阶段是 Circle 和 Coinbase 两家公司区分。然后不同的项目比如说 Hyperliquid，这两家公司就会把多数的一些美债的获益全部给 Hyperliquid 这家公司当做是一个渠道费，然后让 Hyperliquid 这个平台本身选择用 USDC 作为他们主要的交易配对。

所以现阶段 USDC 大多数的一些来自于美债这一块的一些收益全部是 Circle 和 Coinbase 两家公司去分的，那其实多数是进了 Coinbase 的口袋。为什么？是因为 Coinbase 其实就是 USDC 或者 Circle 的一个渠道。

那 Open USD 不一样，Open USD 是只要你是参与者、只要你是 partner，你将会一起去平分这一部分 Open USD 背后的一个 reserve、背后的一个储备金。如果说未来 Open USD 达到了 1000 亿美金，那 1000 亿美金每一年有多少美债的收益，所有的 partner 大家一起拼分。也就是说并不是两家公司、并不是现在 Circle 和 Coinbase 两家公司去拼分的。

我的质疑：这其实是个伪命题

所以可以把这一条 principle 理解为它其实是一个吸引点——吸引更多的人来参与。但其实我认为这是一个伪命题。

也就是大多数的公司来参与，它并不是看中这一个 USD 一个 reserve，它并不是看中美债这一块的一些利息，它不是那么看中的。它主要看中的是：任何一家公司只要能够进入你这个生态，他看重的是你这个生态可以去辅助他们原本现有的这个生态——就是你要不就是改善现有的这个生态，从各个角度：廉价、价格是不是便宜，或者说是不是便捷、是不是有人用。

所以其他公司既然选择加入你这一个 project 的话，一定不会因为你付给他们一些美债的收益率他们才过来的，不会的。一定是因为你这个生态本身值得进来。

亮点二：治理模式的两面性

然后另外一点就是 governance，就是整个公司的一个治理。未来接下去公司的一些方向是由所有参与者——首先这是一个独立的公司，然后有很多的参与者组成一个董事会或者组成一个委员会，董事会或者委员会一起去决定这家公司的一个方向。

这就会出现一个问题：你背后有很多家代表——你是放银行进来、你还是放贝莱德进来、还是放其他的资管公司进来、还是放其他的支付公司进来？每一家公司对于稳定币都有自己不同的需求和痛点。他当然希望你 Open USD 网的一个路径是能够解决自己的痛点，而不是其他公司的痛点。包括你未来的方向、你所去的方向会不会破坏我现有的业务？比如说从 Visa、从 Mastercard 这些角度去触发，他们也会往这个方向去思考。

所以这种治理的模式很难说好还是坏。你不同的公司进来，公司之间有不同的需求，大多数就所有的公司在一起，能不能一条心把这家公司本身往一个方向去带？这是不一定能够实现的。反而是一家公司埋头苦干先去解决市场里面最大的痛点，反而能够做好。所以不一定说 Open USD 这种模式就一定会比 Circle 这种模式要好或者坏。

我的总结：几个关键判断

所以这边我总结了几点：

第一点，我前面说过了，Open USD 的一个 CEO 是来自于 Stripe 所收购的 Bridge 这家公司的一个 CEO 出任了 Open USD CEO。所以这个项目本身是由 Stripe 所牵头所牵起的。其实 Stripe 基本上和很多大公司之间都有合作，所以他们把这些大公司就全部拉进来。但至于这些大公司最后的参与程度，真的不一定，也不可能所有的大公司都花非常多的精力在你这个项目身上。

而且最重要的是你这个项目本身的一个钱哪里来——激励机制非常的重要。任何的开源项目，你前期如果有任何一家公司投入非常多的钱下去，那是不是能够主导了你这家公司的一个方向呢？你既然是一个开源项目，也就是说大家的意见都应该听取，大家的意见可能都应该采纳，最终你应该是采纳谁的意见？

所以 140 多家公司在支付、转账、结算都有自己的需求，最终怎么去平衡？公司 vision 这个方向到底是什么？把美债利息营收这一块储蓄的运营费用——公司本身要有运营费用的——储蓄运营费用分摊到其他所有的参与者。其实这种模式和币圈里面那些 DAO 的项目是非常类似的：大家一起去建设。这一类的方式问题就是：如果缺少了足够的激励，没人会去建设的。今天贝莱德凭什么要来帮你建设这个项目？除非我自己本身有非常大的 incentive，因为你这个项目的一个方向是会利好我现有的这个业务，这样的话他才会来建设。或者说我从中可以聊到利益。

举个简单的：为什么我们很多付钱看的这些付钱看、或者说我们要看广告的这类产品，会比免费的产品要好？

亮点三：零铸币费/销毁费的迷思

然后这个项目有另外一个特色，就是 mint 和 redeem 它是没有费用的，就是铸币和销毁的费用是没有的，也算是这个项目的一个特色之一。

但是有很多人也是担心，就好比现在 Circle 一部分的一个营收是来自于铸币和销毁的费用——也就是你公司今天要用我 USDC，你在换成、把美金换成 USDC 的时候你要付费，你在销毁的时候也需要付费。

但是你想一下，为什么现在公司愿意去支付这个费用？为什么今天公司愿意把美金、把 USD 换成 USDC，然后用 USDC 去完成他们——无论他们想做什么这件事情——完成了这件事情之后再把 USDC 转成 USD？就中间的铸币和销毁的费用为什么这些公司愿意去用？那么为什么他们愿意去支付这个费用？

背后的一个理由有且只有一个：就是 USDC 在使用场景里面比美金的使用场景要便捷、要高效，或者有其他的一些 benefit，能够理解吗？

所以铸币费和销毁费从来都不是任何一家公司愿不愿意去用你这个稳定币的一个理由。所以最终无论是 USDC、USDT 还是 Open USD 都可以，最终我并不认为能够把大家吸引来一起去建立你这个生态的是你分发给他们美债的这些营收，或者说没有铸币费、没有销毁费——并不是这样，而是你整个应用场景。

这个 use case 是不是有人要用，你这个 use case 是不是能够改变我现在的这种运营模式，能够吸引人进来，这才是最重要的。而吸引人进来一定是这个 utility 本身，并不是铸币费、销毁费，或者你去给他们分给他们一些美债的一些营收，并不是这样。

也就是说，到底还是回到了产品本身，和这个产品本身到底有没有人用。

我的仓位：Circle

好吧，这是我的一些看法。然后至于仓位这一块，我是有 Circle 的仓位的，然后现在今天跌完之后降到了 5% 了。然后接下去我并不会进行加仓，除非它真的跌到了非常低的这个位置我可能才会考虑进行加仓。

另外一点是我今天其实我比较意外的是，Circle 这家公司它总共的 market cap 才 170 亿——170 亿美金，就没想到那么小的一家公司今天和稳定币相关的一些消息就能够引起那么大的这个共鸣和市场上的这个讨论。

那我个人对于 Circle 的这个看法，我以前其实在上次发完财报之后我有聊过：把它当作是一家初创公司。它现在其实就是一家初创公司，很多的一些产品现在都没有落地的，连产品的雏形本身都没有。那么虽然它有深度合作的对象 Coinbase，然后 Black Rock 背后也有应用场景，所以它但是它现在还是在一个 build、还是在一个建设的过程之中，未来能够走到哪其实完全是取决于管理层的一个执行力。而你现在无非就是花了 20 个 billion 不到一些去买未来——在 AI agent、未来在电子支付、未来在交付这一块，可能一个 infrastructure、可能一家基建公司。

四、麦当劳：低估值下的持有逻辑

最后两家公司。第一家公司麦当劳，是在上期视频下面有人问我，他说他一直在卖麦当劳的 put，然后麦当劳是一直在下跌，所以问我接下去怎么办。

我的分析方法论

首先麦当劳本身，一般有这类问题我没办法非常详实的去回答，除非这家公司比如说我有比较深度的了解。麦当劳这家公司本身它在基本面不会有太多的一些变化，它就是非常稳的一家公司。对于这类的公司来说，首先我先会检验什么？

我先去看了一下，因为既然问我这个问题，我先去看一下估值在什么位置。

看了一下现在麦当劳估值从过去 8 年的角度去看已经在一个相当低的位置。上一次在那么低的位置是在 2024 年的年中，再上一次的话就要追溯到 2022 年当时的暴跌。所以现在麦当劳其实估值已经在一个相当低的位置。

从这个角度去看，我认为处在这个位置的话，麦当劳就应该 hold、就应该继续持有。估值在一个低位可以继续持有的一个前提一定是基本面没有改变。

另外一点我去做的一个印证是看一下市场现在对于未来 EPS 增速的预期：6%、9% 和 8%。所以市场对于——这就是平时麦当劳的一个正常的 EPS 增速，在这样的一个增速的情况下，我认为麦当劳的估值就是偏低了。

五、开仓标的：Intuitive Surgical (ISRG)

最后一家公司的分享，也是我在周二开仓的一家公司 ISRG，Intuitive Surgical。

增速下调与估值调整

这家公司在 24、25 年是非常的好，走得非常的好，估值一直非常非常的高。然后直到最近过去一年多的时间里面，估值终于出现了下调。

先来看一下这家公司未来三年的一些增速：16%、14% 和 15%，就差不多平均每一年 15% 左右的增速。其实和过去三年做对比的话，确实出现了下降。过去三年平均差不多在 23%、24% 左右。从 23%、24% 到 15%，所以估值出现了下降，是一个合理的结果。

PEG 与 Forward P/E 的两难

当然了，即使在这样的情况下，根据现在静置的市盈率 42 倍、15% 的 EPS 增速，PEG Ratio 可能还是接近于 3 倍。估值的话就是两个角度了：

第一个角度，PEG Ratio 还是在 3 倍。从 PEG Ratio 这个角度去看还是算高的。所以这一点我可能没有那么大的把握说他一定可以，比如说 P/E 能够稳定在这样的一个位置。

第二个角度，我这边看的是 Forward P/E。你可以看到在 25 年年初的时候，最高一度要趋近将近 80 倍了，非常高的一个 Forward P/E。静止市盈率的话一定是 100 多倍了。Forward P/E 在 25 年的年初将近要到 80 倍，在过去一年半的时间里面 P/E 一直在下降。上一个季度财报发布完之后出现了一波反弹，之后然后继续出现下降。

对吗？然后现在的一个位置——这是截止到昨天的时候 36.8 倍。什么样的一个概念？反正 25 年是没有跌到过、24 年没跌到过，22 年的时候（22 年是整个成长股熊市的时候）ISRG 当时的市盈率、向前看的市盈率跌到了 36.8 倍。再上一次的话就是 2020 年疫情的时候。

护城河与估值溢价

所以这就是我前面说的，我可能没有那么大的把握，因为我知道 P/E 已经在一个低位、已经在一个相当低的位置，但是 PEG Ratio 还是在一个相对比较高的位置。

可能存在的一种情况就是：因为他这家公司本身基本面的特殊性，他起码在行业里面的护城河还是比较深的。一般来说行业里面护城河比较深的，市场可能会愿意给出他一个相对比较高的 PEG Ratio。有很多个例子，比如说吉列、比如说沃尔玛、比如说 Costco，这类的公司在行业里面一般是比较领先的公司，市场愿意给出这一个估值上的一个溢价。

所以绝对的估值这个角度去看 PEG Ratio 是在高的，你从 P/E 这个角度来看也是在相对比较高——就你和其他公司去做对比的话。但是在行业里面不见得是非常高。所以这一点是需要市场可以去找到共识——这一点我自己这个判断不重要，市场能不能找到这个共识？

就像类似于特斯拉一样，那么为什么特斯拉的 P/E 能够在 150 倍、200 倍？市场愿意——就是愿意给特斯拉 150 倍、200 倍。那你去期待你说我 10 倍能够买到特斯拉这个股票，基本上是完全不现实的这种期待。

结尾：合作活动

最后今天的活动还是 Thinking Alpha 他们 Alpha Picks，但是我对数据进行了更新。

YTD 到现在为止标普 500 上涨了 8.65%，他们量化的选股策略上涨了 42%。这是他们过去所挑选的 7 个标的数据，这边我也做出了更新。基本上在 7 家公司里面，5 家公司是跑得比市场要好，然后两家公司相对跑得差一些。跑得比较好的公司跑赢了标普 500 40% 以上。

他们这一次的活动是全场七五折： - Alpha Picks 量化选股策略：原本 499 美金一年，现在七五折之后是 375 美金一年 - Premium 订阅（可以看到他们上面整个平台上面所有的一些研报和文章）：现在也是 75 折，并且还有 7 天的免费使用，非常的划算 - 另外还有个 Combo，大家对 Combo 感兴趣的话也可以在置顶的留言里面找到订阅的这个链接。

好吗？那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的"理发指标"：情绪套利者的自我暴露
小桃开场的"理发梗"看似玩笑，其实暴露了一个耐人寻味的**行为金融学悖论**——他不是因为理发才准确，而是**只在情绪低点才想去理发**。这是一个典型的"内生性偏差"陷阱：当分析师的行动本身成为情绪指标时，指标与市场之间的因果关系就被彻底扭曲了。
更深的一层意外观察是：**这其实是所有"感觉型交易者"的隐秘操作系统**。华尔街那些说"我就是有感觉"的老交易员，他们的"感觉"往往就是这种未被明确编码的身体信号——理发、失眠、突然想吃甜食。他们本质上是在用生理系统做一种**低维压缩的市场情绪采样**。这解释了为什么AI量化模型再强，也难以完全替代资深交易员的直觉——不是直觉神秘，而是身体本身就是一台被市场噪音训练了几十年的传感器。
## 二、Open USD 的真正威胁：不是"开源"，而是"稳定币的安卓化"
小桃把 Open USD 的核心分析停留在了"美债利息分配"和"铸币费"这两个层面，但我认为他**低估了这条新闻背后的结构性重排**。
真正值得警惕的类比不是"USDC vs USDT"，而是**iOS vs Androi

item.title: [Latent Space] 🔬 "The Most Innovative Diffusion Research Is Happening in Drug Discovery, Not Image Generation"
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

当"过时的工具"成为前沿的实验场

最让人意外的观察藏在 Evan 一句轻描淡写的话里：当下最前沿的 diffusion 研究，不在图像生成领域，而在 3D 蛋白质结构预测里。这是一个反直觉到几乎需要重读三遍的论断——主流叙事一直是"Sora、Midjourney 在推动 diffusion 的极限"，而药物发现被默认是"应用方"，是技术的下游消费者。

但仔细想想其中的系统性逻辑就豁然开朗：图像 diffusion 已经在快速被 autoregressive 模型反噬（参考新一代视频模型），因为图像的容错率高、约束弱、人类感知粗糙；而蛋白质-小分子结合需要 sub-anstrom（亚埃，0.1 纳米级）的精度，几乎没有任何冗余度可以让你"差不多就行"。正是约束最严酷的领域，反而成了某种生成范式真正能被压榨到极限的地方——就像高压锅决定了某些化学反应只能在那里发生。这是一种"领域选择技术、而非技术选择领域"的反向适配。

隐藏的对偶：LLM 的 inference-time scaling 与晶体结构生成

Sergey 揭示了一个极其优美的跨域同构：LLM 的 thinking tokens 在"语言空间"里反复迭代，而 Pearl 在"晶体结构表示"里反复迭代——思维（reasoning）的本质是某种潜在空间里的 refinement loop，而和媒介无关。

这里有个更微妙的洞见：LLM 的 thinking 是"自由发挥+RL 校准"，而结构预测的 thinking 是"diffusion 步骤+物理引导（physics-based guidance）"。物理学在这里扮演了 RLHF 在语言模型里的角色——物理定律就是分子世界的"人类偏好"。这暗示了一个更普遍的范式：未来所有"思考型模型"都需要某种 verifier，而 verifier 的质量取决于该领域是否存在一个客观的"准则源"（语言里是人类偏好，分子里是物理，数学里是证明，代码里是编译/测试）。没有 verifier 的领域，inference-time scaling 是空转。

反常识：CRO 商业模式如何意外塑造了 AI 公司的护城河

这一段几乎被一笔带过，但极具启发：因为西方制药业过度依赖 CRO（合同研究组织），导致其内部湿实验能力退化，Genesis 选择与中国的 Insights 合作——Insights 的核心竞争力恰恰是反潮流的纵向整合。

这里出现了一个跨领域的镜像：和 OpenAI、Anthropic 的故事完全一样——最强的 AI 模型公司，必须重建上游基础设施（GPU 集群、数据生成能力、评估流水线），不能再依赖外部商品化供给。Insights 之于 Genesis = TSMC 之于 Apple Silicon = 数据中心之于前沿模型——当一个产业链的关键节点被商品化太久，回归垂直整合就成了下一轮的 alpha。

更进一步的洞察：RL 闭环不仅是算法问题，更是组织架构问题。Genesis 在 RL 上能跑得比同行快，本质上是因为它找到了一个不抗拒"被一个模型实时塑造的湿实验流"的合作伙伴。许多药企不愿意做这种闭环，不是技术原因，而是组织文化原因——它要求科学家放弃"科学家直觉是最高仲裁者"的身份。这是组织拓扑决定了算法上限的典型案例。

一埃门槛的真正含义：不是工程指标，而是认知诚信

最后一个隐秘洞见：Evan 反复强调 "1Å vs 2Å" 不仅是精度问题，而是幻觉的可识别性问题。模糊的图像你看得出它模糊，但翻转 180° 的芳香环看起来完全合理——这正是 LLM 幻觉最危险的形态：可读、自洽、错误。

这把一个看似工程化的精度指标，提升到了认识论层面：对一个生成模型而言，"知道自己错"比"少错一点"更有价值。这种洞见也可以反向辐射到 LLM 领域——也许评测 LLM 时，比起 benchmark 分数，我们更应该追求"幻觉是否在结构上可被检测"。

📝 转录文本

引言：意外的预言

I remember very clearly in like 2017 2018 talking about GANs and how generative adversarial networks and how uh they're clearly the future of image generation. Obviously, they didn't work very well for proteins or protein ligand systems, and we sort of had to wait for the right primitive to get created — and that turned out to be diffusion, which turned out to be a much more useful primitive for the space.

What's kind of cool is right now, for people that are interested in really core fundamental AI research, actually some of the most innovative diffusion research is happening in our field, is happening in 3D structure prediction right now. No one would have predicted that then, but now that's a pillar of diffusion, I'd say.

嘉宾介绍

Hi there. Welcome to the Latent Space AI for Science podcast. I'm RJ Hanicki, CTO of Mirmix. I'm joined by my co-host Brandon Anderson, and we're privileged to have Evan Fineberg, founder and CEO of Genesis Molecular AI, and Sergey Udov, who led Llama 2 and Llama 3 pre-training before he joined Genesis as CTO.

Sergey: Hi, I'm Sergey. I studied physics at school — this is a long time ago. After graduation, I happened to work in software engineering. Thought I would never need physics again. When ML came up and became a thing, turns out that a lot of things you're doing in ML were actually very similar to what you would do in physics. So I jumped on the ML bandwagon and I did a lot of AI research being part of the FAIR team in Facebook for quite a while. I later on led the Llama team — Llama 2 and Llama 3 models — and then recently I decided to pivot my career again and recover my roots in physics a little bit, and I joined Genesis as CTO.

Evan: Hey, I'm Evan. I'm the founder and CEO of Genesis Molecular AI, and like Sergey, also a physics major. I was a bit different from everyone in my family growing up. Almost everyone was a medical professional of some kind, and my sister became an accomplished TV writer, playwright, novelist. My love was physics and computer science, but my mental model of an adult was: well, you should help people and help patients ideally. So I was always searching for the right way to do that.

After arriving at Stanford doing my PhD in VJ Pond's lab, we were excited in the mid-2010s of everything amazing going on in machine learning — to use a dated term — for images and for language. Around the same time that Sergey was at FAIR working on a lot of big graphs, I was at Stanford working on many small graphs. Molecules are really networks of atoms and bonds and spatial interactions. Being at the right place at the right time to bring to bear our backgrounds in physics to improve AI algorithms for looking at molecules, we published a few papers in the area of graph machine learning.

Sort of like Sergey, I thought, well, I won't need this physics again because there's machine learning. But turns out the massive amounts of simulations on GPUs of proteins that we ran also came in quite handy as Genesis evolved, and we've been really excited the past few years to figure out how to build foundation models for a totally new domain and make them useful for patients.

十年回望：蛋白-小分子相互作用为何如此难解

Brandon: That's a really nice lead into my first question. We've both been in this domain of machine learning for molecules and bio for roughly 10 years. It's an entire generation of tech-bio that has come and gone since then. While a lot of machine learning for molecules has been quite effective, one domain where it's been not effective — has historically really resisted ML modeling — has been the world of protein–small molecule interactions. With some of the recent advances that Genesis has put out, it seems like you might have actually started to make real improvement on this. Can you talk about what Genesis has done, the developments that led to improvement, and why you think this is actually a real improvement?

Evan: I totally agree, Brandon. The amazing thing is that when we were founding the company initially as a spin-out of the research we were doing in AI at Stanford, we were afraid that we could be too late. You remember that you and I first met around that time, right? Seven plus years ago — almost a different generation in the area. At the time, when we were a little seed company, there were already incumbents in the space who had raised orders of magnitude more money than us. There were already some really exciting academic papers, and we had to constantly answer the question: "Is there room for another company doing AI in drug discovery?" — which is a crazy thing to say looking with a backward-facing lens. Hindsight's 20/20.

The claim that we made then is the same that we're making now: there are 20,000 protein-coding genes, and each of them can cause a disease. In the same way that in other domains of AI it's not like they were zero-to-one and then suddenly that problem was solved, the reality has been: there's been no single iPhone moment. Even the phone required iterative development over time to become what it is today. People love to talk retrospectively about the ChatGPT moment, but the first ChatGPT models that became widely used were vastly less useful than they are now.

We should look at drug discovery from an AI context in the same way: as we go, we should expect to solve more and more problems, there will be large epochs, there will be iterative improvements, but they'll compound over time. That's why I think that in the same way that 10 years ago was very early but clearly value was starting to be created for drug development then from an AI perspective, fast forward 10 years and our technologies are vastly more useful. In the next 10 years, we'll see another exponential improvement as well. Just like your car in terms of autonomy — we're recording this in San Francisco, you look outside and see Waymos. We'd be blown away a few years ago by those technologies.

锁与钥匙：药物发现的根本任务

Brandon: Maybe we can go back in time a little bit and explain the state of protein–small molecule drug discovery about a decade ago. What could we actually expect to do with machine learning models, and what were some of the failure modes that people didn't really see coming?

Evan: Breaking the fourth wall a little bit — I came in not really knowing any of these questions, but Brandon really knows how to speak my love language. Though my background is much more in the physics and CS side, I've been very deeply passionate about how we can make medicines with AI for over a decade. I've seen so many tectonic shifts.

To give you one concrete example and a little background for those who don't have as much biological background: drug discovery is akin to finding a key for a lock, where the lock is usually a protein, in some cases a nucleic acid. The drug is the key — usually some small molecule or a peptide or an antibody or some other modality that wants to bind to that lock, that protein, the receptor, and change something about it. If it's an enzyme, you usually want to stop it from functioning. Sometimes you want to activate that endogenous protein, but the idea is to introduce an external (exogenous) molecule to change something about that biological pathway.

A necessary but not sufficient part of that process is finding a molecule that binds well to that receptor. We also need to make sure it does not bind to certain anti-targets — proteins you want to avoid — that often mediate toxicity. You need ADMET (absorption, distribution, metabolism, elimination, toxicity), which is typically like 30-ish properties, to make sure your molecule is safe in addition to being effective, and gets to the right tissue.

3D 结构假说：一个等了十年的赌注

One concrete example to answer your question about protein–ligand interactions: we had a hypothesis for a long time that if we can predict the 3D structure — the 3D coordinates, imagine for the CS people like a point cloud — the 3D positions of your drug and the 3D positions of the protein, and if you could model that with high accuracy, that will lead naturally to measuring binding affinity or predicting potency more accurately. That was a hypothesis that fundamentally could not be tested because models for predicting those 3D poses were so bad.

Brandon: Or if you can predict this, it requires so much computational resources that you might as well just solve the problem —

Evan: Right — you might as well solve the 3D crystal structure or cryo-EM structure, which can cost tens of thousands of dollars, can be months or years. Entire PhD theses, postdocs can sometimes work literally 24/7 trying to solve one 3D structure. The hypothesis was that if we make this orders of magnitude faster — sometimes more accurate — with artificial intelligence, or before that with molecular dynamics, you would thereby not only accelerate drug discovery but enable the discovery of medicines for targets that were previously thought to be undruggable.

That was just a hypothesis, and it took until the last few years to show that if we systematically improve the accuracy of those predictions, if we can improve the accuracy of a protein–ligand 3D complex, we can make potency prediction more accurate as well. That's something I am personally extremely gratified to feel like we've been showing — because that was just a hypothesis when we started a company a few years ago, and now it's become a reality thanks to the convergence of a lot of different ideas that were able to enable Pearl and other foundation models like it.

Pearl 模型：扩展那看似无法扩展的

Brandon: Can you talk about Pearl a bit? One of the interesting things about Pearl in my mind is it was an attempt to scale what seems unscalable to me. RNA, small molecules — normally if you scale them, models just come out as pure pattern matchers. These things love to tell you what you already know, and sometimes they're maybe not so great at telling you something you don't know. It seems like there's hints that Pearl has actually moved beyond this. What were the key insights?

Evan: What you describe shouldn't surprise any pure ML practitioners in the audience, because machine learning is initially constructed for pattern recognition. It's the same with when you fire up Claude or ChatGPT — it's going to do best when it's closest to the train data, and it's going to pattern-match off it. That has been the big sense of urgency in AI meeting the physical world: how to extrapolate, how to make generalizable models. We've been really hard at work on that. Maybe Sergey wants to give some more color.

什么是 Pearl

Sergey: Maybe to step back a little bit and discuss what Pearl is. It's a structure prediction model, which basically means it takes as an input a protein sequence and a ligand representation that you try to attach to this protein, and it predicts how this protein and ligand are going to look together as a structure in 3D space.

Brandon: A term for this people sometimes have used is co-folding. AlphaFold 3 and then Boltz, OpenFold 3 and so on have also implemented some of these ideas in their own way.

Sergey: Yes, there are several models — some open source, some closed source — that are pursuing similar directions. Our fundamental difference is that we're focusing on the small molecular space. A lot of models are doing protein–protein interactions, and it works well for small molecular space. On the first glance, it may sound like it's an easier task — well, it's a small molecule, right? Why would it be challenging? But the reality is the search space is so vast for small molecules. There are 10^60 drug-like small molecules in the universe. Good luck searching that space. There are also all variations — you can rotate it, you can have different conformations of those molecules.

Brandon: Maybe the intuition there is: when you type in a Google search, if you just do a few words you're going to get a huge list of matches. But if you have a very specific query, you're going to have a few matches and they're more likely to match. Similarly with a small molecule, if you have a very small query — your molecule — then it's likely you're going to have to search a huge space of possible matches. Whereas if you have something very complex, then it's easy to rule out a match. Is that a good way to think about it?

Sergey: That's one way to think about it, definitely. For me, it's just the computational complexity of figuring out which small molecule will attach to this protein. It's such a vast problem to solve that it's impossible to do without good models.

Evan: It's like finding a needle in a haystack.

Sergey: Where everything except your needle is very, very dangerous, or just doesn't bind.

Evan: Or they are needles, right? We're finding hay in a needle stack might be a more apt analogy.

训练数据的稀缺与突破

Sergey: Now we can think about how we train those models. A lot of training data that people use is the so-called PDB, which is a public database of all of the historical crystal structures. It's not that big — like 200,000 crystal structures. And it's very hard to expand. It takes a lot of time and energy and money to create new crystal structures. Although there are some projects that are pursuing this, it's still expanding at a glacial pace.

What we figured out is possible to do — and it's very relevant to our small molecular space — is that in small molecular space you can actually model your small molecules with physics. You can model their behavior, and that allows you to create more data. You can train a model on something which is not necessarily possible in protein-to-protein —

Brandon: Because they're too complex for a protein.

Sergey: Yeah, those are very big molecules. So it's very hard to model them with physics. Not impossible — just computationally very, very hard.

Brandon: Right. You do a really good job of modeling these small molecules using MD or something like that, and you can put together those structures in your training set at a much lower cost.

三阶段 scaling 路线图：与 LLM 的镜像

Sergey: Maybe to step back a little bit about our roadmap. It's not fundamentally different from the roadmap for LLM scaling. Remember in LLMs we have all the stages: you have pre-training scaling, then post-training scaling where you do either fine-tuning or RL (now everybody's doing RL), and then you do inference-time scaling. All of those three concepts connect, and that's what led us to state-of-the-art LLMs these days.

It's not fundamentally different on our side. We also have pre-training scaling where we create a lot of synthetic data to train better models. Then the second thing we started doing is actually the third step in LLM scaling — we started doing inference-time scaling. In LLMs when we talk about inference-time scaling we're talking about thinking tokens, where the LLM, instead of giving you the answer right away, kind of goes and thinks for a while and then comes up with a response. We're doing a very similar thing with our models, where a model is forced to think — except it's not thinking in language tokens, it's thinking in terms of crystal structures. Not fully materialized crystal structures, but some sort of crystal structure representation in memory. The model kind of goes back and forth with those. We use physics-based guidance during this process to steer the model output in the right direction. What we found is that it improves model performance by a lot.

扩散头与物理引导：可解释性的边界

RJ: It's easy to understand how thinking tokens work because it's just basically parts of the transcript that you don't see. But for other models, do you have some sort of loop — is that similar to a loop transformer or something? You mentioned there's some physics-based verification as part of that in the loop. Is there more to it than that?

Sergey: One fundamental block of our models is a diffusion-based head. So it's the same diffusion models that people are using for image and video generation. Well, we use them for crystal structure generation. The diffusion head is by nature iterative — it's multiple steps. You're refining your predicted structure. As you're doing this process, you can steer the model in the right direction.

RJ: So there's like a steering simulation in the loop. You go through a pass of diffusion, you look at what comes out, you run some verifier and use that to say "I like this" or "I don't like this," or give a vector to go towards. Is that the idea?

Sergey: Yeah.

RJ: So your diffusion head is basically learning something like a force field, and you're kind of balancing out between a diffusion-based force field and a physics-based force field? Is that a way of thinking about it?

Sergey: I struggle to understand what those models are really learning underneath. The whole idea of explainability is a big research challenge. You can identify what a neuron in a transformer is potentially thinking about, but it's a really difficult problem. We have some internal representation, but at the output it comes out as a crystal structure. It's really hard to tell what exactly is happening inside.

RJ: Is it possible to do something to the effect of the standard mechanistic interpretability things — sparse autoencoders and the more complicated things — in this context?

Sergey: I think it would be interesting as a research direction. That's not something we are pursuing. I guess potentially we could explore this.

物理先验作为表征学习

Evan: Sergey is a lot more credible than I am talking about the LLM translatability to our space. Sergey is being humble, but Sergey led the Llama 2 research team at Meta. He's trained one of the most widely used language models on the planet.

What I can add on the physical and interpretability side: I like to look at AI models in terms of the inputs, the model themselves, and the output. We find it really improves outcomes as well as interpretability when you can include as many physical priors as possible — without of course biasing the model to what we puny humans believe.

I've always had the view that AI fundamentally is representation learning. To me, that's actually not that different from what's happened in the language and vision domains, where we're enforcing a real human prior on images when we started using convolutional neural nets — saying "pictures are grids of pixels, there's something inherent about that." That's a human construct, and we constructed ConvNets on top of that. And language as sequences of tokens in one dimension — we first built RNNs, then built transformers. That's still baking in a pretty serious prior on how to look at that data.

We don't view it very differently here. From the input perspective, the disadvantage we have in our field over the more traditional domains of AI is that we don't have the internet to work with. We can't just download Reddit posts and buy some subscriptions to the Wall Street Journal and train a model and voilà — the pre-trained model works fairly well. The closest equivalent that Sergey mentioned is the RCSB Protein Data Bank, which has more like a couple hundred thousand structures. Although there's a lot more — a lot more tokens per structure, so there's a lot more latent information.

So we have to be clever on the input side, which is generating more pre-training data (as RJ was mentioning), using physics as much as possible in terms of the model architecture itself — the idea is to not have the model have to relearn as much physics as possible, so it's less likely to overfit. And then at the output stage, enforcing physicality.

聚焦小分子：明确的差异化

That also goes to one of our main focuses as a company, which is we've been focused from the beginning on small and medium-sized molecule discovery. Small molecules tend to be drugs that you can take as a pill — orally bioavailable. It's how most people think of medicine. And medium-sized molecules: think about macrocycles, peptides, modalities that break the traditional rule of five but are still growing modalities in medicine. That said, small molecules are still 65% of FDA-approved drugs, so we're talking about the biggest part of the pie.

We're building this with a judicious focus from day one on: what do drug hunters, what do medicinal chemists, what do CAD scientists, what do they need to discover drugs faster and better than they could before? We wanted to build that human interpretability and usability from the beginning, and also physical usability. We want outputs from these models to be used by physical methods that require 3D coordinates to actually make sense. We want the outputs of our model to be useful as inputs to a force field.

There was this — a few papers came out, one was in Cell last year — which showed that for all the claims about AlphaFold solving drug discovery, people tried to take AlphaFold-produced protein structures, use them for traditional docking, and found no value in it. Those pockets just weren't high resolution enough. They couldn't be used by physical screening methods. So we wanted to build our systems so they'd be useful by humans, useful by all the many adjacent and powerful tools from the physics and computational chemistry communities from day one — to have that interoperability.

药物发现的全流程，与 AI 的最高杠杆点

RJ: This might be a good time to step back. When you develop a drug, there are many steps — from discovery and toxicity and availability, the clinical stuff, and finally approval. Where do your models sit in that, or which parts of that process do they say? What are the steps, just briefly? I've read that there's 12 steps in drug discovery.

Evan: Acceptance is the last one —

Sergey: Except that your phase three trial failed.

Evan: Exactly. So if we go from the very beginning to the very end: first, we need to identify what target is causing the disease, what signaling cascade, what's going wrong, what's causing the phenotype of the patient. That's not a trivial exercise, but there are many — certainly not all but many — diseases where the cause is actually very clear. The most clear are monogenetic diseases. Even in others, from various panels it's clear that this overexpressed or overactive or deleted gene or protein is causing the disease. So we know many but not all of the universe of causes of diseases.

Then once a target is identified, we have the process of finding a drug for that target that's ideally selective, very potent, gets to the right tissue. Some diseases are multi-system; some are very specific to a certain organ or tissue. You want to make sure that drug can get to that tissue. Then there is the process of GLP tox and IND enablement. IND — investigational new drug.

RJ: GLP in this case is not —

Evan: No, no — Good Lab Practices. Thank you. We're not talking about the metabolic space right now.

There are clinical trials, usually ascending size: phase 1 starts typically more with safety, then phase 2 and phase 3, and as you point out, ideally approval at whatever regulatory body — the FDA or EMA, or what have you.

为什么药物发现是最高杠杆点

Our contention is that the highest-leveraged application of artificial intelligence is the drug discovery and drug design process. Even though they're all valuable — and I cheer on all the peers, many of whom I know, who are working at all the different parts of the stack — the first thing to make clear is: just like a vision model is going to be very different than a coding model, they might share some similarities under the hood, but it requires real focus to do any one area correctly. There's a similar if not greater difference from the sort of models you need for target identification (i.e. biology), to drug discovery, to preparing for regulatory filings, to helping segment patients for clinical trials. These are all very important, complementary, but ultimately distinct problems.

I think about all of the times where a patient goes to a doctor, gets told that they have a very clear diagnosis, and in addition the physician will tell them, "There's been breakthroughs where we understand why this disease happens. We've sequenced your genome, we've sequenced your tumor, we know what's causing your condition — but we do not have a selective therapy. There is no precision medicine for your condition." At least they know what's wrong with them, but they don't really know how to treat it.

Our aim is to have as many moments as possible where patients are told they have a very specific condition and there's a specific treatment for them. That's going to require bending the cost curve of discovering and developing new medicine, but also importantly solving those cases where there's certain "undruggable" targets — proteins that we need to figure out how to drug, where those targets have proven resistant to traditional methods or even intractable in some cases.

没有中间环节，发现等于零

RJ: Just to clarify my understanding: there's the discovery part and there's the clinical part. There can be effort on "what's the target that is impacting this disease the most?" — but if you don't have the thing in the middle that's "okay, here's how we actually build this drug," so that I can hit my target — if it's not selective enough so it kills cells or does things to cells that I don't want it to do — then the answer doesn't matter. I can have the answer "this drug should go after this target," but if I don't know how to do that effectively, potently, then the answer doesn't matter. Is that what you're saying?

Evan: Yeah, RJ — people love to debate about success rates in clinical trials, but the reality is: if one focuses on those drug candidates that are aimed at proteins that have a close genetic linkage to a disease, where the biology is well understood, where the animal models translate well to the disease, where the molecule is predicted to have good pharmacokinetics and the levels in the blood/serum in patients are going to be high enough — those molecules have fairly high FDA approval rates. The success rate from phase 1 to end of phase 3 is substantially higher than average.

People love to cite the 10% success rate, but that's really a lowball. We often know what genes, what proteins, what targets are causing the disease — they're just really hard to drug, or the molecules we put into patients are inferior to the target product profile that they deserve. In the cases where if you just look at the preclinical data, you focus on good targets with good biology that are predicted to be distributed well and have good safety profiles, those molecules are very likely to get approved and create tremendous value for patients. Our view is that this is the highest-leverage application of AI in healthcare medicine more broadly.

容易的靶点都被摘完了吗？

RJ: A business question: what is the space of things where the target's biology is understood, the structure is understood, and the only thing you really need to do is find the right lock? That seems like you probably would have already picked over that class of targets in some sense.

Evan: There are two orthogonal concepts. Known biology is orthogonal to the ease with which one can drug that target. Sometimes unfortunately it seems they anti-correlate, in that often it seems that the most appealing targets from a validation perspective seem to be really hard to drug.

RJ: But do they anti-correlate, or is it just that we have picked all the low-hanging fruit which do solve both those?

Evan: I think it is likely the case that many of the "easier" targets, for a variety of reasons, have been drugged. Not all — but even in those cases, people love to have this false dichotomy of easy versus hard targets, and "well this one's drugged, versus it's picked over therefore it's not."

We at Genesis do most of our work through large pharma. Most of what we do is providing AI services basically to major pharma companies like Gilead. We recently announced our expansion of our collaboration with Incyte, which we're excited about. I can't give details of specific targets they're working on, but there's a real range from first-in-class chemical matter — meaning we believe this target causes a disease, there's no known molecules that bind to that target, we need to find the first binders ever, the true zero-to-one cases — to a wide variety of targets we work on that are more "1 to 10" cases. Sometimes there's an approved agent, or there's molecules that are only pre-clinical but they're not optimal. If you can improve upon those preclinical molecules and get them to development candidates ready to get into patients, or if you can improve upon the existing clinical or approved agents, you can create a lot of value for patients.

I'll give a public example. We're not working on this target at all, but you just look at the progression of ALK inhibitors. You might have said, well, when the first ALK inhibitor came out, why do we need another? We've drugged ALK, we're done. But it is a qualitative, not just a quantitative difference, when you compare patient survival curves from first-generation ALK inhibitors to later-generation ALK inhibitors — how much longer those patients live, how many more get benefit. There's not only value in first-in-class, there's enormous value for patients in best-in-class too.

AlphaFold 不等于药物发现已被解决

RJ: So far we've talked about Genesis modeling in terms of one specific problem of protein–ligand binding. How does your overall world work in terms of developing this? I do know you have other things you've worked on. I keep joking that when the Nobel Prize was given for AlphaFold 3, a lot of people thought drug discovery was solved.

Sergey: And it's very far from the truth. Very far.

RJ: You can maybe predict crystal structure —

Sergey: At low resolution —

RJ: At low resolution. Well, we're doing better, I think. Assume you can predict crystal structure. Does it mean drug discovery is solved? No, obviously not.

Brandon: The broader community — the protein folding community and drug discovery community — immediately said there are other things you care about, like dynamics. A single static structure is not enough to understand what's going on, interactions. Many people thought those were very useful — they've really accelerated science — but it was very clearly a starting point, not an end condition.

Sergey: Right, but if you look outside of people working in the field — like the general popular community — it was a pretty wide belief that the problem is solved. Besides that —

Brandon: I just have to clarify that, otherwise the entire ML structure biology community will jump on us and say "No, it's not a solved problem."

Sergey: Absolutely not a solved problem.

药物的多目标优化

Sergey: In reality, you need to predict so many other properties — like ADMET properties that I haven't mentioned. You're designing a key for a lock — that small molecule that sticks to your protein in the body. But you don't want this small molecule to stick to everything in your body, cuz that's probably going to cause issues. You also want to make sure that small molecule is soluble so that you can ingest it as a pill. Those are important properties too. You want to make sure it doesn't have any other side effects. Predicting all of those properties is also just as important as — or maybe even more important than — predicting the crystal structure itself. As a company, we're very proud — we're not just building models for predicting crystal structure, we're building models for predicting all of those properties and basically enable drug hunters to become 100x more effective in their daily job.

与 Incyte 的合作：从临床端到工具端

Brandon: I'm curious about the pipeline that you have. You mentioned you have different pharma partners — GSK or whomever you're working with. Do they have targets that were drugs designed by you that are in clinical trials, approved? Where do we stand?

Evan: We're limited in what we can say — pharma companies are famously secretive, which makes sense. What I can say which was recently publicly disclosed: we just expanded our partnership with Incyte. That started approximately a little over a year ago. The initial work together spanned the two bookends of the drug discovery process.

One of the programs we worked on together was a case where a very challenging target where chemical matter existed, but there's a key binary event which is getting to a DC (development candidate). That is the molecule — or ideally a set of molecules — all of which are possible to be the agent for a phase 1 clinical trial. We had to do some work together where we take our foundational base models, fine-tune them on Incyte data, and use them in close collaboration to get to a DC faster. We're getting substantially closer in that case, which is one of the reasons we're excited to expand our work together.

On the flip side, one of the other areas we worked together was on a protein with also very nice linkage to a very severe disease, where there was no known chemical matter — no patents, no papers, no co-crystal structure of a ligand binding to that protein. We had to find the first-ever known chemical matter to bind to it, and then we progressed those "hits" — the first molecules that bind your protein — into inhibitors that are active in biochemical assays (more enzyme-based) and cellular assays (in actual living cell models). So it was really based on a concrete set of accomplishments together that we want to expand the collaboration.

The objective of Genesis is to create medicines that patients wish they had. The way we'll be able to do that is by working with as many pharma and biotech partners as possible for whom their comparative advantage is discovery, clinical development, commercialization, and our comparative advantage is in AI. We can put those two expertises together in a synergistic, not just additive, way and make medicines together that otherwise would not be possible.

一埃的门槛：精度即诚实

Brandon: One thing you guys talk about on your website is this one-angstrom threshold at which a protein structure prediction and the binding between that and a small molecule becomes useful. Can you talk a little bit about — okay, why is that the case? How did you do it when others have failed?

Sergey: When we're talking about molecular interaction, the scale of two angstroms that people typically measured is just too big. You can think about, like in an image generation model, you would generate a picture and it's fuzzy —

RJ: Yeah.

Sergey: But you can't discern the details. And the details really matter here. With two-angstrom accuracy, your entire aromatic ring can be flipped and it will still be a valid output. The worst part is that, unlike an image which is blurry — you know it's blurry, right? — you flip around an aromatic ring and it looks just fine.

Evan: Yeah, or even worse, a heterocyclic aromatic ring, and then you're really in trouble.

Sergey: It really matters in this case because individual atoms need to establish connections here. So that level of accuracy what we're pushing for — one angstrom — is really important.

RJ: Maybe it's almost like with a lot of generative image models, they will fill in a detail which just doesn't exist. If you were to try to do forensics and tell your vision transformer to "enhance" an image, and suddenly it just pops up a face — but that's actually not the person's face. You don't know that, and it feels just fine. So if you're trying to use this as a structural hypothesis as a med-chemist, you're kind of screwed. If you're looking at the wrong face as an investigator, it's not going to take —

Sergey: Yeah. And then as Evan said, downstream, you run a bunch of other models like physics-based models, and all of those little issues, they compound. And if you made the wrong prediction — fundamentally wrong — then the downstream predictions are also going to be wrong.

Evan: I just wanted to say, for your question, I really appreciate your mid-2000s TV reference about enhancing forensic images.

"Pose" 这一抽象本身是否可疑

Brandon: We talked about poses. Maybe the contrarian take is that a pose isn't even a well-defined concept, and the best way of thinking about these things is this probability distribution over things. There's a small molecule which probably lives in a binding pocket something like this. Is this pose an abstraction that humans use and not really in some sense ground truth? Some chemists would say "this pose isn't even real" — versus a most probable configuration. How do you interact with a single structure versus an ensemble?

Sergey: Yes, it's an abstraction. But it's a very useful abstraction. It helps us to build up confidence that a particular model output is actually valid — that you did not just straight up hallucinate something. Cuz yes, ultimately what matters is binding affinity or potency, and you can straight up predict that with your model and just skip the entire pose generation step. But then you only have a single number, and that number might as well be completely hallucinated, and you have no means to validate whether that number even makes any sense. So as much as poses are not perfect, they're still a very useful tool for the entire process.

Brandon: Sorry, going into that direction — just because you have a pose, there's entropic and enthalpic contributions, and predicting binding affinity is not just about getting the energy right but is this even likely to make it into the binding pocket and is it likely to live there long term? It's much more than just affinity. Potency prediction is much more than "is this the right pose." Does this have the broader properties it needs — to be a long-lived molecule in this state — and how do you deal with those things too?

Agent 时代的必要前提

Evan: A note for the wider AI audience that probably resonates both with practitioners and users: obviously something that's blossomed in the past six months is agents. And we love agents.

RJ: Who doesn't?

Evan: Necessary but not sufficient conditions. We all remember what agents were like, let's say, mid last year. Let's just say there is positive value and there is negative value. And both can be amplified by agents. Why? Agents are only as useful as the underlying models they're orchestrating. Let's think about coding. If your coding model even makes subtle but real bugs, your agents are just going to amplify those issues, and you're going to end up with not only slop but something that may be anti-useful that gives the user incorrect information. We all remember what that age was like mid last year — that made a lot of people lose patience with claims about LLMs for agent engineering. Something changed — clearly a threshold was met. And even though these models are still not perfect, the utility of LLMs for software engineering are so obvious now.

You can draw a direct analogy from that to what we're talking about here. We're working on an agentic platform for 24/7 drug discovery. You can imagine fleets of hundreds of med-chemists and CAD scientists working nights and weekends all the time for your drug targets. The codename for that gem is Sapphire. The prerequisite for that was: we needed the underlying models for pose, 3D complex prediction, potency, ADMET to all be good enough for an agent using these models 24/7 to create molecules that medicinal chemists would actually want to make and not laugh at.

If your model is sitting at 1.8, 1.9 RMSD — that's slop. Let me be really direct about it. You're hearing a bimodal distribution from people of the utility of a 3D pose and how real it is. The reality is that for a highly potent ligand, almost certainly there is a large portion of that molecule with a very well-defined 3D position, down to even half an angstrom. If you don't believe me, you can open up the electron density diagram in the PDB. It's all available online and you can literally see in some cases aromatic rings with missing density in the middle. So it's not just a construct on a blackboard that your organic chemistry professor showed you. You can literally see a donut, a torus of electron density for an aromatic ring.

However, there's going to be solvent-exposed areas oftentimes that will be less important for binding affinity but maybe important for solubility or other properties. Those sometimes are less defined because in reality they're more dynamic — they're flopping around in solvent. But the critical piece as an upstream indicator that's valuable for predicting the free energy of binding is to get the core of your molecule that specifically interacts with the protein to be correct to sub-angstrom resolution.

Why does this matter intuitively? A hydrogen bond — everyone in the audience has probably heard about a hydrogen bond. It's the most critical form of non-covalent interaction. It's how nucleic acids are held together. It's how most ligands and proteins interact. It's how proteins form secondary structures. Hydrogen bonds have a very specific angle and distance. The distance from the donor to the acceptor heavy atom is 2.7 angstroms to 3.3 angstroms. If I do my math right, that's a 0.6-angstrom gap. Outside of that, it's not a hydrogen bond. If it's less than that, it's a clash. If it's more than that, the interaction is much weaker pretty quickly. An angstrom, for those who don't know, is one-tenth of a nanometer.

Drug discovery really is a science of resolution. If your accuracy is not sufficient, it will therefore not be useful for the downstream things that you care about — which is both potency prediction but also prospective design: what molecule do I make next? The history of innovation in startups is that the ones that do best are ones that focus on one well-defined but very important problem. We think our ability to get higher-resolution predictions stems from our judicious focus on small/medium-sized molecule design rather than boiling the ocean.

怎么做到 1Å？一个无聊到刺眼的答案

Brandon: So that's the what and the why. What about the how — how did you get to one angstrom and sub-one-angstrom?

Sergey: I'm going to give you an extremely boring answer. Which I think is actually true for the entire AI field. Three things matter in AI: data, infrastructure, and evals. You can only improve what you measure. Once you are very careful about measuring what matters and you have really talented people on the team, we're going to figure out how to hill-climb that measure.

From the start, we actually focused on less than one-angstrom precision, and that led to a bunch of small decisions in the process that compound. If your team never looks into this metric at all, then you will never train a model that is good at it. And if you're constantly looking into that, then you're going to achieve that.

RJ: So it's the right objective plus good science.

Sergey: And it propagates everywhere through the whole stack. It propagates to how you look at the data — how do we filter out data? Some data is more noisy, so maybe we don't need to see it, or maybe you don't need to see it later in the training. It propagates through your modeling architecture, propagates through your loss.

Brandon: How long have you been working towards this specific goal? This is not something that you broadly hear in the community talk about wanting. People have decided that two angstroms is the canonical benchmark, and I've never heard someone say one before is the cutoff until reading things coming out of Genesis. How direct has this focus been in the evolution of the company?

Sergey: I think one of our most important secrets is that we are working on real drug programs — either with partners or in-house. When you actually work on real drug programs, you see the failure modes — what works and what doesn't work. It's pretty obvious when you see the outputs of real programs, what kind of failure modes are happening. It becomes very obvious that two-angstrom is just not working out.

Brandon: But there are a lot of really smart med-chemists who also think very carefully about benchmarks and who have prosecuted actual drug discovery programs. Why hasn't this become part of the community? Is this literally just that the community has never been able to succeed at winning a benchmark — that it kind of settled at "okay, two-angstrom is something we can aspire to"?

In my experience with pharma, there are plenty of problems where there's kind of these known things amongst the technical experts in a sub-domain, but it doesn't get out of pharma. Some of the information is proprietary and gets passed from company to company but never really released into the public. Is this your estimation of what's going on?

SWE-Bench 类比与评估危机

Evan: Have you ever heard of SWE-Bench? Gemini does pretty well in SWE-Bench. Sometimes Gemini publishes models that win on some of those software benchmarks. Raise your hand if you're using Gemini to write code right now instead of, you know, the obvious other-name competitors.

RJ: Not me.

Evan: No one. Why would you do that? It's obviously worse in practice.

In this case, if you look at the provenance of how that happened: RMSD-less-than-two came originally from — and I've been in the space forever — from docking studies, long before AI models for pose prediction, when physics-based docking studies from academic institutions [were dominant]. Because usually large proprietary software makers didn't want to benchmark their methods against other methods, so academics had to get licenses and try it. Then they'd introduce "RMSD less than two," which is not surprising — they're academics, they're not using these things to make drugs, they're using it to write papers. That got repurposed by the AI community.

But the actual trend is much more in the direction we're saying. The first big innovation there was PoseBusters, put out by a lab at University of Oxford, which pointed out that RMSD itself is insufficient and we need to look at physical validity as well. So Oxford improved that. In the latest release from OpenBind — we just published our benchmarks at Genesis on OpenBind, we'll talk about that in a bit — if you look at their original publication, their default metrics are using RMSD, PoseBusters validity, and LDDT. There's clearly an acknowledgement already made that RMSD less than two is insufficient, and the field is now rapidly evolving to acknowledge that.

Brandon: You think that the academic literature is going to establish some benchmark — maybe it's one angstrom, maybe it's some LDDT or some other metric — that will converge, and then hopefully as a community drive forward more accurate modeling strategies?

Evan: I would say there was an eval crisis in our field that is now in transition right now. Our field was previously a lot more quiet, but if you go to NeurIPS and ICML every year, the workshops get a lot more crowded. Those evals are in transition now that we realize the flaws of what came before.

公司的演化：从 PotentialNet 到生成式时代

Brandon: Pick the right evals and then just do good machine learning. But I picked up on something Evan said earlier — you started out with PotentialNet, this graph-based network, and then you did a lot of computational simulations, and now you're sort of going back into generative modeling. I'm curious about how that evolution worked. Did computational lead back into generative? Did you start collecting data to build a generative computational data as a data augmentation? What's the history?

Evan: I will say that the last line of PyTorch I've written is much further back in history than the most recent line of PyTorch that Sergey has committed. I want to make sure he gives his opinion as well.

I want to make sure I address something you said a little bit of time ago that I think kind of got lost — you asked about how we do other things including ADMET prediction. I can spend all day diving into details about Pearl and its evolution. I just want to give a shout-out to the fact that it is one important pillar but not the only pillar that matters in the trajectory of a drug discovery campaign.

You mentioned that PotentialNet paper, which we're happy to see had become influential since you and I were working in the space when it was very much in the future. Now it is the future. Another paper we published around the same time was on neural networks for ADMET prediction. We published two, actually — ADMET, can you —

Brandon: Oh sorry, thank you — absorption, distribution, metabolism, elimination, and toxicity are the five prongs that comprise ADMET. These are all the properties you talked about before that you have to get right to make a drug successful.

Evan: Correct. There's over 30 or so assays, each of which — if you're a neural net person, like a multitask neural network or multi-head — has to predict over three-dozenish properties. Each of which, if it's in the wrong range, means your molecule is not a drug, it's just a tool. Things like solubility (which Sergey mentioned, important for formulation — as in, can it be made into a pill you can take orally), oral bioavailability, whether or not you're inhibiting certain enzymes called the cytochrome P450s and its different variants, hERG — a critical channel which if you inhibit too much can cause cardiotoxicity. These are an alphabet soup of things that most people here haven't heard of.

Brandon: And a lot of these are extremely hard specifically because it's not a single causal effect. There are often many processes, many pathways which are involved in defining this one endpoint.

Evan: Correct.

Brandon: And data sets are oftentimes comically small, unless you have pharma to help you out. At least in open source, it's a hard problem.

Evan: It's sparse in the public domain. There's actually a range from really directly predictable properties to ones that, as you point out, are actually amalgams of other signaling events. Whether or not you're inhibiting CYP3A4 — that's really specific, it's a certain protein that you're inhibiting. So that's something that Pearl, for example, could actually model and expect to see useful prediction.

We've been doing 3D work on ADMET prediction before anyone else was. There was a bunch of work that ended up launching the company back in the day when AI could still be in peer-reviewed journals — not just random white papers on arXiv. We published MoleculeNet, which has been cited a few thousand times at some point. We also published this paper showing that multitask graph neural networks were the best at the time for doing ADMET prediction on large pharma data sets. That paper is one of the most cited papers on AI for ADMET ever now.

Our history from the beginning has been to work on not just one problem but to focus on drug discovery, and in so doing being able to have the bandwidth to focus on building all of the ML models that are needed for drug discovery — and not get distracted by the biological discovery processes that are needed (target ID side, or the clinical trial side) but really focused on all those tasks required for drug discovery, and also molecular generation. These are all important.

湿实验闭环与 RL：与 Incyte 的天作之合

Brandon: I want to talk about kind of the feedback between how your computation, your ML, and how wet-lab data went into developing this model. We talked about priors. It's been typically hard to scale protein–ligand models well in a way which meaningfully generalizes, and this recent Pearl paper has shown true generalization. How do these different aspects feed together to give you this power?

Evan: There's this classic concept of the narrative fallacy where in retrospect everything seems obvious and you can draw really linear processes of how we got from point A to point Z, but the reality is always more interesting and much messier and much more nonlinear. For Sergey and I, who've been training or taming neural nets for over a decade, we remember a lot of different eras. We would have been so excited if we could tell ourselves the capabilities of our technologies 10 years ago.

The concept of using generative AI for the molecular space is not a new concept, but the reduction-to-practice would have been very difficult a decade ago. I used to co-run the Stanford AI Salon — we had fun organizing, you know, it's Stanford in the Gates Computer Science Building, and we'd get some interesting speakers and like 25 people having wine and cheese, and they're all now like just straight-up famous people. Back then it was a much smaller field.

I remember very clearly in like 2017–2018 talking about GANs, and how they're clearly the future of image generation. There was a lot of work then on "well, can we just apply GANs to produce conformations of proteins, or to produce protein–ligand poses?" For all the same reasons those models were really tricky to train for images — mode collapse was the most famous problem — they didn't work very well for proteins or protein–ligand systems. We had to wait for the right primitive to be created, and that turned out to be diffusion.

Interestingly, a lot of image and video models — some are using diffusion, but some of those have actually gone autoregressive. What's kind of cool is right now for people interested in really core fundamental AI research, actually some of the most innovative diffusion research is happening in our field, in 3D structure prediction. No one would have predicted that then, but now that's a pillar of diffusion, I'd say. That was unpredictable 10 years ago.

In parallel to that, long before the advent of 3D diffusion models for generative tasks in chemistry, we were building a variety of tools for the problems of drug discovery at hand. Some were using physics-based methods for predicting potency or even for helping predict certain ADMET properties. Same with molecular generation — using different techniques to generate new molecular ideas. As Sergey pointed out, there are 10^60 drug-like molecules. Searching that space efficiently is hard.

Those things happened to be available when we wanted to take what was then the very nascent area of co-folding — which was clearly exciting but not at all ready for prime time, not at all ready for what a medicinal chemist would want to use in their day-to-day — and take it to the realm of useful, and in some cases irreplaceable, like clearly superior to a non-co-folding method. We had been building those other primitives, so they were available to us. We could put them together to build out, for example, the synthetic data pipeline or to use some of the inference-time techniques. We were ready to do those rapidly because we had been working on orthogonal techniques.

It's like almost any discovery — like the discovery of penicillin. I'm not saying what we're doing is on the same order of magnitude of benefit to humanity as penicillin, but there's always this element of: if you're laser-focused on a problem for long enough and spend enough time in the lab — or in our case on the computer banging your head against the wall in these problems — you can create the luck that will enable some of the developments.

RL 的雏形与 Incyte 合作

RJ: How does the lab interact with the development process?

Sergey: As I mentioned before, we train other models — not just structure prediction models — and for those specific models, lab outputs are extremely useful right away, like potency prediction. Those outputs can be used directly for model training. But what I'm most excited about going forward is reinforcement learning. I think that's coming in our field, and we have seen early signs of it working already with our models.

Initially, maybe physics-based feedback to improve your models through RL — typical RL loop. But eventually you can go all the way down to lab-in-the-loop setups where your model is not only producing predictions, but you synthesize based on those predictions and then you measure downstream properties and feed back those predictions into that model.

RJ: So how do you get enough volume? Do you have automation to do that, or do you have large campaigns that aren't necessarily automated but generate a lot of data?

Sergey: One thing I'm super proud of is our partnership with Incyte. They're such an amazing company. They are extremely good at producing data — basically taking the compounds, generating them, creating them, and then measuring the downstream properties and sending those results back. This is such a partnership born in heaven for us between Genesis and Incyte, where we are able to train models, give predictions, and have results back from Incyte super quickly.

RJ: So your rollouts include a lab iteration essentially.

Sergey: Yeah.

RJ: That's amazing.

Sergey: And diffusion is slow, so it's about the same speed sometimes. Give it a week.

Evan: It is true. I'm a big believer that companies are typically really good at one or two things and do best when they can focus on it. In the same way we've been just doggedly focused on developing the best AI models for drug discovery, Incyte has that level of maniacal focus on optimizing drug discovery and development.

Another trend people like to talk about is the rise of China in biotech. It's the elephant in the room. So many western companies have come to rely more on CROs to do a lot of their wet lab work — Incyte became state-of-the-art in terms of the experimental capabilities they had in-house. Their productivity is extremely high. It's a match made in heaven for us because what we thrive on is continuous learning of the models. We want to have design-make-test-analyze cycles that are as rapid as possible, and continuously fine-tune (in some cases retrain) the models based on what we see in the lab.

That partnership is one of, if not the first ever, that enables that sort of true joint foundation model training on historical and also prospective data. As ML practitioners, we all know data is just such a critical input — the critical ingredient. It's extremely exciting for us, and that range, reflecting our conversation so far, goes from structure, potency, and a variety of ADMET properties as well. It immediately will improve the strength of our models and be really powerful for generally accelerating drug discovery.

自动化湿实验的真实限制

RJ: We were joking around about the time that it takes to do diffusion, but how long does it take? You email them with "here's 10, 100 compounds" — and they get back to you with measurements of those realized compounds in a day, a week, a month, an hour?

Evan: It depends. Some compounds are really easy to knock out — really well-characterized reactions where the usual conditions just work. Sometimes it's "oh, this coupling actually didn't work like the literature said it would, and we have to try different conditions." I'm saying this so the audience understands that for all the claims out there about robotic labs automating synthesis, the reality is just a lot more complicated than that.

RJ: Sorry, what are some of the complications there? They all do some form of automation. What goes wrong with automated labs?

Evan: This is also one of the reasons, as Sergey mentioned, we're really excited about reinforcement learning, because it circumvents the problems of having to do very fast design-make-test-analyze cycles from the lab to feed back into the model. Ideally we can throw more and more GPUs at the problem and have the model self-train, in the same way we once did for board games in the late 2010s or most recently for coding. That's a key North Star for us.

However, before anyone tells you that there will be a country of geniuses in a data room just solving drug discovery — there are some problems in our space that don't lend themselves to RL as naturally and that will require experimental intervention. I myself spent a fair bit of time in the wet lab as a very mediocre experimental scientist before I found my true joy back in graduate school when I could just focus on coding all day.

In order to make a new molecule you have to synthesize it. That means different chemical reagents and catalysts and temperatures and solvents coming together in the right way in the right protocol to try to make your new molecule. Once you do that, you need to purify it. If you don't, your positive or negative assay readout could be an artifact. You need to characterize it — use NMR and other techniques like mass spec to indicate that what you made in your vial is what you actually sought to make in the first place. That's not as trivial as it sounds either. That's not only true for us, by the way — for anyone doing material science for AI, "did I make what I actually made?" is actually not a trivial question. It's easier in small molecule drug discovery than in materials, but still non-trivial and takes time.

We've seen so many cases where screenings of large molecules sounds great on paper because you can test millions, billions of compounds in one go, so you have more shots on goal — and as a bonus you have data for your model. Okay, the reality is that the translation of high-throughput screens, whether it's DEL (DNA-encoded libraries) or more traditional screens, to the actual business of resynthesizing a molecule de novo and doing a low-throughput high-fidelity experiment — that R² is shockingly low. That false positive rate is enormous for a variety of reasons. That's one of the many reasons why people are so excited about AI making those predictions, because in theory they can be even cleaner than a lot of the wet lab work, especially the high-throughput work.

RJ: Why can it be more? Is it just because the experiments go wrong a lot? Even with a very precise robot doing it, are there just too many variables, too much slop in the system?

Evan: It's a few things. One is that drug discovery frequently involves finding the outliers. One of the many aspects that's so wild about our space is that the properties one is seeking in a molecule very often anti-correlate. Binding tends to improve the more hydrophobic, the more greasy your compound is — because protein binding pockets are greasy. Guess what? That makes your solubility worse. Then you want to improve your solubility often by adding polarity to your compound — and suddenly, for those that remember high school biology, cells are surrounded by lipid bilayers. Your molecule might not get through the cell membrane because you made it too polar.

The properties you want often anti-correlate, and that's where multi-parameter optimization of molecules ends up feeling like playing whack-a-mole. You're often searching for real outliers, and that often requires making molecules that are fundamentally novel. The kinds of chemistries that today can be automated are fairly constrained. Your ability to search chemical space broadly for those really top-Pareto-optimal compounds is very limited. The benefits you get in speed have a very harsh trade-off with the actual quality and novelty of molecules that you can make.

RJ: So you can do a lot of boring stuff very quickly.

Evan: If you're lucky.

Sergey: Yeah, if you're lucky.

RJ: But to actually get to the cutting edge questions, it's hard to get a robotic system to do those kinds of things.

Evan: Yes. We would love for that to happen. It'd be a huge tailwind for what we do, but it's not available today.

Incyte 的纵向整合

RJ: What is Incyte's approach that makes them so fast and effective?

Evan: If one is willing to focus on one problem and say no to distractions, it's amazing what can happen. Their talent density is very very high, and they're willing to — what is not universally done now in large or medium-sized pharma — build so many experimental capabilities in-house. I actually think that it's possible for more companies to try to do that, given the global dynamic of pharmaceutical development that's changed so dramatically.

Brandon: Right — so CROs taking over everything has now suddenly become commoditized, and your capability to do the cutting-edge experiments goes down.

Evan: It's been a debate for hundreds of years about the benefits of vertical integration. It comes with all the areas of risk and challenges and upfront cost, but has all the benefits of controlling the process end-to-end. When it works, you can do amazing things when you do things end-to-end. It comes with greater challenges and an appetite needed to do it.

从 Genesis Therapeutics 到 Genesis Molecular AI：身份的澄清

Brandon: This brings me to another question. You guys — so maybe now's a good time to talk about your pivot. There's a name change from Genesis Therapeutics to Genesis Molecular AI, and that goes hand-in-hand with maybe a pivot in the strategy. What it sounds like to me is "we're going to focus on the AI portion and sell that to pharma, rather than be a drug company that also sells the tool." Is that accurate?

Evan: When we started the company, the origin of the company was fundamental deep learning research methods we had developed at Stanford. The objective we were trying to solve for was: how can we have the greatest impact for patients? At the time, there was no real precedent for a pure model company really making it in the realm of just creating value for partners in pharma, or really most legacy industries. The other component was: the company being founded on AI research, we really wanted to show people that we were serious about the biotech domain.

Brandon: You seem a lot more serious to all the tried-and-true folks in pharma — the dyed-in-the-wool med chemists, drug developers — who we really wanted to work with.

Evan: There was an element of an attempt at nominative determinism there. But also an acknowledgement that I don't think anyone had really shown at that time that you could be a pure AI model company and really make it in the space. That was obviously — this is 2019 — we had the benefit of being real forebears, pioneers in the space. We had the downside of being pretty early.

As the company grew and evolved, we really were structured kind of like a double helix in that we had extremely strong, focused AI research, and we were fortunate to be able to recruit some of the most experienced and accomplished drug hunters — people on our management team and board who have discovered, co-invented, developed many FDA-approved drugs. You talk to most med-chemists, most feel very fortunate if they're able to work on one molecule that gets FDA approval in their whole career. We were really fortunate to convince these accomplished drug hunters to come join our management team and board at the beginning and steer the show.

The outcome has always been the same: everything we're talking about, it only matters if we end up helping patients and their families at the end of the day. We have felt from the beginning that the way to do that at scale is to primarily partner with large and medium-sized pharma companies and biotech companies. So they can do what they're best at — novel biology, clinical development — and we can do what we're best at — AI — and bring those two together.

In addition to that, as Sergey has said, there are numerous advantages to having in-house wet lab data generation. Clearly dogfooding the models, getting really direct human feedback from real med chemists on what we need to build. And the third thing is that the single most valuable thing in the universe — what I like to joke is the Mirror of Erised — if you ask people what is the one thing you most want, and you really thought about it, most people would say a medicine for themselves or a family member. There is just immense intrinsic value to having one's own pipeline programs.

When we interact with pharmaceutical partners, on average they love that we're working on molecules ourselves, because it means we're not just a bunch of keyboard jockeys — we live the dream that we are pitching, and we need to use these models in practice for ourselves too. So they're battle-tested in an additional way, and we're sort of domesticated in that we know what's really difficult about the space. We're not just selling some pipe dream.

RJ: It seems to me there's been a shift in the last year to two years where suddenly pharma has become interested in buying tools off the shelf. Whereas previously, a lot of companies started trying to build AI and then had trouble and became pharma companies because that's the only way they could make money — you raise a bunch of money and hope that in 10 years you have a drug. And now that's changing — you have a lot of recent AI acquisitions in drug discovery and pathology. Have you seen this shift, and is that part of why you're shifting toward selling models? What's going on?

Evan: To finish that narrative arc: the name change was just to reflect what we were really doing in practice — which is we are an AI company, as we have been from day one when we were coding in PyTorch 0.17, building, you know — when Sergey was scaling transformers on PyTorch, the first one to do that — we were scaling graph neural nets in PyTorch, one of the first ones to do that. It's reflecting what our identity has been from day one. But also alluding to the fact that we are very serious molecule makers ourselves. We are full-stack in that way.

Our mission is to create as many medicines as possible as would otherwise not have been created. I think the way to get there is to put our technology directly into the hands of as many drug developers as possible — not just our own, but in big pharma, midcap pharma, and biotech. That's partially why we've been building our agents, so that our models — which have become so much more powerful — can be easily used and orchestrated in the hands of med chemists and CAD scientists who might have never written a line of code in their life.

智能体的崛起：从工具到代理

Brandon: I find it interesting to hear that you have now started bringing in agents into your development process. From the very get-go, your philosophy was much more "these are tools for scientists." Now you are switching and saying "we are actually going to have automated systems which make decisions." You did allude to maybe hitting the threshold like last November where it just magically became useful. Are there other reasons for going in this direction, and why now?

Sergey: Well, let me ask you a question in return. How many tools do you think a human med-chemist or CAD scientist can realistically learn and be proficient in?

Brandon: Oh, how many can they learn and how many do they use? Because I've seen that they like to use lots of them, but maybe they're not necessarily good with —

Sergey: But it's really hard, right? A lot of the tools are extremely complex. They have a lot of parameters that you need to set properly and configure them. This is where agents are extremely good at. They actually know how to use all of the tools and how to orchestrate them well.

Brandon: I wouldn't interrupt you, but when you say "agent" — some people like using the term agent to just mean something automated, but other people use agent to specifically mean you have an LLM which is responsible for orchestrating decisions. Outside of biotech, it's almost universally the latter. But sometimes in the space of bio, people are still using "agents" to refer to any decision-based system which is automated out of humans, and maybe some of them are more like traditional rules-based systems.

Sergey: In our case, it's the AI definition of agents — an LLM which is able to use all of the tools that we have been painstakingly building over the many years of existence of this company. That's a very powerful concept. Now as a CAD scientist or med chemist, you don't need to deeply understand all the details and all the hyperparameters you need to set — you can just let this thing go and figure out and solve the problems.
